query: Provide an in-depth review of code-based, programmatic, and 'design-as-code' approaches to electronics and PCB design that can be version-controlled and driven from a CI pipeline (analogous to code-CAD such as CadQuery/OpenSCAD for mechanical design). Cover tools and frameworks including atopile (the .ato language), tscircuit (React-for-PCB), SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA/gaf, Horizon EDA, Edea, KiCad's Python / IPC scripting and kicad-cli, JITX (Stanza-based programmatic PCB), and any LLM/text-to-PCB or text-to-schematic research prototypes. For each, describe the programming model, what is generated automatically vs. specified by the designer, support for parametrization and reuse, manufacturability/DRC integration, rendering/export (Gerber, BOM, netlist), maturity, and licensing. Discuss the trade-offs versus GUI-based EDA and versus AI/generative EDA, and which approaches best fit an open-hardware, reproducible, CI-rendered workflow for a modular multi-powder dosing device. Cite project pages/repos and ~5-8 recent (2020-2025) papers or technical sources.

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):
        - InterventionOtherName (0.75): Alternative intervention names
        - Phase (0.65): Trial phase
        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups
        - Keyword (0.6): Study keywords
        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

Supported Enums:
    Phase:
        - EARLY_PHASE1: Early Phase 1
        - PHASE1: Phase 1
        - PHASE2: Phase 2
        - PHASE3: Phase 3
        - PHASE4: Phase 4
        - NA: Not Applicable

    StandardAge:
        - CHILD: Child
        - ADULT: Adult
        - OLDER_ADULT: Older Adult

    Status:
        - RECRUITING: Currently recruiting participants
        - ACTIVE_NOT_RECRUITING: Active but not recruiting
        - COMPLETED: Study completed
        - ENROLLING_BY_INVITATION: Enrolling by invitation only
        - NOT_YET_RECRUITING: Not yet recruiting
        - SUSPENDED: Study suspended
        - TERMINATED: Study terminated
        - WITHDRAWN: Study withdrawn
        - AVAILABLE: Available
        - NO_LONGER_AVAILABLE: No longer available
        - TEMPORARILY_NOT_AVAILABLE: Temporarily not available
        - APPROVED_FOR_MARKETING: Approved for marketing
        - WITHHELD: Withheld
        - UNKNOWN: Unknown status

    StudyType:
        - INTERVENTIONAL: Interventional studies
        - OBSERVATIONAL: Observational studies
        - EXPANDED_ACCESS: Expanded access studies

    PrimaryPurpose:
        - TREATMENT: Treatment
        - PREVENTION: Prevention
        - DIAGNOSTIC: Diagnostic
        - ECT: Educational/Counseling/Training
        - SUPPORTIVE_CARE: Supportive Care
        - SCREENING: Screening
        - HEALTH_SERVICES_RESEARCH: Health Services Research
        - BASIC_SCIENCE: Basic Science
        - DEVICE_FEASIBILITY: Device Feasibility
        - OTHER: Other

    InterventionType:
        - BEHAVIORAL: Behavioral interventions
        - BIOLOGICAL: Biological interventions
        - COMBINATION_PRODUCT: Combination product interventions
        - DEVICE: Device interventions
        - DIAGNOSTIC_TEST: Diagnostic test interventions
        - DIETARY_SUPPLEMENT: Dietary supplement interventions
        - DRUG: Drug interventions
        - GENETIC: Genetic interventions
        - PROCEDURE: Procedure interventions
        - RADIATION: Radiation interventions
        - OTHER: Other interventions

    DesignAllocation:
        - RANDOMIZED: Randomized allocation
        - NON_RANDOMIZED: Non-randomized allocation
        - NA: Not applicable

    InterventionalAssignment:
        - SINGLE_GROUP: Single group assignment
        - PARALLEL: Parallel assignment
        - CROSSOVER: Crossover assignment
        - FACTORIAL: Factorial assignment
        - SEQUENTIAL: Sequential assignment

    ObservationalModel:
        - COHORT: Cohort
        - CASE_CONTROL: Case-Control
        - CASE_ONLY: Case-Only
        - CASE_CROSSOVER: Case-Crossover
        - ECOLOGIC_OR_COMMUNITY: Ecologic or Community
        - FAMILY_BASED: Family-Based
        - DEFINED_POPULATION: Defined Population
        - NATURAL_HISTORY: Natural History
        - OTHER: Other

    DesignMasking:
        - NONE: None (Open Label)
        - SINGLE: Single
        - DOUBLE: Double
        - TRIPLE: Triple
        - QUADRUPLE: Quadruple

    WhoMasked:
        - PARTICIPANT: Participant
        - CARE_PROVIDER: Care Provider
        - INVESTIGATOR: Investigator
        - OUTCOMES_ASSESSOR: Outcomes Assessor
query.title: Query

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Provide an in-depth review of code-based, programmatic, and 'design-as-code' approaches to electronics and PCB design that can be version-controlled and driven from a CI pipeline (analogous to code-CAD such as CadQuery/OpenSCAD for mechanical design). Cover tools and frameworks including atopile (the .ato language), tscircuit (React-for-PCB), SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA/gaf, Horizon EDA, Edea, KiCad's Python / IPC scripting and kicad-cli, JITX (Stanza-based programmatic PCB), and any LLM/text-to-PCB or text-to-schematic research prototypes. For each, describe the programming model, what is generated automatically vs. specified by the designer, support for parametrization and reuse, manufacturability/DRC integration, rendering/export (Gerber, BOM, netlist), maturity, and licensing. Discuss the trade-offs versus GUI-based EDA and versus AI/generative EDA, and which approaches best fit an open-hardware, reproducible, CI-rendered workflow for a modular multi-powder dosing device. Cite project pages/repos and ~5-8 recent (2020-2025) papers or technical sources.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

function.arguments:
{"plan_json": "{
  "objectives": [
    {
      "id": 1,
      "objective": "Research atopile (.ato language) - programming model, features, licensing, maturity",
      "rationale": "Atopile is a key tool in the design-as-code space for electronics. Need detailed information about its approach.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 2,
      "objective": "Research tscircuit (React-for-PCB) - programming model, features, licensing, maturity",
      "rationale": "tscircuit is a newer approach using React/TypeScript for PCB design. Need detailed coverage.",
      "status": "pending",
      "result": null,
      "evaluation": null
    },
    {
      "id": 3,
      "objective": "Research SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA/gaf, Horizon EDA - programming models and features",

Retrieved Papers:
## 1. ORFS-agent: Tool-Using Agents for Chip Design Optimization
Valid Text Names:
'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 1-2', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 2-3', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 3-4', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 4-5', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 4-4', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 5-6', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 6-7', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 7-8', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 8-9', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 9-10', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 10-11', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 11-12', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 12-13'

BibTex:
@article{ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents,
    author = "Ghose, Amur and Kahng, Andrew B. and Kundu, Sayak and Wang, Zhiang",
    title = "ORFS-agent: Tool-Using Agents for Chip Design Optimization",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2506.08332",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2506.08332",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.08332"
}


Abstract:
Machine learning has been widely used to optimize complex engineering workflows across numerous domains. In the context of integrated circuit design, modern flows (e.g., going from a register-transfer level netlist to physical layouts) involve extensive configuration via thousands of parameters, and small changes to these parameters can have large downstream impacts on desired outcomes - namely design performance, power, and area. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new opportunities for learning and reasoning within such high-dimensional optimization tasks. In this work, we introduce ORFS-agent, an LLM-based iterative optimization agent that automates parameter tuning in an open-source hardware design flow. ORFS-agent adaptively explores parameter configurations, demonstrating clear improvements over standard Bayesian optimization approaches in terms of resource efficiency and final design metrics. Our empirical evaluations on two different technology nodes and a range of circuit benchmarks indicate that ORFS-agent can improve both routed wirelength and effective clock period by over 13%, all while using 40% fewer optimization iterations. Moreover, by following natural language objectives to trade off certain metrics for others, ORFS-agent demonstrates a flexible and interpretable framework for multi-objective optimization. Crucially, RFS-agent is modular and model-agnostic, and can be plugged in to any frontier LLM without any further fine-tuning.

Relevant Snippet:
… In the context of integrated circuit design, modern flows (eg, … clear improvements over standard Bayesian optimization … turns high-level hardware descriptions into manufacturable layouts…

## 1. Python Framework for Modular and Parametric SPICE Netlists Generation

@article{gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor,
    author = "Gutiérrez, Sergio Vinagrero and Natale, Giorgio Di and Vatajelu, Elena-Ioana",
    title = "Python Framework for Modular and Parametric SPICE Netlists Generation",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2306.12224",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Due to the complex specifications of current electronic systems, design decisions need to be explored automatically. However, the exploration process is a complex task given the plethora of design choices such as the selection of components, number of components, operating modes of each of the components, connections between the components and variety of ways in which the same functionality can be implemented. To tackle these issues, scripts are used to generate designs based on high-level abstract constructions. Still, this approach is usually ad hoc and platform dependent, making the whole procedure hardly reusable, scalable and versatile. We propose a generic, open-source framework tackling rapid design exploration for the generation of modular and parametric electronic designs that is able to work on any major simulator.

## 2. BHDL: A Lucid, Expressive, and Embedded Programming Language and System for PCB Designs
Valid Text Names:
'li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2', 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3', 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-2', 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5', 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6', 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 6-6'

    year = "2021",
    journal = "2021 58th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC)",
    pages = "355-360",
    month = "Dec",
    doi = "10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086",
    publisher = "IEEE"
}


Abstract:
Graphical PCB design tools like KiCAD lack support for high-level abstraction such as functions and loops. To improve PCB design productivity, we hereby present BHDL, a programming framework for PCB designs. In its compact and declarative syntax, schematics and layouts can be modeled effectively and expressed concisely. Treating all circuits, even a resistor, as functions, BHDL naturally supports modularized development that builds a complex design up from smaller designs hierarchically. As an embedded Domain Specific Language (eDSL), BHDL allows users to leverage the full feature of the host language for customization and extension. Our Jupyter kernel supports web-based, REPL-style development and generates auto-placed PCBs.

## 2. HardOps: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design
Valid Text Names:

    year = "2022",
    journal = "International Journal of Computer Integrated Manufacturing",
    volume = "35",

    doi = "10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188",

    publisher = "Informa UK Limited",
    issue = "12",
    issn = "0951-192X"
}


Abstract:
ABSTRACT Open collaborative design of physical products between remote partners poses unique challenges. This is due to both the complex and interconnected data required for product design and manufacture, and to the centralised computing infrastructure traditionally used to manage product lifecycle data. While modern cloud-based solutions to collaborative design are gaining popularity, they diminish the control of each design partner. In contrast, software designers readily collaborate on highly complex software, while retaining direct control of the files they are editing due to the dominance of distributed version control. Distributed version control for software is often coupled with ‘Developer Operations’ or DevOps tools to automate critical processes and facilitate communication. This paper explores how DevOps workflows can be adapted for open development of hardware. An example of how DevOps for hardware (HardOps) can be implemented is provided by the OpenFlexure Microscope project. While much ground remains to be broken in this field, HardOps can support a new paradigm of distributed hardware development, with enormous benefits not only for open-source hardware, but also commercial hardware.

## 2. Repurposing a digital kitchen scale for neuroscience research: a complete hardware and software cookbook for PASTA
Valid Text Names:
'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 1-2', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 2-4', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 4-6', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 6-7', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 7-8', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 8-8'

BibTex:
@article{virag2021repurposingadigital,
    author = "Virag, D. and Homolak, J. and Kodvanj, Ivan and Perhoč, A. Babić and Knezović, A. and Barilar, J. Osmanović and Šalković-Petrišić, M.",
    title = "Repurposing a digital kitchen scale for neuroscience research: a complete hardware and software cookbook for PASTA",
    year = "2021",
    journal = "Scientific Reports",
    volume = "11",
    month = "Apr",
    doi = "10.1038/s41598-021-82710-6",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-82710-6",
    publisher = "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory"
}


Abstract:
Widely available low-cost electronics encourage the development of open-source tools for neuroscientific research. In recent years, many neuroscientists recognized the open science movement for its potential to stimulate and encourage science that is less focused on money, and more on robustness, validity, questioning and understanding. Here, we wanted to contribute to this global community by creating a research platform based on a common digital kitchen scale. This everyday ordinary kitchen tool is sometimes used in neuroscience research in various ways; however, its use is limited by sampling rate and inability to store and analyze data. To tackle this problem we developed a Platform for Acoustic STArtle or PASTA. This robust and simple platform enables users to obtain data from kitchen scale load cells at a high sampling rate, store it and analyze it. Here, we used it to analyze acoustic startle and prepulse inhibition sensorimotor gating in rats treated intracerebroventricularly with streptozotocin, but the system can be easily modified and upgraded for other purposes. In accordance with open science principles, we shared complete hardware design with instructions. Furthermore, we also disclose our software codes written for PASTA data acquisition (C++, Arduino) and acoustic startle experimental protocol (Python) and analysis (ratPASTA R package—R-based Awesome Toolbox for PASTA, and pastaWRAP—Python wrapper package for ratPASTA). To further encourage the development of our PASTA platform we demonstrate its sensitivity by using PASTA-gathered data to extract breathing patterns during rat freezing behavior in our experimental protocol.

## 3. Iterative printing of bulk metal and polymer for additive manufacturing of multi-layer electronic circuits
Valid Text Names:
'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 1-2', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 2-3', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 3-4', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 4-6', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 6-7', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 7-8', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 8-10', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 10-12', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 12-13', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 13-14', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 14-15', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 15-15'

BibTex:

    author = "Khan, Zeba and Gururajan, Dheepesh and Kartmann, Sabrina and Koltay, Peter and Zengerle, Roland and Shu, Zhe",
    title = "Iterative printing of bulk metal and polymer for additive manufacturing of multi-layer electronic circuits",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "npj Advanced Manufacturing",
    volume = "1",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1038/s44334-024-00001-0",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1038/s44334-024-00001-0",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "3004-8621"
}


Abstract:
In pursuing advancing additive manufacturing (AM) techniques for 3D objects, this study combines AM techniques for bulk metal and polymer on a single platform for one-stop printing of multilayer 3D electronic circuits with two novel aspects. The first innovation involves the embedded integration of electronic circuits by printing low-resistance electrical traces from bulk metal into polymer channels. Cross-section grinding results reveal (92 ± 5)% occupancy of electrically conductive traces in polymer channels despite the different thermal properties of the two materials. The second aspect encompasses the possibility of printing vertical bulk metal vias up to 10 mm in height with the potential for expansion, interconnecting electrically conductive traces embedded in different layers of the 3D object. The work provides comprehensive 3D printing design guidelines for successfully integrating fully embedded electrically conductive traces and the interconnecting vertical bulk metal vias. A smooth and continuous workflow is also introduced, enabling a single-run print of functional multilayer embedded 3D electronics. The design rules and the workflow facilitate the iterative printing of two distinct materials, each defined by unique printing temperatures and techniques. Observations indicate that conductive traces using molten metal microdroplets show a 12-fold reduction in resistance compared to nanoparticle ink-based methods, meaning this technique greatly complements multi-material additive manufacturing (MM-AM). The work presents insights into the behavior of molten metal microdroplets on a polymer substrate when printed through the MM-AM process. It explores their characteristics in two scenarios: When they are deposited side-by-side to form conductive traces and when they are deposited out-of-plane to create vertical bulk metal vias. The innovative application of MM-AM to produce multilayer embedded 3D electronics with bulk metal and polymer demonstrates significant potential for realizing the fabrication of free-form 3D electronics.

Abstract:
The convergence of the Internet of Things (IoT) and the construction industry represents a potent opportunity to fundamentally redefine the processes of constructing and maintaining buildings. The integration of IoT technology has the potential to revolutionize building safety, efficiency, and sustainability, offering a promising avenue for innovation and advancement within the industry. In this work, we propose an innovative design and implementation of an interoperable physical access control IoT-based system that can be easily integrated with existing access control security systems and used in various environments such as offices, homes, and public buildings. Based on our developed and implemented physical access control IoT-based system, we identified several challenges in the current state of access control systems. These challenges include difficulties in integrating different security systems, the absence of interoperable standards, and the need for further research to enhance the scalability and performance of such systems.

## 6. Multiplexed superconducting qubit control at millikelvin temperatures with a low-power cryo-CMOS multiplexer

'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 1-2', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 2-4', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 4-5', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 5-7', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 7-9', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 9-14', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 14-21', 'acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit pages 21-22'

BibTex:
@misc{acharya2023multiplexedsuperconductingqubit,
    author = "Acharya, R. and Brebels, S. and Grill, A. and Verjauw, J. and Ivanov, Ts. and Lozano, D. Perez and Wan, D. and Damme, J. Van and Vadiraj, A. M. and Mongillo, M. and Govoreanu, B. and Craninckx, J. and Radu, I. P. and Greve, K. De and Gielen, G. and Catthoor, F. and Potočnik, A.",
    title = "Multiplexed superconducting qubit control at millikelvin temperatures with a low-power cryo-CMOS multiplexer",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "Nature Electronics",
    volume = "6",
    pages = "900-909",
    month = "Sep",
    doi = "10.1038/s41928-023-01033-8",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1038/s41928-023-01033-8",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "11",
    issn = "2520-1131"
}


Abstract:
Large-scale superconducting quantum computers require the high-fidelity control and readout of large numbers of qubits at millikelvin temperatures, resulting in a massive input–output bottleneck. Cryo-electronics based on complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor technology could provide a scalable and versatile solution. However, detrimental effects due to cross-coupling between the qubits and the electronic and thermal noise generated during cryo-electronics operation need to be avoided. Here we report a low-power radio-frequency multiplexing cryo-electronics system that operates below 15 mK with a minimal cross-coupling. We benchmark its performance by interfacing the system with a superconducting qubit and observe that the qubit’s relaxation times are unaffected, whereas the coherence times are marginally affected in both static and dynamic operations. Using the multiplexer, single-qubit gate fidelities above 99.9%—that is, above the threshold for surface-code-based quantum error correction—can be achieved with appropriate thermal filtering. We also demonstrate time-division multiplexing capabilities by dynamically windowing calibrated qubit control pulses. A low-power radio-frequency multiplexing cryo-electronics system, which is based on complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor technology, can operate below 15 mK and provide the control and interfacing of superconducting qubits with minimal cross-coupling.

    issn = "3004-9261"
}


Abstract:
Impedance analysis is a powerful technique that has become increasingly important in various applications, it represents a leap forward in the field of electronic measurments and diagnostics. In this work, we present the development of miniaturized, multiplexed, and connected platform for impedance spectroscopy. Designed for online measurements and adapted to wireless network architectures, our platform has been tested and optimized to be used for multi-selective chemical organic sensor nodes. This compact and versatile circuit is built from low cost and low power consumption (250 mW) microelectronics components that achieve long duration operability (5 days and 16 h) without compromising on sensor measurement accuracy and precision. We used the well-known impedance network analyzer AD5933 (Analog Devices, Norwood, MA, USA) chip which can measure a spectrum of impedances in the range 5 kHz to 100 kHz. The proposed system is based on ESP32-C3 Microcontroller enabling the management of the AD5933 through its I2C interface. Our system benefits from two multiplexer components CD74HC4067 allowing calibration process and the interface of 15 conductimetric sensors with real time acquisition (less than 90 ms per acquisition). The system is capable of relaying information through the network for data analysis and storage. The paper describes the microelectronics design, the impedance response over time, the measurement’s sensitivity and accuracy and the testing of the platform with embedded chemical sensors for gas classification and recognition.

## 1. Polymorphic Blocks: Unifying High-level Specification and Low-level Control for Circuit Board Design

Abstract:
Mainstream board-level circuit design tools work at the lowest level of design --- schematics and individual components. While novel tools experiment with higher levels of design, abstraction often comes at the expense of the fine-grained control afforded by low-level tools. In this work, we propose a hardware description language (HDL) approach that supports users at multiple levels of abstraction from broad system architecture to subcircuits and component selection. We extend the familiar hierarchical block diagram with polymorphism to include abstract-typed blocks (e.g., generic resistor supertype) and electronics modeling (i.e., currents and voltages). Such an approach brings the advantages of reusability and encapsulation from object-oriented programming, while addressing the unique needs of electronics designers such as physical correctness verification. We discuss the system design, including fundamental abstractions, the block diagram construction HDL, and user interfaces to inspect and fine-tune the design; demonstrate example designs built with our system; and present feedback from intermediate-level engineers who have worked with our system.

Abstract:
This paper describes the history of the Verilog hardware description language (HDL), including its influential predecessors and successors. Since its creation in 1984 and first sale in 1985, Verilog has completely revolutionized the design of hardware. Verilog enabled the development and wide acceptance of logic synthesis. For large-scale digital logic design, previous schematic-based techniques have transformed into textual register-transfer level (RTL) descriptions written in Verilog. As of 2018 about 80% of integrated circuit design teams worldwide use Verilog and its compatible descendant SystemVerilog.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000000

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt frames board-level HDLs and programmatic PCB tools as a space that benefits from programming-language ideas such as generator languages and type systems to automate design and encapsulate low-level knowledge in reusable libraries. It highlights a key practical challenge: confidence in code-generated hardware — unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly — so mechanisms to establish trust in community-shared generators and subcircuits are important (for example, labeling subcircuits as “physically fabricated and tested”). The authors note that simulation and static modeling alone are insufficient for modern practice, implying that tooling and processes (community verification, provenance, testing artifacts) are needed alongside programming models. Finally, the field is nascent with many open questions across models, abstractions, and user interfaces, and will benefit from cross-disciplinary work across electrical engineering, programming languages, and HCI. This context informs how tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, and PCBmodE might be compared by their programming models, reuse mechanisms, and approaches to verification and trust.


---

## pqac-00000001

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 97-100'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The provided excerpt is a bibliography that cites multiple papers and resources on code-based and programmatic approaches to PCB and electronics design. It includes explicit references to work on circuit-board-level hardware description languages and custom HDLs for PCB capture, as well as interactive visual editing that weaves schematics and code (Richard Lin et al.). It cites research on high-level abstractions and polymorphic blocks that unify specification and low-level control, suggesting programmatic/design-as-code models that combine declarative specs with concrete layout control. The bibliography also points to practical tooling and ecosystems: KiCad and its action plugins (extensibility and scripting), PCB CAD products (Mentor Xpedition), prototyping-centered tools (Fritzing, AutoFritz, VirtualComponent), and component library resources (Ultra Librarian). While these citations indicate active research and tooling around HDL-like languages, plugins, autocomplete for breadboarding, and reusable circuit blocks, the excerpt does not contain detailed descriptions of specific tools named in the question (SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, PCBmodE) nor their programming models. To answer the question fully, consult the cited papers by Lin et al., the PCB West talk on custom HDLs, and the KiCad plugin and product documentation referenced here.

## pqac-00000002

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 27-30'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt situates code-based PCB tools in the lineage of HDLs and hardware construction languages (HCLs). It highlights that recent PCB-focused projects like SKiDL and JITPCB adopt a generator-style programming model by embedding circuit-construction primitives into a general-purpose programming language, enabling parameterized module families and programmatic board assembly. PCB HDLs tend to be structural rather than behavioral, making behavioral abstractions from chip design less applicable. The author argues that adding richer electronics models (parts metadata, substitutable parts, correctness checks) on top of generator-based approaches enables features such as parts selection and automated checks. The text also notes relevant tooling considerations from programming-language research: live programming can provide immediate feedback but must manage latency between edits and results, and techniques used to accelerate chip tool compilation (partitioning, incremental compilation) may also benefit board-level compilation. The excerpt does not specifically describe BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, or PCBmodE, so those tools are not covered here.


---

## pqac-00000003

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 27-30'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt situates programmatic PCB/design-as-code tools within the broader HDL/HCL landscape. Chip HDLs (e.g., Verilog, VHDL) combine structural and behavioral descriptions, and analog/mixed-signal variants (Verilog-AMS, VHDL-AMS) exist but are not full design/synthesis languages. Hardware construction languages (HCLs) extend HDLs by providing generator constructs to produce families of modules from high-level parameters. For PCB work, traditional PCB HDLs tend to be purely structural; however, recent tools such as JITPCB and SKiDL introduce generator-style programming by embedding circuit-construction primitives in general-purpose languages, enabling programmatic, parameterized board creation. The dissertation referenced builds on these ideas and adds richer electronics modeling to support parts selection and correctness checks. The excerpt also highlights live programming concepts — continuously running code and low-latency feedback — as important for interactive textual design workflows. The excerpt does not explicitly describe BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, or PCBmodE, so details on those specific tools are absent.


---

## pqac-00000004

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt frames board-level HDLs as a programmatic, higher-abstraction approach to PCB/electronics design. It contrasts simple Verilog-like translations with a Chisel-like generator model where code describes construction and sizing methodology (e.g., automatically sizing resistors/values per application). Object-oriented concepts — type hierarchies, inheritance, and parameterized types (voltage, current ratings) — are proposed to let designers instantiate abstract components and either choose or automatically select compatible concrete implementations, and to enable correctness checks. Polymorphic Blocks is given as a concrete, Python-embedded DSL and library-based flow that demonstrates these ideas: top-level blocks are declared in code (example class Blinky(Block)), sub-blocks are instantiated (self.Block(MagicMcu()), self.Block(IndicatorLed())), and connections are made programmatically (self.connect(...)). The paper emphasizes that generators and typing encapsulate specialized knowledge, increase automation, and raise design efficiency and accessibility. This provides a conceptual model relevant to programmatic tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, and others even though those specific tools are not named in the excerpt.

## pqac-00000005

From text 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2'.

Hebi Li, Youbiao He, Qi Xiao, Jin Tian, and Forrest Sheng Bao. Bhdl: a lucid, expressive, and embedded programming language and system for pcb designs. 2021 58th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC), pages 355-360, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086, doi:10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086. This article has 1 citations.

BHDL (Board HDL) is presented as a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive embedded DSL implemented in Racket. It models every circuit element, including discrete parts, as functions to enable hierarchical, modular composition of larger designs from smaller components. BHDL offers concise connection syntax (net) and higher-level connection forms — series, parallel, and bus — to express wiring succinctly. For layout co-design, BHDL supplies relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and adapts VLSI auto-placers to place free parts. The system supports an interactive REPL-style development model and is provided with a Jupyter notebook environment/kernal for incremental coding and visualization. The toolchain generates placed PCB boards which can be exported and routed via external tools (e.g., FreeRouting) to produce manufacturable PCBs. As an eDSL, BHDL also allows leveraging full host-language features (functions, loops, libraries) for programmability, parameterization, and extension of PCB designs.

## pqac-00000013

From text 'stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9'.

Julian Stirling, Kaspar Bumke, Joel Collins, Vimal Dhokia, and Richard Bowman. Hardops: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design. Feb 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188, doi:10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188. This article has 17 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes applying DevOps/DVCS and CI/CD practices to open-source hardware (HardOps). Key DVCS benefits include cryptographically signed commits, distributed branches, and merge requests with unique IDs, threaded discussion and issue links to control protected branches. A major challenge is CAD files: many are complex binary formats making diffs and merges difficult; even text-based CAD (e.g. KiCad.kicad_pcb) often needs specialist tools. Mitigations include centralised file locking, Git extensions, and external diff/merge tools, but the preferred route is CAD vendors providing comparison/merge functionality. OpenFlexure leverages OpenSCAD (CAD-as-code) enabling parametric designs and programmatic testing: unit tests, static analysis, and CI jobs generate STLs, renderings, documentation, and BOMs reproducibly. CI pipelines on DevOps platforms run automated quality checks, build artifacts, deploy websites, and can execute on external infrastructure for resource-intensive tasks or licensed software. Scheduled jobs and bots help manage issues and keep assets in sync, reducing manual overhead and ensuring a single source of truth for production files.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 12-13'.

Julian Stirling, Kaspar Bumke, Joel Collins, Vimal Dhokia, and Richard Bowman. Hardops: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design. Feb 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188, doi:10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188. This article has 17 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes HardOps as a decentralised alternative for hardware design using distributed version control (DVCS) and DevOps platforms. Users keep full local copies of designs and history, synchronise via SSH, and have cryptographically hashed records for traceability. Collaboration works by fetching and selectively merging changes; merge requests can be gated by review/sign-off. HardOps supports automation pipelines (e.g., clash analysis) but these rely on tools providing command-line interfaces so they can be scripted. Many CAD packages lack robust CLI and proprietary licensing can complicate automation (sequential jobs or multiple licenses may be required). The authors note that most CAD functionality can be automated via APIs and that command-line wrappers could be written, but significant work is needed to generalise HardOps beyond OpenSCAD and to scale from prototyping to production. They also emphasise that production-related structured data (quality control, production records) may be better handled in databases linked to the DVCS-managed design data, and that project-management federation between servers remains an open challenge.

URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188, doi:10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188. This article has 17 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes HardOps as applying DevOps practices and toolchains to hardware development, using the OpenFlexure Project as a worked example. HardOps presents a six-stage workflow (Plan; Design and Document; Prepare and Verify; Distributed Production; Physical Testing; Feedback) and emphasizes using distributed version control systems (DVCS) alongside project management and automation tools. Task automation is organised into CI pipelines — server-run scripts for static analysis, building and testing — with GitLab given as an example platform. The text contrasts HardOps with traditional PLM/PDM approaches, highlighting that DevOps/HardOps are distributed (each user keeps a full copy and history) rather than centralised. It also notes that certain types of design (gateware: FPGAs and ASICs, written in HDLs) are more naturally amenable to source-control and automation because they behave more like software. The excerpt does not explicitly mention KiCad or PCB-specific automation, but the described principles (DVCS, CI pipelines, reproducible builds/tests, and distributed collaboration) are directly applicable to automating KiCad workflows for open-source hardware projects.

The paper introduces "HardOps": applying software DevOps toolchains to open hardware development. It argues that distributed version control (e.g., Git) and Git-based platforms (GitHub, GitLab, CERN's Open Hardware Repository) are better suited than centralized PDM for open, collaborative hardware projects because they give contributors control of copies, branches, and a rich design history. HardOps adapts DevOps practices — version control, project management, and automated workflows — to capture and control design data and to automate computational tasks needed for design and manufacturing. The authors present the OpenFlexure Microscope as a case study of implementing HardOps. The paper also notes the growing automation trend in DevOps (automated tests, deployments, releases) and the maturing legal/standards context for open hardware (e.g., CERN open hardware license, DIN definition). While the excerpt outlines the conceptual fit between DevOps and hardware and highlights tooling choices and benefits, it does not provide detailed, tool-level examples (e.g., specific CI jobs for KiCad) in these pages.

The pipeline uses open-source tools (Python, OpenSCAD, Inkscape, ImageMagick, etc.) run inside Ubuntu Docker images to render illustrations and auto-generate STL files, assembly instructions, and bills of materials whenever the repository is updated. Prepare-and-verify CI pipelines create digital production assets, enabling production and quality teams to trial parts, record QA/QC, and feed results back into the DVCS issue/merge-request workflow. The process links issues, merge requests, and commits so design changes and production feedback remain auditable; problems discovered in distributed production are tracked as issues tied to the relevant merge request or commit. The paper also notes potential PLM integrations (automated BOM passing, manufacturing/QA tracking) are desirable but not yet implemented, and highlights that automation complements but does not replace quality personnel. ## pqac-00000021

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a workflow for LLM-driven schematic generation that is guided by a domain knowledge graph (KG) encoding pin-role semantics and electrical constraints. Components in kg component.json receive normalized pin roles derived from automated datasheet extraction and expert validation (example: MGJ2D pin mappings). The KG defines four formal constraint types (e.g., supply pair, must be connected, driving pair, differential pair) used during fast-fail verification to catch topology and net-assignment errors. Data sources include aggregated manufacturer datasheets parsed by scripts; semantic normalization maps vendor-specific pin labels into a unified ontology of 36 pin-role semantics. Role-based verification enables generic checks (e.g., VDD and GND must be on distinct nets). The system supports multi-level feedback (Full/Weak/None) for iterative correction; Full, with pin-level localization, significantly improves pass rates. A custom KiCad component library (symbols, footprints, datasheets) is provided for SKiDL integration, enabling standardized component defs and accurate pin mappings for synthesis and downstream PCB workflows.


---

## pqac-00000022

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen targets text-to-PCB (text-to-schematic) by using SKiDL as the executable intermediate: SKiDL’s Python basis helps LLMs map functional requirements to package info and pin constraints. The authors build a domain-specific Knowledge Graph (KG) from IC datasheets to encode component types, pin-role semantics (R includes 36 pin role types), electrical properties, topological constraints and isolation boundaries; they formally define KG as KG = (C, R, A, Φ, I). The KG dramatically reduces context needs by compressing datasheet content — stated as a reduction from ~16k tokens per IC to ~300 tokens (30–70x less). Prompt engineering techniques include in-context learning (ICL) with multiple design examples and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to guide stepwise reasoning about connectivity and pin roles. Generation is followed by a multi-stage verification pipeline (Figure 2): Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in Electrical Rule Checking (ERC), and topology verification. The verification algorithm builds bipartite and topology graphs (BuildTopologyGraph(σgen, KG)), trims to critical subgraphs, and performs inter- and intra-connection checks to catch violations (e.g., VCC–GND shorts), combining verification KG and design rules.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen proposes a training-free LLM-driven pipeline for PCB schematic generation that uses an LLM as a SKiDL code proposal generator with domain-specific prompting, Chain-of-Thought, and In-Context Learning to synthesize executable design code without fine-tuning. To enforce physical correctness, the system builds an IC-domain knowledge graph (KG) from real-world datasheets capturing pin-role semantics, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints, and couples this KG with SI-driven multi-phase verification to produce interpretable error messages and iterative feedback. The authors curated a benchmark of 23 real-world circuits across digital, analog, and power domains to evaluate PCB generative ability and demonstrated end-to-end capability from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB layout files accounting for real-world IC packages. The paper highlights challenges specific to PCB schematic generation — vendor-specific schematic capture, scarce board-level datasets, and NP-hard multi-component/ routing/placement decisions — and positions PCBSchemaGen as addressing gaps left by prior work that focused mainly on IC-level digital/analog generation and lacked modeling of board-level packaging and pin constraints.


---

## pqac-00000024

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen frames text-to-PCB schematic generation as constraint-guided synthesis with multi-stage verification. Circuits are represented as bipartite graphs (components and nets), and topology verification is cast as a subgraph isomorphism problem; the paper uses the VF2 algorithm for efficient checking. Verification combines knowledge-graph (KG) information and design rules to detect pin/net and ERC violations, and the pipeline emits interpretable, localized error messages that are fed back to the LLM. The system supports three feedback message styles (concise, binary, and detailed with syntax/ERC/subgraph errors and precise pin/net/passive value info). It also implements subcircuit reuse via a library of validated modules to improve hierarchical/complex designs; ablation studies show retrieval helps success rates. The authors built a benchmark of 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks and a component library based on IC datasheets, intentionally including similar-package components to test LLM discrimination. Experiments evaluate various LLMs using prompt strategies (ICL and CoT) alongside the multi-stage verification, feedback loop, and subcircuit reuse, reporting main results and human expert validation.


---

## pqac-00000025

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-based pipeline that translates natural-language PCB schematic specifications into SKiDL code and ultimately KiCAD schematics/layouts. The system favors SKiDL because Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning. A careful prompt-design methodology is used to improve comprehension, connectivity awareness, and adaptability. To inject IC-level physical constraints without flooding the model, PCBSchemaGen encodes essential device rules from datasheets into a compact Knowledge Graph (KG), shrinking per-device token usage from ~16k to ~300 tokens. Generation accuracy is enforced via a multi-stage verification framework combining the KG, electrical design rules, and Subgraph Isomorphism topology checks; verification feedback is returned to the LLM for iterative refinement. The design also includes semantic pin-role labeling (mapping pins to one of 36 roles) and reuse of validated subcircuits. The pipeline steps: user NL input -> agent reads KG and datasheet-derived rules -> agent generates SKiDL -> KiCAD schematic and layout generation.
