## pqac-00000029

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench evaluates LLMs on board-level schematic design using a generative pipeline that mimics human engineering by decomposing tasks (e.g., module partitioning) and a verification engine with two strategies: static rule checking and dynamic transient (SPICE) simulation. Static checks abstract netlists into a topology graph and use pin property matching and protocol consistency algorithms to find connectivity faults, while dynamic verification compiles generated netlists into SPICE scripts to drive LTspice and performs electrical metric monitoring; "the probe voltage or current at each test point must precisely fall within the preset logic threshold window." The benchmark enforces preprocessing of datasheets into structured JSON. Evaluation uses three dimensions—static checks, dynamic simulations, and global correctness—and standardized sampling (T=0.1, top-p=0.95). Models tested include Claude Sonnet 4.5, DeepSeek-v3, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-max, and Grok-4. Static pass rates are relatively high (avg 71.84%; Claude 77.73%; Industrial 83.05%, Automotive 77.03%), dynamic pass rates lower (Claude 67.92%; Qwen3-Max 60.21%; Gemini 60.00%), and global full-design correctness is very low (Claude 8.15%; GPT-5.2 6.58%; others 4–5%), highlighting LLMs' physical-intuition gaps and hallucinations on ambiguous/multiplexed pins.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is a benchmark that evaluates large language models on board-level schematic design across static rule checking and dynamic (SPICE-like) behavior simulation. The paper reports considerably different results between static and dynamic evaluations: average static rule pass rates are around 71.84% while dynamic simulation pass rates drop to about 58.65%, and the overall correct-design success rates reported in TABLE V are very low (single-digit percentages for tested models). Key failure modes include context overload when handling very large, homogeneous component lists (many pins), entity role confusion and label conflicts across modules, and pin-multiplexing errors where LLMs assign mutually exclusive functions to the same MCU pin. The authors note models often produce functionally equivalent connections sufficient for simulation, but struggle with rigid physical constraints and global logical consistency. To mitigate these issues, HWE-Bench applies prompt-engineering interventions such as internal signal-chain inference and structured signal-chain or functional-group constraints to reduce component disorder and resolve pin-multiplexing conflicts. The benchmark highlights that circuit design non-uniqueness and strict physical rules remain major challenges for LLM-based EDA agents.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is an evaluation framework for LLMs on board-level schematic design that includes 300 real-world design tasks across 8 application domains and 2,914 IC datasheets. The benchmark emphasizes strict physical constraints and applies a novel functional group-based rule verification method for static testing. It builds a design constraint model that partitions MCU physical resources into functional bus groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) and a GPIO resource pool based on electrical attributes, and uses preset interface protocol contracts to enforce correct connectivity. A verification system introduces a pin-allocation simulation (‘pin locking’ / ‘pin-locking’ mechanism) that marks pins as ‘locked’ when a netlist connection passes attribute checks and emits LOCK CONFLICT warnings for reuse attempts, preventing resource contention and logical short circuits. HWE-Bench also integrates dynamic validation via SPICE simulation. Evaluation with this framework revealed critical limitations in current models’ physical reasoning, especially in MCU pin multiplexing scenarios. Future work aims to guide LLMs toward reliable reasoning and generation of practically deployable circuit designs.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is an execution-driven benchmark for evaluating large language models on board-level schematic design. The benchmark contains 300 real board-level tasks across 8 application domains sourced from GitHub and OSHWLab and is paired with a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets. For each task, an LLM must generate a schematic from functional requirements and provided datasheets; outputs undergo static electrical rule checking and dynamic verification via an industry-standard LTspice simulator. The framework emulates engineering workflows by using structured prompts that guide module decomposition, component allocation, and netlist generation. HWE-Bench deliberately avoids text-similarity scoring and instead verifies functional correctness through simulation and rule-based checks. The authors evaluated multiple state-of-the-art LLMs (DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4) and report that models show some documentation understanding but generally lack physical intuition, with the top-performing model achieving an overall pass rate of 8.15%. The paper’s contributions include task collection, standardized, machine-checkable task representations, an engineer-like test pipeline, and SOTA model evaluation.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is a benchmark and end-to-end evaluation pipeline for testing LLMs on board-level schematic design. The authors assembled a structured dataset of 300 circuit design tasks and 2,914 cleaned component datasheets, converting multimodal PDFs into JSON and using guided LLM extraction to produce component descriptions and pin definitions. The generative pipeline prompts LLMs sequentially for component allocation, module-level local netlist generation (using a strict plain-text syntax like netX: Component|Pin|Function to reduce formatting hallucinations), and system-level global netlist integration. Outputs are post-processed by scripts that strip Markdown and serialize to a predefined JSON schema. For verification, HWE-Bench uses dual-stage evaluation: static rule checking (rule-based label matching to verify topology and protocol alignment) and dynamic SPICE transient simulation (LLM netlists converted to SPICE and injected into a Python testbench against a human-prepared ground-truth SPICE circuit). The primary metric is the aggregate pass rate across static and dynamic checkpoints; success requires no physical damage and compliance of key node voltages/currents with safety thresholds under specified excitations.


---

## pqac-00000034

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 2-3'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is presented as a comprehensive, execution-driven benchmark to evaluate large language models on end-to-end board-level schematic and circuit design tasks. Motivated by limitations of prior text- and software-centric evaluations, it emphasizes the zero-fault-tolerance nature of physical board design and the insufficiency of discriminative or isolated subtask methods. The benchmark proposes a test pipeline that emulates human engineering cognitive pathways and guides models through multi-step design tasks. Core to HWE-Bench is a dual-verification evaluation: (1) expert-level static electrical rule checking and (2) dynamic physical simulations, together establishing a new standard for validating LLM-generated physical-level system netlists. The benchmark specifically targets the model’s ability to translate unstructured natural language specifications and multimodal datasheet constraints into autonomously generated, globally verified schematic designs, filling a capability gap left by previous benchmarks that focused on parsing, assistive text generation, or isolated routing/placement subtasks.

## pqac-00000035

From text 'song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3'.

Inpyo Song, Eunji Jeon, and Jangwon Lee. Pceval: a benchmark for evaluating physical computing capabilities of large language models. ArXiv, Dec 2601. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2601.02404, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2601.02404. This article has 0 citations.

PCEVAL is a benchmark for assessing LLMs on physical-computing tasks that combine hardware and software (Arduino projects). It is presented as the first benchmark enabling fully automatic evaluation of both logical and physical aspects: circuit generation and code generation, across varying project complexity. The framework decomposes tasks into four dimensions: Logical Circuit Generation, Physical Circuit Generation, Code from Logical Circuits, and Code from Physical Circuits. Outputs are validated automatically using standardized test procedures and circuit validation metrics within a simulation environment; metrics measure logical correctness and physical implementation feasibility (e.g., pin conflicts, extraneous components). The benchmark includes functional success criteria (example: "The test is successful when: Piezo buzzer runs when the button is pressed") and supports reproducible, automated validation without human graders. The authors evaluated 13 LLMs and report that models perform reasonably well on code generation and logical circuit design but struggle with breadboard-level physical layout, especially correct pin connections and avoiding circuit errors. The paper contrasts prior work that relied on manual expert evaluation and often ignored physical layout constraints, highlighting PCEVAL’s contribution toward automated, hardware-aware LLM evaluation.

## pqac-00000037

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks, a board-level hardware description language implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language. The authors position board-level HDLs as a way to raise abstraction and increase automation beyond traditional schematic capture. They emphasize generator-based, programmatic construction (a Chisel-like approach) so a generator defines methodology and can automatically size internal component values for each application. Object-oriented concepts such as type hierarchies and inheritance are proposed to raise design level: types can encode parameters (e.g., voltage and current ratings) to enable correctness checks and to support automated selection among compatible alternatives. Polymorphic Blocks is presented as a library-based flow that uses these ideas; the provided Blinky example shows block instantiation (self.Block(...)) and explicit connections (self.connect(...)). Subcomponents like IndicatorLed are themselves generators that can contain code to size parts (e.g., resistors) based on inputs. Overall, the paper highlights automated component sizing, generator reuse, and type-directed selection as core features for PCB-level HDLs.

## pqac-00000038

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes a Polymorphic Circuit Blocks model and a Python-like hardware construction language (HCL) that supports abstraction and gradual refinement. A class hierarchy lets designers instantiate abstract, generic blocks (e.g., a step-down converter or LED) and later refine them into concrete subclasses; however, replacing an abstract block with a subclass can be unsafe without checks because physical constraints (like current limits) may differ. Ports and links carry typed electrical parameters: ports expose properties (voltage_out, current_limit, current_draw, voltage_limit), and links aggregate those to compute net-level parameters (total current, acceptable voltage range) and run assertions. The electronics layer models power, digital, and analog port types and bundles for multi-wire protocols. Libraries combine primitives and generator blocks — e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, inductors, capacitors, diodes, transistors, and higher-level blocks (pull-ups, decoupling caps) that translate port parameters to component ratings. Automated component selection and sizing examples include RC filter calculation from cutoff frequency and resistive divider selection from E24 values. The system exposes a generator HCL/Python DSL for composing blocks (example: class Blinky(Block): ...).

## pqac-00000039

From text 'lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7'.

Richard Lin, Rohit Ramesh, Connie Chi, Nikhil Jain, Ryan Nuqui, Prabal Dutta, and Björn Hartmann. Polymorphic blocks: unifying high-level specification and low-level control for circuit board design. Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology, Oct 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3379337.3415860, doi:10.1145/3379337.3415860. This article has 26 citations.

Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-based HDL implemented as a library of base classes with mypy annotations, enabling a typed DSL for circuit design. The system uses a hierarchical type/refinement model: abstract blocks can be interactively refined to concrete subclasses (for example, refining an abstract resistor to an SMD part), keeping high-level HDL while handling specifics via refinements. Some blocks act as generators: during lowering the generator is provided concrete parameter values and expands into internal elements. Generators execute once and in no guaranteed order, so they must handle parameter ranges via worst-case bounds. Parameter constraints are parsed into a directed constraint graph and evaluated lazily; constraint forms like assignments and subset bounds are recorded specially. The compiler incrementally lowers the high-level model, performs simplifications, and finally netlists the design as a tree walk, producing netlist files that can be imported into KiCad. The flow supports deterministic component naming tied to HDL variable names to enable netlist updates to in-progress layouts. The project is open-source on GitHub.

## pqac-00000041

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 56-60'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded domain specific language (HCL) for hierarchical circuit/block design. It uses classes and a library of base classes to represent reusable block templates, with Ports, Blocks, and GeneratorBlocks allowing programmatic construction of subcircuits. Generators are member functions that instantiate internal sub-blocks, ports, and parameters; they receive (concrete or bounded) parameter values during compilation and can "access solved values like digital logic thresholds, and use those to automatically size internal blocks like the resistor," enabling automated component selection and sizing. The DSL includes syntactic helpers such as implicit_connect (for common nets like power/ground) and chain (for linear signal-through topologies). A GUI visualizer supports refinement by letting users choose block subclass refinements interactively; refined subcircuits are generated and model checks run. The compiler lowers the high-level model via a tree walk performing refinement and generation, updates a constraint graph (constraints parsed into a directed graph), and requires generators to supply worst-case bounds because generators run once and in no particular order. The lowered design can be exported as a netlist for KiCad. The project is open-source on GitHub.

Polymorphic Blocks introduces an HDL built around hierarchical block diagrams, a type system, and an electronics model. The language exposes blocks, ports, and links: blocks are the primary user-facing elements, ports describe interfaces (pins, GPIO, busses) and can carry parameters, and links describe connections and parameter propagation. The system supports block-level polymorphism and a class hierarchy so users can write abstract blocks (e.g., a step-down converter) that are later refined into concrete subcircuits (e.g., specific buck converters tied to controller chips). Generators and subcircuit generators are a core mechanism (and the work cites approaches that embed generators in general-purpose languages), but this system augments generators with explicit electronics modeling to enable design support such as automated parts selection and correctness checks. The tool provides a visualization/refinement interface and can export a netlist for PCB layout. Overall, the HDL emphasizes higher-level, hierarchical specification plus generator-driven reuse, with type and parameter constraints to enable automated selection and synthesis support.

Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded hardware construction language (HCL) where classes form a type hierarchy (re-usable block templates) and objects are instances. The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks' layered model combining structural and class hierarchies to support both encapsulation and abstraction in PCB/schematic design. Structural hierarchy (hierarchy blocks, bridges) lets sub-sheets export or bridge ports to containing blocks, preserving internal parameters; bridges are two-ported blocks that map external-facing ports to flipped internal roles. The class hierarchy enables abstract parts and subclasses (is-a relationships), allowing delaying concrete specification and reuse. An electronics layer models pin/link types and part ratings; ports carry physical properties (voltage output, current limits, current draw, voltage limits) and parameters "flow" through connected ports into links, which aggregate net properties and include assertions to check correctness. The system includes generators (e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, plus inductor, capacitor, diode, and transistor generators from parts tables) and wraps primitives in higher-level library blocks. Block constraints and link assertions provide automated checks for compatibility and invariants. Together, these features support automated component selection and propagation of electrical parameters across hierarchical and typed block abstractions.

## pqac-00000052

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 97-100'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt is a bibliography pointing to multiple research and tool directions relevant to programmatic circuit creation and automated smart-device design. Key citations discuss circuit-board-level hardware description languages and their opportunities and challenges, and propose integrating code and visual schematics for interactive editing. Work on "Polymorphic Blocks" aims to bridge high-level specifications and low-level control in PCB design, while examples of custom HDLs for PCB capture show practical language-based capture approaches. Several prototyping and authoring tools are referenced—Fritzing, KiCad (and action plugins), AutoFritz (autocomplete for virtual breadboards), and VirtualComponent (mixed-reality tuning)—indicating emphasis on rapid iteration, mixed visual/code workflows, and support for amateurs and designers. Foundational and historical automation efforts (e.g., AUTO CARD) and studies on scaling hardware production highlight manufacturing and scaling challenges beyond prototyping. References to live programming and interactive visualization (e.g., "The Future of Programming will be Live", "Usable Live Programming", and Bifröst) suggest benefits of immediate feedback in embedded/hardware development. Together these works support approaches that combine HDLs, interactive visual tools, and automation to enable programmatic generation of smart devices.

## pqac-00000053

From text 'guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 20-24'.

J Garza Guardado. “sudo make me a smart device”: accelerating and automating the end-to-end design of smart devices. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes three tools that automate and programmatically generate smart-device designs by encapsulating circuit and firmware information into higher-level representations. Amalgam embeds firmware functionality into web technologies (HTML/CSS/JS), enabling rapid prototyping and allowing designers to ‘‘transform web pages into fully functional electronic devices in a couple of minutes,’’ but it provides limited low-level configurability (low threshold, low ceiling). Appliancizer builds on Amalgam to also integrate circuit design data and automatically generate complete electronic designs (including PCB layout) and low-level code from static web prototypes; it uses "essential interface mapping" to map HTML controls to tangible controls, enabling circuit synthesis and device simulation, and can turn web apps (e.g., video player, smart thermostat) into physical devices without changing source code, showing a 6.5X speed improvement over Amalgam. TypedSchematics targets schematic reuse and scalability: it offers real-time validation of mismatches, an abstraction called Mats to remove repetitive ground/voltage connections, and a flow-based model for constraint checking; users produced thermostat schematics 2.8x faster than with Fusion 360. The work highlights trade-offs between ease-of-use, automation, configurability, and library scalability in programmatic circuit creation and end-to-end smart-device design automation.

The excerpt discusses board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) and the potential to apply programming-language concepts—specifically generator languages and type systems—to automate and simplify circuit board design. Such programmatic approaches can encapsulate low-level knowledge in libraries, increasing efficiency and accessibility for creating devices. A key challenge identified is user confidence: simulation and static modeling are incomplete, and community mechanisms (for example, marking subcircuits as “physically fabricated and tested”) may help vets reusable components and generators. The authors note that confidence in generators (which produce many instances) is different from confidence in individual fabricated subcircuits and may require additional processes. They highlight many open research questions spanning models, abstractions, and user interfaces, and recommend cross-disciplinary work across electrical engineering, programming languages, and HCI to develop powerful, usable systems. This context is directly relevant to efforts like JITX, Stanza, and other programmatic/design-automation approaches for creating smart devices, particularly around generator-based design, reusable libraries, testing/validation, and tooling/UI needs.

Lin et al. describe Polymorphic Blocks, an approach that implements an HDL and compiler around a hierarchy block-diagram abstraction to provide design automation. The system lets designers compose high-level blocks while experienced engineers write re-usable generators—executable code that implements block designs—so complex subcircuits can be generated from high-level specifications and instantiated in hardware-proven examples. The approach is library-driven: good, complete part and subcircuit libraries are needed to maximize reuse, and many complex part details remain trapped in PDF datasheets. The paper suggests automating datasheet extraction (e.g., Tabula, DocParser) to populate libraries. They note potential integration with graphical, schematic-like interfaces to lower the need for programming experience, but also list current limitations: the electronics model only defines a few signal interfaces and lacks support for multiple grounds. The work targets intermediate-level designers and demonstrates a generator-based, programmatic path to circuit creation while highlighting scalability, tooling, and data requirements for broader adoption.

Most relevant evidence (with ids):

## pqac-00000060

From text 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 4-4'.

Sergio Vinagrero Gutiérrez, Giorgio Di Natale, and Elena-Ioana Vatajelu. Python framework for modular and parametric spice netlists generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224. This article has 5 citations.

The excerpt describes a Python framework for modular, parametric SPICE netlist generation (Gutierrez et al., 2023). It demonstrates dynamic, parameter-driven construction of a ring-oscillator-based design where N_RO_PER_CHAIN and N_CHAINS control inverter count and number of chains, and where multiplexer inputs are generated from N_CHAINS. The framework reads external analog components written in Verilog-A via a VerilogAReader and integrates them (VCounter = reader.load("/path/to/counter.va")). Examples show high-level primitives: Subcircuit, Circuit, NamedChain, Chain, and into_subckt to build hierarchical netlists, plus device-level additions like Mosfet declarations for nmos/pmos. Netlist composition operators (e.g., netlist += chains) and parallel composition (Parallel(VCounter([""]), N_CHAINS)) are used to assemble systems. The paper cites integration/compatibility with ecosystem tools, explicitly referencing PySpice and SKiDL in the references, implying interoperability with existing Python EDA and SPICE workflows. Overall, the excerpt shows a code-oriented, reusable approach to programmatically generate hierarchical SPICE netlists and incorporate external Verilog-A components for analog/ mixed-signal designs.


---

## pqac-00000061

From text 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3'.

Sergio Vinagrero Gutiérrez, Giorgio Di Natale, and Elena-Ioana Vatajelu. Python framework for modular and parametric spice netlists generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224. This article has 5 citations.

The paper contrasts existing Python EDA utilities such as PySpice and SKiDL with a new framework focused on modular, parametric SPICE netlist generation. It notes PySpice is a utility to generate SPICE netlists (and SKiDL is built on top of it) but that PySpice-based flows are limited to NGspice and Xyce and mainly export netlists for PCB designs. The presented framework is designed to support any simulator and adds higher-level features for design-space exploration: parameters may be static or dynamically computed via Python functions or SymPy, grouped into ParamSets (like process corners), and loaded from common files/PDKs via parser interfaces (e.g., Reader.load). Components are modeled via a Component class (with metadata and Verilog-A support), and instantiation/connection are simplified with overloaded operators (@ and %) to create multiple instances and modify connections/parameters programmatically. Additional utilities include Array generation for 1D/2D layouts and defect-injection examples, enabling modular, reusable circuits and easier parametrization compared to traditional PySpice/SKiDL PCB-focused flows.


---

## pqac-00000062

From text 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 3-4'.

Sergio Vinagrero Gutiérrez, Giorgio Di Natale, and Elena-Ioana Vatajelu. Python framework for modular and parametric spice netlists generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224. This article has 5 citations.

The paper presents a Python framework for generating modular, parametric SPICE netlists with manipulation primitives to quickly build complex and reusable connection patterns. It supports instantiating components in 1D or 2D arrays (useful for crossbar arrays, 2D CMOS sensors, MEMS matrices) and provides operations such as Parallel and Chain to create parallel groups or daisy-chains of components. Components can be grouped into subcircuits (SPICE subcircuits) and circuits can be converted into subcircuits for reuse. The framework includes an Inject manipulation example (class Inject(Manip)) that can probabilistically inject defects into component chains, enabling process-variability and fault-resilience studies. Export is flexible and simulator-agnostic via exporter interfaces (e.g., exporter.dump(circuit) and exporter.dump_to_file("/path/to/file")), allowing designs to be saved in different formats or fed to various simulators. The authors emphasize fast design-space exploration and parametrization, plan to add library/layout management and layout generation for technology nodes, and consider supporting tools to create schematics from generated netlists.


---

## pqac-00000063

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, which leverages LLMs to generate SKiDL code in a training-free manner and iteratively refines designs via a verifier. It explicitly uses SKiDL for Python-based schematic generation (example SKiDL Python Code lines show Part(...) declarations and ERC() checks) and integrates with KiCAD footprints to produce KiCAD PCB layout files from natural-language specifications. For analog simulation and SPICE netlist-style modeling, prior work (AnalogCoder) modeled analog circuits as PySpice code, indicating PySpice is a relevant tool for parametric SPICE netlist generation. The paper highlights a major obstacle for data-driven PCB automation: vendor-specific schematic capture formats limit large-scale training. To address correctness and physical constraints, PCBSchemaGen builds an IC-domain knowledge graph and rule-driven verifier that provides interpretable feedback, anchoring LLM outputs to datasheet pin roles and topological constraints. The authors also curated a 23-circuit benchmark spanning digital, analog, and power domains to validate end-to-end generation from NL spec to KiCAD-ready files. These points link SKiDL, PySpice, and knowledge-augmented verification as components of PCB design automation and parametric netlist generation workflows.

## pqac-00000064

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free framework for automating PCB schematic design that uses LLM-driven code generation targeting SKiDL (a Python-style hardware description). The method treats schematics as SKiDL code and leverages an LLM as a proposal generator to produce that code, combined with domain-specific prompting, chain-of-thought, and in-context learning techniques. Generated proposals are verified by a datasheet-derived Knowledge Graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism-based verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological constraints to ensure correctness at IC package and pin levels. The authors note a scarcity of open PCB schematic data and argue that SPICE, while used for analog simulation, is insufficient to validate PCB schematic correctness under real IC package/pin constraints. The work is open-sourced (GitHub link) and emphasizes a decoupled architecture (generation + constraint-guided verification) that avoids large-scale training while improving interpretability and reliability for PCB schematic generation.

## pqac-00000065

From text 'rukade2026esimsemesterlong pages 5-8'.

AA Rukade. Esim semester long internship autumn 2025 on ihp openpdk integration in esim. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt describes eSim's Python-based integration for generating and simulating SPICE netlists. eSim uses a Python/PyQt5 frontend to glue together KiCad (schematic capture/PCB), Ngspice (simulation), and HDL tools. The KiCad-to-Ngspice converter resides in src/kicadtoNgspice/ and implements an 8-step pipeline: reading the KiCad-generated .cir netlist (Processing.py), preprocessing (parameter replacement, lowercasing, merging continuation lines), separating dot-options, identifying sources and parsing their parameters, converting ICs by matching components to XML model parameter files, presenting a GUI for model/device/subcircuit parameters (KicadtoNgspice.py), producing a final .cir.out with .model/.include/.lib directives (Convert.py), and running Ngspice for simulation. The document notes PDK integrations (e.g., SKY130, IHP) hook into steps 4–7, indicating where modular parametric model mapping and netlist generation are implemented. This provides a concrete example of a Python framework for parametric SPICE netlist generation and integration with schematic and PDK workflows.

## pqac-00000066

From text 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 1-2'.

Sergio Vinagrero Gutiérrez, Giorgio Di Natale, and Elena-Ioana Vatajelu. Python framework for modular and parametric spice netlists generation. ArXiv, Jun 2023. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224. This article has 5 citations.

The paper presents a Python-based, open-source framework for generating modular, parametric SPICE netlists intended to support design space exploration for analog/mixed-signal circuits. The authors emphasize creating "modular and reusable" electrical components and providing "powerful manipulation primitives" so designs can be serialized and "even exported to work with any major available simulator." The motivation is that analog/Mixed-Signal design automation lags behind digital RTL flows: many high-level tools target Register Transfer Level (RTL) and "are not very well suited for analog and mixed signal designs." The excerpt also notes that scripting approaches are commonly used to "generate designs based on high abstract" descriptions to speed iteration, and it references PySpice as an existing Python utility to "generate SPICE netlists," but with simulator limitations ("limited to NGspice and Xyce and the netlists can only be exported"). Overall, the framework aims to provide a simulator-agnostic, reusable Python tooling layer for parametric SPICE netlist generation where existing tools like PySpice or RTL-oriented frameworks fall short for analog DSE.

## pqac-00000067

From text 'rukade2026esimsemesterlong pages 8-14'.

AA Rukade. Esim semester long internship autumn 2025 on ihp openpdk integration in esim. Unknown journal, 2026.

The excerpt describes eSim's Python-based netlist generation and PDK-integration pipeline. The converter module (src/kicadtoNgspice) contains Convert.py, Processing.py, DeviceModel.py and related GUI/conversion code; Convert.py produces the final .cir.out with .model, .include, and .lib directives. PDK integrations (SKY130, IHP, future PDKs) hook into specific pipeline steps, so the tool must detect, parse, and map PDK components into the netlist. For the IHP SG13G2 PDK, additional runtime model loading is required: Verilog-A compact models are compiled with OpenVAF into .osdi shared libraries, and Ngspice must be built from source with --enable-osdi (Ngspice 45.2 recommended) to load them at runtime. The project updated installation scripts to automate Ngspice compilation, OpenVAF installation, and the IHP PDK setup. Directory structure and model libraries (modelParamXML, deviceModelLibrary) are used to store component definitions and SPICE model files. These details illustrate a Python framework approach that combines parametric device libraries, a netlist converter, and runtime linkage of compiled device models for simulation.

| atopile | Custom `.ato` HDL compiled by Python tooling into KiCad projects; textual electronics “design-as-code” | Netlist, KiCad schematic/PCB artifacts, BOM; Gerbers typically via downstream KiCad export | Strong module/library reuse and parameterized blocks; better than raw netlists for reusable subcircuits | Relies largely on compiler checks plus downstream KiCad ERC/DRC/manufacturing checks | Very good: text-first, Git-friendly, scriptable, suited to CI-generated outputs | Early-stage but active | Apache-2.0 |
| tscircuit | TypeScript/React/JSX model: PCB described as components in code (“React-for-PCB”) | Gerbers, BOM, pick-and-place/net connectivity outputs via toolchain | Strong software-style composition, props, package reuse, monorepo ergonomics | Tool-native checks plus downstream fab/export validation; less proven than KiCad-native DRC flows | Excellent: npm/TS ecosystem, deterministic builds, easy CI rendering | Early-stage / rapidly evolving | MIT |
| SKiDL | Python EDSL for schematic capture; instantiate `Part`, `Net`, connect in Python | Netlists directly; can feed KiCad flows, BOM generation, and downstream PCB/Gerber export | High: full Python for loops/functions/classes; reusable subcircuits and generators | Built-in ERC, syntax/runtime checks; often paired with KiCad/PCB tool DRC later (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) | Excellent: plain text, Python testing, easy automation in CI | Mature open-source niche; widely cited in research/prototypes (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000061) | MIT |
| BHDL | Embedded DSL in Racket; every circuit is a function; layout combinators and Jupyter REPL (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000011) | KiCad-format PCB, BOM, Gerber via export/routing flow (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047) | Strong: first-class circuits, host-language loops/functions, hierarchical composition, series/parallel/bus combinators (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000012) | Autoplacement, legalization, routability-aware placement; verification less mature than mainstream EDA DRC (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047) | Good for research/CI due to text + notebook flow, but ecosystem smaller than Python/KiCad | Research prototype | Open-source research code |
| Polymorphic Blocks | Python-embedded HDL/HCL with types, generators, constraint graph, GUI-assisted refinement (pqac-00000037, pqac-00000039) | Netlist export to KiCad/layout tools (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000043) | Very strong: abstract blocks, subclasses, generators, automated sizing/selection from constraints (pqac-00000038, pqac-00000041) | Strong semantic checking via typed ports/links and constraint propagation, but mostly research-grade (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000044) | Good in principle: textual HDL and deterministic naming aid version control (pqac-00000039) | Research prototype | Open-source research code (pqac-00000039) |
| JITX | Commercial programmatic PCB in Stanza; PCB generators, constraints, and layout automation | Netlist, BOM, placement/routing outputs, manufacturing files incl. Gerber from product flow | Very strong: libraries, generators, algorithmic placement/routing, reusable design patterns | Strong integrated rule/constraint-driven automation; one of the most capable code-native PCB systems | Good technically, but proprietary/runtime licensing complicates open CI compared with FOSS | Commercially mature | Proprietary/commercial |
| PCBmodE | Python + SVG/XML-driven PCB definition; board artwork and geometry as code/data | SVG-centric board descriptions; Gerber/manufacturing output supported through flow | Moderate: parametric geometry/artwork good; electrical abstraction weaker than HDL-style tools | Limited electrical verification compared with mainstream EDA; best for geometry-driven/simple boards | Good for Git because source is text/SVG, but narrower scope and smaller ecosystem | Older niche project; limited recent activity | Open-source (CC-BY-SA/GPL-style project assets/code mix) |
| gEDA/gaf | Traditional Unix-style open EDA suite; text schematics/netlists plus scriptable utilities | Netlist, BOM, Gerber via pcb/gerbv toolchain | Moderate: scriptable and library-based, but less ergonomic parametrization than newer HDL flows | Established ERC/DRC depending on subtool; manufacturing outputs proven | Good: text files and CLI tools suit CI, though workflow feels legacy | Mature but aging | GPL |
| Horizon EDA | GUI-first open-source EDA with structured text-backed project data and some scriptability | Gerber, drill, BOM, pick-and-place, netlist | Moderate: strong part/library reuse; less “full code CAD” than atopile/SKiDL/JITX | Strong native DRC/manufacturing outputs for open-source desktop EDA | Good: project files are Git-manageable and CLI/export automation is possible, though not as code-native as HDL tools | Active and increasingly capable | GPL-3.0 |
| Edea | Python library/tooling for generating or manipulating KiCad project files programmatically | KiCad schematics/PCB data, netlists, and downstream Gerber/BOM through KiCad | Moderate to strong: Python-based templating/manipulation enables reuse around KiCad artifacts | Usually delegates true ERC/DRC to KiCad after generation | Very good: ideal for repository automation, transformations, and CI wrappers around KiCad | Early-stage / tooling layer rather than full EDA | Open-source |
| KiCad Python / `kicad-cli` | Hybrid model: GUI editor plus Python API/action plugins and command-line automation | Native Gerber, drill, IPC netlist, BOM, STEP, position files, PDFs | Moderate: reuse via hierarchical sheets, templates, scripts, plugins, generated footprints/boards | Strong mainstream ERC/DRC and manufacturing checks; CLI-friendly in newer releases | Very good: central choice for open hardware CI/CD; scriptable exports/checks align with HardOps principles (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017) | Very mature, dominant open-source PCB tool | GPL-3.0 |


*Table: This table compares major code-based and scriptable PCB/electronics design approaches across programming model, outputs, verification, reuse, CI fit, maturity, and licensing. It is useful for selecting a reproducible, version-controlled workflow and distinguishing research prototypes from production-capable tools.*

function.arguments: {"artifact_type": "table", "description": "Create a summary table of LLM/AI-based text-to-PCB and text-to-schematic research prototypes. Include rows for: PCBSchemaGen (Zou et al.), HWE-Bench (Qiu et al.), PCEval (Song et al.), and general LLM+SKiDL approaches. Columns: System, Approach, Intermediate Representation, Verification Method, Benchmark Size, Best Reported Performance, Key Limitations, Year/Status. Use the evidence from the gathered papers."}
function.name: create_artifact

messages.content:
### Artifact (ID artifact-01)

| System | Approach | Intermediate Representation | Verification Method | Benchmark Size | Best Reported Performance | Key Limitations | Year/Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCBSchemaGen (Zou et al.) | Training-free LLM pipeline for natural-language-to-schematic generation using domain-specific prompting, CoT, ICL, KG-guided constraints, and iterative feedback (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000028) | SKiDL Python code, then KiCad schematic/layout artifacts (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025) | Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in ERC, knowledge-graph pin-role checks, topology verification via subgraph isomorphism/VF2, iterative localized feedback (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000021) | 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks across digital, analog, and power domains (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000027) | Hard-task success reported at 78.1%; easy-task success 93.3%; best model ~23k tokens/task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 min/task (~37× human speed) (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027) | Failures from misunderstanding pin functions and generating incorrect abstract topologies; depends on curated KG/component library (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000021) | 2025 research prototype / open-source benchmark+code (pqac-00000028) |
| HWE-Bench (Qiu et al.) | Execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design; prompts models through module decomposition, component allocation, local netlists, and global integration (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | Structured plain-text/JSON netlists derived from NL specs and datasheets; converted to SPICE/test representations for checking (pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034) | Dual verification: static rule checking on topology/protocol/pin properties plus dynamic LTspice transient simulation with threshold checks; pin-locking/resource-conflict simulation (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033) | 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000031) | Best overall full-design pass rate only 8.15%; average static pass ~71.84%, dynamic ~58.65% (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032) | Major failures in context overload, role confusion, multiplexed-pin conflicts, and poor global physical reasoning; models often pass local/static checks but fail full design correctness (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030) | 2025 benchmark/evaluation framework |
| PCEval (Song et al.) | Benchmark for physical-computing LLM tasks spanning circuit + code generation for Arduino-like projects; evaluates both logical and physical design ability (pqac-00000035) | Logical circuit descriptions and physical circuit layouts with associated code artifacts in simulation (pqac-00000035) | Automated simulation-based validation of logical correctness and physical feasibility, including pin-conflict and extraneous-component checks (pqac-00000035) | Not stated in gathered excerpt; covers multiple project complexities and 13 LLMs (pqac-00000035) | No single percentage cited in gathered excerpt; qualitative result is that models do reasonably on code/logical circuit generation but struggle on breadboard-level physical layout (pqac-00000035) | Weakness on physical wiring/layout correctness despite better performance on code and logical circuits; oriented to physical-computing tasks rather than full PCB CAD (pqac-00000035) | 2025 benchmark |
| General LLM + SKiDL approaches | Use an LLM to synthesize executable Python-based schematic code from text, leveraging SKiDL because it is concise, code-native, and better aligned with LLMs than vendor schematic formats (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000064) | SKiDL Python as the central executable IR; downstream conversion to KiCad schematics/PCB files (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000063) | Typically combines SKiDL/ERC checks with downstream KiCad generation; stronger systems add datasheet-derived knowledge graphs, topological checks, and iterative feedback loops (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000063) | No single standard benchmark; examples include PCBSchemaGen's 23-task suite and related evaluation frameworks like HWE-Bench/PCEval (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035) | Most convincing evidence comes from PCBSchemaGen rather than generic SKiDL prompting; generic approaches remain highly variable and dataset-constrained (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000064) | Limited by scarce open PCB data, vendor-specific capture formats, package/pin constraints, and insufficient physical verification if using only syntax/ERC or SPICE (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000064) | 2023-2025 emerging pattern rather than a single mature tool (pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064) |


*Table: This table compares the main recent LLM-based text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB research systems, their intermediate representations, verification strategies, benchmark scales, and limitations. It is useful for distinguishing promising constraint-guided approaches from broader benchmarks that reveal current reliability gaps.*

Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer: [artifact-00, artifact-01]

function.arguments: {"plan_objective_id": 9, "updated_plan_objective_json": "{\"id\": 9, \"objective\": \"Create summary artifacts (comparison table, key findings) and submit final answer\", \"rationale\": \"Need to organize findings into a clear, well-structured answer with artifacts.\", \"status\": \"completed\", \"result\": \"Two comparison tables created (artifact-00: tool comparison table, artifact-01: LLM/AI systems table). Ready to submit final answer.\", \"evaluation\": \"Artifacts created and comprehensive evidence gathered to support a thorough answer.\"}"}
function.name: update_plan

info.name: create_artifact
info.description:
Create an artifact that can be used in the final answer, based on information
you have collected so far.

How it works: the full message history thus far is passed to another LLM,
along with the question and your description. The LLM is tasked to create
an artifact that matches your specifications, including a title, caption,
and markdown content. You drive the process by specifying the type and
describing what you want.

Only use this tool *after* you have gathered sufficient evidence or
information to create a new artifact. Do not call this tool in parallel
with tools that are searching for information or evidence that would be
necessary to create this artifact.

It is very helpful after calls to several search tools,
after calls to the `gather_evidence` tool, but it is not required.
However, it should be called before the `answer` tool at
least one time, but more may be given if specifically helpful for
the task, so that the `answer` tool can use the artifacts for the answer.

An artifact can be any string representable fact or list of facts,
formatted using markdown syntax. The artifact will be generated as one of
the following types:
- table: Markdown tables with rows and columns (e.g., findings, methods, molecules)
- code_block: Code snippets in fenced code blocks
- blockquote: Quoted text or important facts (using > prefix)
- image: AI-generated image (chart, diagram, or visualization)

Each artifact consists of:
- A short title (3-8 words) that names what the artifact contains
- A caption (1-2 sentences) describing what the artifact shows and why it's useful
- Markdown content in a single element type (validated automatically with retries if needed)
  For image type, content is a markdown image embed ``![title](data:image/png;base64,...)``,
  embedding the PNG directly as a base64 data URL.

artifact_type.description:
Artifact type to create. Must be one of: table,
code_block, blockquote, image.
artifact_type.title: Artifact Type

description.description:
Description of what you want in the artifact. Be specific
about what information should be included, how it should be organized,
and what columns/sections/content you want. This guides the summary LLM.
For image type, describe the visual you want generated in detail.
description.title: Description

info.name: gather_evidence
info.description:
Given a specific question, gather evidence from full-text of documents that have been retrieved by
previous tool calls.

Here is how the tool works: the provided question is posed to all texts retrieved thus far
by other tools. An LLM sees each text independently and is prompted to find information relevant
to the question.

That is, we sample evidence `e \sim LLM(prompt, text, q)` for each text. We only return pieces of
evidence `e` that are sufficiently relevant to the question; if the text is irrelevant, then `e`
is not included in the final response of this tool. Note that this LLM does not see the conversation
history thus far, only the question and each text. Note that it only sees text, no images or other
media from the contents (though tables and formulae may be parseable in some cases).

Think of this tool as an efficient way to deeply scan all retrieved documents for evidence. It
is complementary to the `read_text` tool, which can be used to directly inspect a subset of the
retrieved documents.

A valuable time to invoke this tool is after another tool finds potentially relevant documents.
Feel free to invoke this tool in parallel with other tools, but do not call this tool in parallel with itself.
Only invoke this tool when the document count is above zero, or this tool will be useless.

If you call this tool 2+ times and repeatedly get 'No relevant evidence found',
consider whether the answer might be in visual content (figures/tables) rather than text,
and use appropriate tools if available to access that content.

Once you receive evidence from this tool:

1. Read Carefully: Don't skim. The evidence may contain multiple similar terms
   or values—make sure you identify the RIGHT one.

2. For Comparative Questions: If the question asks "which X has greatest Y",
   check that the evidence includes information about ALL relevant X options, not
   just one or two. If you only see partial information, gather more evidence.

3. Check for Noise: Verify that numbers or terms aren't just metadata
   (page numbers, reference IDs, dates). Look for the value in meaningful
   scientific context.

4. Context Matters: A term appearing 71 times doesn't mean it's always the answer.
   Verify which occurrence actually answers the question asked.

If the evidence doesn't clearly answer the question, gather more evidence before
concluding. Don't guess based on partial information.

question.description: Specific question to gather evidence for.
question.title: Question

text_name_focus_list.description:
if there are known text names that are relevant to the question,
you can specify them here. This will ensure that the tool will look at these text names
first, and then look at the rest of the evidence. If left as `None`, an embedding
search will be performed on underlying content to find most relevant texts.
text_name_focus_list.title: Text Name Focus List

info.name: collect_cited_papers_in_evidence
info.description:
Collect papers by traversing the citations of relevant papers to increase the paper count.

This tool has no effect if called when paper count or relevant papers are zero.
This tool will find papers that are likely to lead to more relevant evidence.
The papers found have a higher chance of relevance because this tool starts from relevant evidence.
This tool's usefulness increases with the amount of gathered evidence as there's more citations to consider.

info.name: view_images
info.description:
Retrieve images from a document.

Use this tool when you need to see figures, tables, or other visual content.
The tool works by asking a weaker LLM agent to scan through the document, looking for
visual content matching the query you provide. When the agent finds matches, it will
crop the page to the relevant region. It can be tasked to return multiple images,
if desired. Note that the weaker LLM may be worse at *understanding* images than
you, so double-check its conclusions about the returned images. It is also stateless,
i.e. it will not remember what you have asked it to find in the past.

Use this tool carefully. It is difficult to retrieve images, and the images
themselves will fill up your context window quickly. Here are some guidelines:
1. ALWAYS use this tool if the question explicitly mentions 'Table X' or 'Figure Y'.
   These questions require visual content and cannot be answered with text alone.
2. Use this tool as a fallback when gather_evidence fails repeatedly (2+ times
   returning 'No relevant evidence'). This suggests the answer may be in visual content.
3. When gather_evidence returns text mentioning 'see Figure X' or 'shown in Table Y',
   you should call this tool to examine that specific visual reference.
4. Only call this tool after directly inspecting relevant text with read_text
   to confirm the document likely contains the visual you need.
5. Don't use this tool in a broad-spectrum way (don't call on many documents
   just to see what might be relevant).
6. Never call this tool in parallel with itself.
7. Users like visual evidence, so call this tool at least once before answering so you
   can provide some image citations in the answer. Add this to your plan so you do not forget.
   For images to be presented to the user, they must be cited in the final answer after retrieval
   by this tool. You may ignore this instruction if the request is for the question is asking
   for a specific piece of information and is not an open-ended research question.
8. NEVER cite full-page images in your final answer, unless they are full-page panels figures or
   tables. Cite relevant cropped regions if available, otherwise consider calling this tool again
   to find a smaller region that answers the query.

query.description:
Query to search for visual content in the document. If you know the specific
figure/table/formula you are looking for, you can specify it here (e.g. "find me
Figure 3" or "extract the table on page 10"). You can also request based on content
(e.g. "find me all volcano plots of gene X" or "I need the formula for the growth rate").
You can even include explicit instructions for the LLM agent to follow (if helpful, not
necessarily required). The more specific and clear your query is here, the higher the
chance of success.
query.title: Query

text_name.description: Name of a text chunk (e.g., 'smith2023example pages 1-5').
text_name.title: Text Name

info.name: read_text
info.description:
Given a list of text names, read their contents.

This tool is valuable to read the full contents of specific parts of documents,
complementing the `gather_evidence` tool's indirect approach.
This can be an especially useful tool if `gather_evidence` does not find evidence,
but you think it is very likely that specific documents are still relevant to the question.

text_name_list.description:
A list of up to 10 text names to read.
These text names can be any valid text names retrieved by other tools.
text_name_list.title: Text Name List

info.name: update_plan
info.description:
Update the plan for a specific or new objective using a JSON string to create or
replace the existing objective.

The updated_json should be a JSON string representing a single plan objective object.

You may use this tool to update an objective, create a new one, or change
strategy if the current plan is not working. Only change strategy significantly
as a last resort, after trying very hard to make the current plan work.

Only mark an objective as completed if you are absolutely sure that it has been fully
met. If it is only partially met, you may add additional objectives to the plan for
what is missing.

plan_objective_id.description: The id of the objective to update.
plan_objective_id.title: Plan Objective Id

updated_plan_objective_json.description:
A JSON string representing the updated objective.
It should be a dictionary with the with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id matching plan_objective_id, if not found, a new objective is created.
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
updated_plan_objective_json.title: Updated Plan Objective Json

info.name: open_targets_search
info.description:
Search for disease-target associations from OpenTargets database.

Adds new disease-target association data to the state and returns top search results.

disease_names.description:
List of disease names to search for.
Usage:
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Returns top targets for Alzheimer's
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's", "Diabetes"], target_names = ["PSEN1"] ->
    Filters for only PSEN1-Alzheimer's/Diabetes evidence
disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
disease_names.title: Disease Names

target_names.description:
List of target names to search for.
Usage:
target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Returns top diseases associated with BACE1
target_names = ["BACE1", "APP"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for only these target-Alzheimer's pairs
target_names = ["BACE1"], drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
target_names.title: Target Names

drug_names.description:
List of drug names to generate or filter results.
Usage:
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"] ->
    Returns all disease-target pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], disease_names = ["Alzheimer's"] ->
    Filters for Alzheimer's pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names = ["Aducanumab"], target_names = ["BACE1"] ->
    Filters for BACE1 disease pairs treated by Aducanumab
drug_names.title: Drug Names

info.name: clinical_trials_search
info.description:
Search for clinical trials, with support for repeated calls and concurrent execution.

Will add new clinical trials to the state, and return metadata about the number of trials found.

query.description:
The search query string. Supports complex boolean expressions, field-specific
searches, and query modifiers through operators. All configuration is done through
operators in the query string.
Query Syntax:
    Basic Search:
        Simple text automatically uses default EXPANSION[Relaxation] and COVERAGE[Contains]
        >>> "heart attack"

    Modified Search:
        Use operators to modify search behavior:
        >>> 'EXPANSION[None]COVERAGE[FullMatch]"exact phrase"'
        >>> 'EXPANSION[Concept]heart attack'

    Field Search:
        Specify fields using AREA operator:
        >>> 'AREA[InterventionName]aspirin'
        >>> 'AREA[Phase]PHASE3'

    Location Search:
        Use SEARCH operator for compound location queries:
        >>> 'cancer AND SEARCH[Location](AREA[LocationCity]Boston AND AREA[LocationState]Massachusetts)'

    Complex Boolean:
        Combine terms with AND, OR, NOT and parentheses:
        >>> '(cancer OR tumor) AND NOT (EXPANSION[None]pediatric OR AREA[StdAge]CHILD)'

    Date Ranges:
        Use RANGE to specify date ranges with formats like "yyyy-MM" or "yyyy-MM-dd".
        Note that MIN and MAX can be used for open-ended ranges:
        >>> AREA[ResultsFirstPostDate]RANGE[2015-01-01, MAX]

Operators:
    EXPANSION[type]: Controls term expansion
        - None: Exact match only, case and accent sensitive
        - Term: Includes lexical variants (plurals, spellings)
        - Concept: Includes UMLS synonyms
        - Relaxation: Relaxes adjacency requirements (default)
        - Lossy: Allows missing partial terms

    COVERAGE[type]: Controls text matching
        - FullMatch: Must match entire field
        - StartsWith: Must match beginning of field
        - EndsWith: Must match end of field
        - Contains: Must match part of field (default)

    AREA[field]: Specifies field to search
        - See Field Reference for available fields

    SEARCH[type]: Groups field searches
        - Location: Groups location-related fields
        - Study: Groups study-related fields

Usage Notes:
    - All search expressions are implicitly OR expressions
    - Operator precedence (highest to lowest): terms/source operators, NOT/context operators, AND, OR
    - Use quotes for exact phrase matching: "heart attack"
    - Use parentheses for grouping: (heart OR cardiac) AND attack
    - Use backslash to escape operators: \AND
    - Default expansion is EXPANSION[Relaxation]
    - Default coverage is COVERAGE[Contains]

Field Reference:
    High Priority Fields (weight >= 0.8):
        - NCTId (1.0): Trial identifier
        - Acronym (1.0): Study acronym
        - BriefTitle (0.89): Short title
        - OfficialTitle (0.85): Full official title
        - Condition (0.81): Medical condition
        - InterventionName (0.8): Primary intervention name
        - OverallStatus: Trial status

    Medium Priority Fields (0.5-0.79):

        - StdAge (0.65): Standard age groups

        - BriefSummary (0.6): Short description
        - SecondaryOutcomeMeasure (0.5): Secondary outcomes

    Low Priority Fields (< 0.5):
        - DesignPrimaryPurpose (0.3): Primary purpose of study
        - StudyType (0.3)
        - Various descriptive, location, and administrative fields

query.title: Query

info.name: patent_search
info.description:
Search for patents to increase the patent count.

Repeat previous calls with the same query to continue a search.
Only repeat a maximum of three times.
This tool can be called concurrently.
This tool introduces novel patents, so invoke it when just beginning
or when unsatisfied with the current evidence.

query.description:
A search query for patents (using Google Patents). Never include temporal
filters here (e.g. specific years or dates); instead use the `after` and `before`
arguments. Never include author filters here; instead use the `inventor` argument.
Supports the following syntax, but they can be brittle and are not preferred; try
simple queries where possible:
- Boolean operators: AND (default), OR, and - (NOT)
- Exact phrases: "quoted text"
- Proximity: NEAR/x, ADJ/x (ordered), WITH (20 words), SAME (200 words)
- Field search: TI=(title), AB=(abstract), CL=(claims)
- CPC codes: cpc:A01B or CPC=B60R22/low (includes children)
- Wildcards: ? (0-1 char), * (0+ chars), # (exactly 1 char)
- Chemistry: exact molecule names, SSS=molecule (substructure),
  ~molecule (similar), SMILES strings, SMARTS=pattern, InChI keys,
  CL=~molecule (chemistry in claims)
query.title: Query

after.description:
Minimum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20150101' for patents after Jan 1, 2015.
after.title: After

before.description:
Maximum patent date. Format: YYYYMMDD.
Example: '20200101' for patents before Jan 1, 2020.
before.title: Before

date_type.description:
Type of date that after and before refer to. Must be either 'publication'
or 'filing'. Only necessary to set if after and/or before are set.
date_type.title: Date Type

inventor.description: Filter by inventor name(s). Comma-separated for multiple.
inventor.title: Inventor

info.name: create_plan
info.description:
Create a plan for to construct a thorough and accurate answer.

Decompose the input task into a set of sub-objectives that need to
be met in order to form the answer. Subsequently, when working on solving
the query, you can focus on these objectives one by one. Note that the
`update_plan` tool allows you to change the objectives if needed.

In addition to the objectives you deem necessary, add one final objective
to call the `answer` tool with your final answer, so that you do not
forget to submit your answer.

plan_json.description:
A JSON string representing the plan. It should be a dictionary
with top-level key "objectives", whose value is a list of objectives.
Each objective should be a dictionary with the following keys:
- "id": (int) an integer id for the plan objective
- "objective": (str) a human readable description of the objective's goal
- "rationale": (str) a human readable rationale
    describing why this objective is necessary and appropriate for the query.
- "status": (str) updated task status, default to pending,
    one of: pending, in-progress, or completed.
    "pending" is yet to be started,
    "in-progress" is the current objective being solved,
    "completed" is successfully completed with acceptance criteria met.
- "result": (str|None) The result of the objective, default to None.
- "evaluation": (str|None) Description of why the objective result
    meets the criteria for being complete. Default to None.
plan_json.title: Plan Json

info.name: paper_search
info.description:
Search for papers in Google Scholar for you to look at.

Can be called repeatedly with identical parameters to retrieve more results (max 2 repeats).
A short summary of the retrieved papers will be returned, but to examine the full content of each paper,
you must use the gather_evidence tool.

query.description:
A Google Scholar search query, which can be a specific phrase, complete sentence,
or general keywords, e.g. 'machine learning for immunology'. Also can be
given search operators like 'author:'.
query.title: Query

min_year.description:
filter for minimum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
min_year.title: Min Year

max_year.description:
filter for maximum publication year.
The current year is 2026. If unsure about time range, do not set.
max_year.title: Max Year

info.name: answer
info.description:
Submit your final answer to the user. You MUST call this tool on your final answer.
If you mark all objectives as completed but have not yet called this tool, then
the answer will not be submitted. Note that this tool should only be called once, as it
immediately terminates the run and submits the answer.

Write an answer based on the evidence from gather_evidence results in message history.
If the context provides insufficient information, provide a partial answer and explain
why it is partial (e.g. the question is currently an open question in the field,
you were unable to obtain certain papers, etc.).

For each part of your answer, indicate which sources most support it via citation IDs at
the end of sentences, like (pqac-0000ABCD).
You are only allowed to cite available context IDs, indicated in previous messages by "Context IDs that may be cited:".
## Valid citation examples, only use comma/space delimited parentheticals:

- (pqac-0000ABCD, pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000IJKL)

## Invalid citation examples:

- (pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD;pqac-0000EFGH)
- (pqac-0000ABCD-pqac-0000EFGH)
- pqac-0000ABCD and pqac-0000EFGH
- Example's work pqac-0000ABCD
- (pages pqac-0000ABCD)
- ![description](pqac-0000ABCD)
- ()

Do not concatenate citation ids, just use them as-is.

When writing your answer, embed artifacts if relevant artifacts have been previously
created by the `create_artifact` tool. To embed an artifact, simply include this in your
answer, on its own line and without any additional formatting (i.e. no backticks, code fences,
quotes, additional tags, etc.).
Always provide at least some introductory sentence or paragraph before referring to an artifact,
so that the artifact is properly contextualized.
Here is an example reference to an artifact with ID "artifact-XY":
<artifact id="artifact-XY"/>

Valid artifact IDs are indicated in previous messages by 'Artifact IDs that may be injected into the answer:'.


Double-check your answer:

1. Evidence Match: Re-read the specific evidence snippets you're citing. Does your
   answer actually match what they say? Don't rely on memory from 20+ messages ago.

2. For Comparative Questions ("which X has greatest/maximum/highest Y"):
   - Did you examine ALL options mentioned in the evidence?
   - Did you explicitly compare their values?
   - Example: If evidence mentions "Pancreatic: 0.92, Liver: 0.85, Lung: 0.78",
     verify you compared all three, not just two.

3. For Specific Value Questions ("How many", "What is the", specific terms):
   - Does the exact value you're stating appear in the evidence?
   - Are you extracting from the right context (not noise like page numbers)?

4. Sanity Check: Read your answer. Does it directly address what was asked?

Don't use Markdown image syntax like ![...](...) to reference media in your answer,
because media get automatically linked by the system based on your answer's citations.

Write in the style of a scientific article, with concise sentences and
coherent paragraphs. This answer will be used directly,
so do not add any extraneous information.

answer.description: valid answer, cited with citation IDs.
answer.title: Answer

messages.content:
Use the tools to answer the question: Provide an in-depth review of code-based, programmatic, and 'design-as-code' approaches to electronics and PCB design that can be version-controlled and driven from a CI pipeline (analogous to code-CAD such as CadQuery/OpenSCAD for mechanical design). Cover tools and frameworks including atopile (the .ato language), tscircuit (React-for-PCB), SKiDL, PCBmodE, gEDA/gaf, Horizon EDA, Edea, KiCad's Python / IPC scripting and kicad-cli, JITX (Stanza-based programmatic PCB), and any LLM/text-to-PCB or text-to-schematic research prototypes. For each, describe the programming model, what is generated automatically vs. specified by the designer, support for parametrization and reuse, manufacturability/DRC integration, rendering/export (Gerber, BOM, netlist), maturity, and licensing. Discuss the trade-offs versus GUI-based EDA and versus AI/generative EDA, and which approaches best fit an open-hardware, reproducible, CI-rendered workflow for a modular multi-powder dosing device. Cite project pages/repos and ~5-8 recent (2020-2025) papers or technical sources.

Start by making a plan, and update the plan as you go. Your answer will be returned to the user when all plan objectives are completed. Be thorough and ensure your evidence matches the user's question.

messages.content:
Retrieved Papers:
## 1. ORFS-agent: Tool-Using Agents for Chip Design Optimization
Valid Text Names:
'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 1-2', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 2-3', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 3-4', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 4-5', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 4-4', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 5-6', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 6-7', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 7-8', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 8-9', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 9-10', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 10-11', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 11-12', 'ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents pages 12-13'

BibTex:
@article{ghose2025orfsagenttoolusingagents,
    author = "Ghose, Amur and Kahng, Andrew B. and Kundu, Sayak and Wang, Zhiang",
    title = "ORFS-agent: Tool-Using Agents for Chip Design Optimization",
    year = "2025",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2506.08332",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2506.08332",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2506.08332"
}


Abstract:
Machine learning has been widely used to optimize complex engineering workflows across numerous domains. In the context of integrated circuit design, modern flows (e.g., going from a register-transfer level netlist to physical layouts) involve extensive configuration via thousands of parameters, and small changes to these parameters can have large downstream impacts on desired outcomes - namely design performance, power, and area. Recent advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) offer new opportunities for learning and reasoning within such high-dimensional optimization tasks. In this work, we introduce ORFS-agent, an LLM-based iterative optimization agent that automates parameter tuning in an open-source hardware design flow. ORFS-agent adaptively explores parameter configurations, demonstrating clear improvements over standard Bayesian optimization approaches in terms of resource efficiency and final design metrics. Our empirical evaluations on two different technology nodes and a range of circuit benchmarks indicate that ORFS-agent can improve both routed wirelength and effective clock period by over 13%, all while using 40% fewer optimization iterations. Moreover, by following natural language objectives to trade off certain metrics for others, ORFS-agent demonstrates a flexible and interpretable framework for multi-objective optimization. Crucially, RFS-agent is modular and model-agnostic, and can be plugged in to any frontier LLM without any further fine-tuning.

Relevant Snippet:
… In the context of integrated circuit design, modern flows (eg, … clear improvements over standard Bayesian optimization … turns high-level hardware descriptions into manufacturable layouts…

## 1. Python Framework for Modular and Parametric SPICE Netlists Generation
Valid Text Names:
'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 1-2', 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3', 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 3-4', 'gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 4-4'

BibTex:
@article{gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor,
    author = "Gutiérrez, Sergio Vinagrero and Natale, Giorgio Di and Vatajelu, Elena-Ioana",
    title = "Python Framework for Modular and Parametric SPICE Netlists Generation",
    year = "2023",
    journal = "ArXiv",
    volume = "abs/2306.12224",
    month = "Jun",
    doi = "10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2306.12224",
    publisher = "arXiv"
}


Abstract:
Due to the complex specifications of current electronic systems, design decisions need to be explored automatically. However, the exploration process is a complex task given the plethora of design choices such as the selection of components, number of components, operating modes of each of the components, connections between the components and variety of ways in which the same functionality can be implemented. To tackle these issues, scripts are used to generate designs based on high-level abstract constructions. Still, this approach is usually ad hoc and platform dependent, making the whole procedure hardly reusable, scalable and versatile. We propose a generic, open-source framework tackling rapid design exploration for the generation of modular and parametric electronic designs that is able to work on any major simulator.

}


Abstract:
Graphical PCB design tools like KiCAD lack support for high-level abstraction such as functions and loops. To improve PCB design productivity, we hereby present BHDL, a programming framework for PCB designs. In its compact and declarative syntax, schematics and layouts can be modeled effectively and expressed concisely. Treating all circuits, even a resistor, as functions, BHDL naturally supports modularized development that builds a complex design up from smaller designs hierarchically. As an embedded Domain Specific Language (eDSL), BHDL allows users to leverage the full feature of the host language for customization and extension. Our Jupyter kernel supports web-based, REPL-style development and generates auto-placed PCBs.

## 2. HardOps: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design
Valid Text Names:

    title = "HardOps: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design",
    year = "2022",
    journal = "International Journal of Computer Integrated Manufacturing",
    volume = "35",
    pages = "1297-1309",
    month = "Feb",
    doi = "10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188",
