    publisher = "Informa UK Limited",
    issue = "12",
    issn = "0951-192X"
}


Abstract:
ABSTRACT Open collaborative design of physical products between remote partners poses unique challenges. This is due to both the complex and interconnected data required for product design and manufacture, and to the centralised computing infrastructure traditionally used to manage product lifecycle data. While modern cloud-based solutions to collaborative design are gaining popularity, they diminish the control of each design partner. In contrast, software designers readily collaborate on highly complex software, while retaining direct control of the files they are editing due to the dominance of distributed version control. Distributed version control for software is often coupled with ‘Developer Operations’ or DevOps tools to automate critical processes and facilitate communication. This paper explores how DevOps workflows can be adapted for open development of hardware. An example of how DevOps for hardware (HardOps) can be implemented is provided by the OpenFlexure Microscope project. While much ground remains to be broken in this field, HardOps can support a new paradigm of distributed hardware development, with enormous benefits not only for open-source hardware, but also commercial hardware.

## 2. Repurposing a digital kitchen scale for neuroscience research: a complete hardware and software cookbook for PASTA
Valid Text Names:
'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 1-2', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 2-4', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 4-6', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 6-7', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 7-8', 'virag2021repurposingadigital pages 8-8'

    author = "Virag, D. and Homolak, J. and Kodvanj, Ivan and Perhoč, A. Babić and Knezović, A. and Barilar, J. Osmanović and Šalković-Petrišić, M.",
    title = "Repurposing a digital kitchen scale for neuroscience research: a complete hardware and software cookbook for PASTA",
    year = "2021",
    journal = "Scientific Reports",
    volume = "11",
    month = "Apr",
    doi = "10.1038/s41598-021-82710-6",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1038/s41598-021-82710-6",
    publisher = "Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory"
}


Abstract:
Widely available low-cost electronics encourage the development of open-source tools for neuroscientific research. In recent years, many neuroscientists recognized the open science movement for its potential to stimulate and encourage science that is less focused on money, and more on robustness, validity, questioning and understanding. Here, we wanted to contribute to this global community by creating a research platform based on a common digital kitchen scale. This everyday ordinary kitchen tool is sometimes used in neuroscience research in various ways; however, its use is limited by sampling rate and inability to store and analyze data. To tackle this problem we developed a Platform for Acoustic STArtle or PASTA. This robust and simple platform enables users to obtain data from kitchen scale load cells at a high sampling rate, store it and analyze it. Here, we used it to analyze acoustic startle and prepulse inhibition sensorimotor gating in rats treated intracerebroventricularly with streptozotocin, but the system can be easily modified and upgraded for other purposes. In accordance with open science principles, we shared complete hardware design with instructions. Furthermore, we also disclose our software codes written for PASTA data acquisition (C++, Arduino) and acoustic startle experimental protocol (Python) and analysis (ratPASTA R package—R-based Awesome Toolbox for PASTA, and pastaWRAP—Python wrapper package for ratPASTA). To further encourage the development of our PASTA platform we demonstrate its sensitivity by using PASTA-gathered data to extract breathing patterns during rat freezing behavior in our experimental protocol.

## 3. Iterative printing of bulk metal and polymer for additive manufacturing of multi-layer electronic circuits
Valid Text Names:
'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 1-2', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 2-3', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 3-4', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 4-6', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 6-7', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 7-8', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 8-10', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 10-12', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 12-13', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 13-14', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 14-15', 'khan2024iterativeprintingof pages 15-15'

BibTex:
@article{khan2024iterativeprintingof,
    author = "Khan, Zeba and Gururajan, Dheepesh and Kartmann, Sabrina and Koltay, Peter and Zengerle, Roland and Shu, Zhe",
    title = "Iterative printing of bulk metal and polymer for additive manufacturing of multi-layer electronic circuits",
    year = "2024",
    journal = "npj Advanced Manufacturing",
    volume = "1",
    month = "Aug",
    doi = "10.1038/s44334-024-00001-0",
    url = "https://doi.org/10.1038/s44334-024-00001-0",
    publisher = "Springer Science and Business Media LLC",
    issue = "1",
    issn = "3004-8621"
}


Abstract:
In pursuing advancing additive manufacturing (AM) techniques for 3D objects, this study combines AM techniques for bulk metal and polymer on a single platform for one-stop printing of multilayer 3D electronic circuits with two novel aspects. The first innovation involves the embedded integration of electronic circuits by printing low-resistance electrical traces from bulk metal into polymer channels. Cross-section grinding results reveal (92 ± 5)% occupancy of electrically conductive traces in polymer channels despite the different thermal properties of the two materials. The second aspect encompasses the possibility of printing vertical bulk metal vias up to 10 mm in height with the potential for expansion, interconnecting electrically conductive traces embedded in different layers of the 3D object. The work provides comprehensive 3D printing design guidelines for successfully integrating fully embedded electrically conductive traces and the interconnecting vertical bulk metal vias. A smooth and continuous workflow is also introduced, enabling a single-run print of functional multilayer embedded 3D electronics. The design rules and the workflow facilitate the iterative printing of two distinct materials, each defined by unique printing temperatures and techniques. Observations indicate that conductive traces using molten metal microdroplets show a 12-fold reduction in resistance compared to nanoparticle ink-based methods, meaning this technique greatly complements multi-material additive manufacturing (MM-AM). The work presents insights into the behavior of molten metal microdroplets on a polymer substrate when printed through the MM-AM process. It explores their characteristics in two scenarios: When they are deposited side-by-side to form conductive traces and when they are deposited out-of-plane to create vertical bulk metal vias. The innovative application of MM-AM to produce multilayer embedded 3D electronics with bulk metal and polymer demonstrates significant potential for realizing the fabrication of free-form 3D electronics.

Abstract:
The convergence of the Internet of Things (IoT) and the construction industry represents a potent opportunity to fundamentally redefine the processes of constructing and maintaining buildings. The integration of IoT technology has the potential to revolutionize building safety, efficiency, and sustainability, offering a promising avenue for innovation and advancement within the industry. In this work, we propose an innovative design and implementation of an interoperable physical access control IoT-based system that can be easily integrated with existing access control security systems and used in various environments such as offices, homes, and public buildings. Based on our developed and implemented physical access control IoT-based system, we identified several challenges in the current state of access control systems. These challenges include difficulties in integrating different security systems, the absence of interoperable standards, and the need for further research to enhance the scalability and performance of such systems.

Abstract:
Large-scale superconducting quantum computers require the high-fidelity control and readout of large numbers of qubits at millikelvin temperatures, resulting in a massive input–output bottleneck. Cryo-electronics based on complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor technology could provide a scalable and versatile solution. However, detrimental effects due to cross-coupling between the qubits and the electronic and thermal noise generated during cryo-electronics operation need to be avoided. Here we report a low-power radio-frequency multiplexing cryo-electronics system that operates below 15 mK with a minimal cross-coupling. We benchmark its performance by interfacing the system with a superconducting qubit and observe that the qubit’s relaxation times are unaffected, whereas the coherence times are marginally affected in both static and dynamic operations. Using the multiplexer, single-qubit gate fidelities above 99.9%—that is, above the threshold for surface-code-based quantum error correction—can be achieved with appropriate thermal filtering. We also demonstrate time-division multiplexing capabilities by dynamically windowing calibrated qubit control pulses. A low-power radio-frequency multiplexing cryo-electronics system, which is based on complementary metal–oxide–semiconductor technology, can operate below 15 mK and provide the control and interfacing of superconducting qubits with minimal cross-coupling.

Abstract:
Impedance analysis is a powerful technique that has become increasingly important in various applications, it represents a leap forward in the field of electronic measurments and diagnostics. In this work, we present the development of miniaturized, multiplexed, and connected platform for impedance spectroscopy. Designed for online measurements and adapted to wireless network architectures, our platform has been tested and optimized to be used for multi-selective chemical organic sensor nodes. This compact and versatile circuit is built from low cost and low power consumption (250 mW) microelectronics components that achieve long duration operability (5 days and 16 h) without compromising on sensor measurement accuracy and precision. We used the well-known impedance network analyzer AD5933 (Analog Devices, Norwood, MA, USA) chip which can measure a spectrum of impedances in the range 5 kHz to 100 kHz. The proposed system is based on ESP32-C3 Microcontroller enabling the management of the AD5933 through its I2C interface. Our system benefits from two multiplexer components CD74HC4067 allowing calibration process and the interface of 15 conductimetric sensors with real time acquisition (less than 90 ms per acquisition). The system is capable of relaying information through the network for data analysis and storage. The paper describes the microelectronics design, the impedance response over time, the measurement’s sensitivity and accuracy and the testing of the platform with embedded chemical sensors for gas classification and recognition.

Abstract:
Mainstream board-level circuit design tools work at the lowest level of design --- schematics and individual components. While novel tools experiment with higher levels of design, abstraction often comes at the expense of the fine-grained control afforded by low-level tools. In this work, we propose a hardware description language (HDL) approach that supports users at multiple levels of abstraction from broad system architecture to subcircuits and component selection. We extend the familiar hierarchical block diagram with polymorphism to include abstract-typed blocks (e.g., generic resistor supertype) and electronics modeling (i.e., currents and voltages). Such an approach brings the advantages of reusability and encapsulation from object-oriented programming, while addressing the unique needs of electronics designers such as physical correctness verification. We discuss the system design, including fundamental abstractions, the block diagram construction HDL, and user interfaces to inspect and fine-tune the design; demonstrate example designs built with our system; and present feedback from intermediate-level engineers who have worked with our system.

Abstract:
This paper describes the history of the Verilog hardware description language (HDL), including its influential predecessors and successors. Since its creation in 1984 and first sale in 1985, Verilog has completely revolutionized the design of hardware. Verilog enabled the development and wide acceptance of logic synthesis. For large-scale digital logic design, previous schematic-based techniques have transformed into textual register-transfer level (RTL) descriptions written in Verilog. As of 2018 about 80% of integrated circuit design teams worldwide use Verilog and its compatible descendant SystemVerilog.

## pqac-00000000

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt frames board-level HDLs and programmatic PCB tools as a space that benefits from programming-language ideas such as generator languages and type systems to automate design and encapsulate low-level knowledge in reusable libraries. It highlights a key practical challenge: confidence in code-generated hardware — unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly — so mechanisms to establish trust in community-shared generators and subcircuits are important (for example, labeling subcircuits as “physically fabricated and tested”). The authors note that simulation and static modeling alone are insufficient for modern practice, implying that tooling and processes (community verification, provenance, testing artifacts) are needed alongside programming models. Finally, the field is nascent with many open questions across models, abstractions, and user interfaces, and will benefit from cross-disciplinary work across electrical engineering, programming languages, and HCI. This context informs how tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, and PCBmodE might be compared by their programming models, reuse mechanisms, and approaches to verification and trust.

## pqac-00000003

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 27-30'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt situates programmatic PCB/design-as-code tools within the broader HDL/HCL landscape. Chip HDLs (e.g., Verilog, VHDL) combine structural and behavioral descriptions, and analog/mixed-signal variants (Verilog-AMS, VHDL-AMS) exist but are not full design/synthesis languages. Hardware construction languages (HCLs) extend HDLs by providing generator constructs to produce families of modules from high-level parameters. For PCB work, traditional PCB HDLs tend to be purely structural; however, recent tools such as JITPCB and SKiDL introduce generator-style programming by embedding circuit-construction primitives in general-purpose languages, enabling programmatic, parameterized board creation. The dissertation referenced builds on these ideas and adds richer electronics modeling to support parts selection and correctness checks. The excerpt also highlights live programming concepts — continuously running code and low-latency feedback — as important for interactive textual design workflows. The excerpt does not explicitly describe BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, or PCBmodE, so details on those specific tools are absent.

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt frames board-level HDLs as a programmatic, higher-abstraction approach to PCB/electronics design. It contrasts simple Verilog-like translations with a Chisel-like generator model where code describes construction and sizing methodology (e.g., automatically sizing resistors/values per application). Object-oriented concepts — type hierarchies, inheritance, and parameterized types (voltage, current ratings) — are proposed to let designers instantiate abstract components and either choose or automatically select compatible concrete implementations, and to enable correctness checks. Polymorphic Blocks is given as a concrete, Python-embedded DSL and library-based flow that demonstrates these ideas: top-level blocks are declared in code (example class Blinky(Block)), sub-blocks are instantiated (self.Block(MagicMcu()), self.Block(IndicatorLed())), and connections are made programmatically (self.connect(...)). The paper emphasizes that generators and typing encapsulate specialized knowledge, increase automation, and raise design efficiency and accessibility. This provides a conceptual model relevant to programmatic tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, and others even though those specific tools are not named in the excerpt.

## pqac-00000005

From text 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2'.

Hebi Li, Youbiao He, Qi Xiao, Jin Tian, and Forrest Sheng Bao. Bhdl: a lucid, expressive, and embedded programming language and system for pcb designs. 2021 58th ACM/IEEE Design Automation Conference (DAC), pages 355-360, Dec 2021. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086, doi:10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086. This article has 1 citations.

BHDL (Board HDL) is presented as a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive embedded DSL implemented in Racket. It models every circuit element, including discrete parts, as functions to enable hierarchical, modular composition of larger designs from smaller components. BHDL offers concise connection syntax (net) and higher-level connection forms — series, parallel, and bus — to express wiring succinctly. For layout co-design, BHDL supplies relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and adapts VLSI auto-placers to place free parts. The system supports an interactive REPL-style development model and is provided with a Jupyter notebook environment/kernal for incremental coding and visualization. The toolchain generates placed PCB boards which can be exported and routed via external tools (e.g., FreeRouting) to produce manufacturable PCBs. As an eDSL, BHDL also allows leveraging full host-language features (functions, loops, libraries) for programmability, parameterization, and extension of PCB designs.

## pqac-00000006

From text 'li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6'.

Hebi Li, Youbiao He, Qi Xiao, Jin Tian, and Forrest Sheng Bao. URL: https://doi.org/10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086, doi:10.1109/dac18074.2021.9586086. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes BHDL as a declarative, modular HDL and system for PCB design that integrates footprint management, layout generation, and interactive development. Footprints can be imported (including from Internet URLs) and renamed; BHDL currently supports the KiCAD-format footprint library and allows multiple footprints per part with defaults. The language provides extended pin syntax, placeholders for unused pins, and prefixing for part naming. For layout generation, BHDL includes an auto-placer: an analytical global placer based on RePlace (optimizing HPWL) and a simulated-annealing detailed placer that adds rotation capability by "introduc[ing] a third dimension to rotate the components by random angles" to resolve dense placements. Implementation-wise, the system is implemented in Racket with the auto-placer in Julia, follows a REPL-driven incremental development model, and uses Jupyter notebooks as the preferred development environment for interactive editing, visualization, and exporting to KiCAD PCB format, Gerber, and BOM. The system also supports programmatic hierarchical co-design and aims to enable systematic, code-based PCB development.

## pqac-00000007

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 48-50'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes a hardware construction language (HCL) approach called Polymorphic Circuit Blocks that targets system-architecture-level design using block diagrams. Key features include support for high-level blocks defined as reusable subcircuit libraries, generators (executable code) that translate high-level specifications into implementations (e.g., sizing components from parameters), and explicit support for ambiguity and polymorphism (generic parts and subtype substitution). This block-based model separates concerns: system designers work at the architecture level while experts create reusable generator libraries. Designs written in HCL are elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph model, expanded via community libraries, and can be interactively refined through a GUI to reach a layout-ready circuit. The resulting design can be exported as a netlist for import into PCB layout tools. The authors also envision an open-source community around such libraries and generators to lower barriers and enable reuse and exploration.

## pqac-00000010

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes a hardware description language (HCL) that models circuit blocks with a class hierarchy enabling abstract, polymorphic blocks and delayed refinement. Ports and links carry electrical parameters (e.g., source voltage/current limits, sink current draw, digital thresholds, analog impedances) and links aggregate these parameters and define assertions to check compatibility. An electronics layer defines common link/port types (power, digital, analog, multi-wire bundles) and a library of primitives and higher-level blocks (e.g., resistor generator using E24, decoupling capacitors, RC filter that computes R/C from cutoff and impedance). The system supports programmatic block construction via a generator HCL interface, translating high-level inputs into internal parameters to produce families of subcircuits. Example code (class Blinky(Block)) shows block instantiation and explicit connections between sub-block ports. The design aims to let novices compose system-level designs from libraries while experts build the reusable blocks and port/link models. Parameter propagation and automated checks are used to catch compatibility issues when refining abstract blocks.

The excerpt describes BHDL's connection primitives and layout facilities relevant to modular PCB design. Circuits can be called as functions and their arguments must match the pin clause, enabling modular circuit functions and signal wiring at invocation. BHDL offers multiple wire-connection syntaxes: net (list of pins forming a netlist), series (connect left/right pins of listed parts in sequence), parallel (connect components in parallel), and bus (connect corresponding pins across entities). The bus syntax supports variants and uses underscores as placeholders for missing pins; examples show use for bridging Arduino form factors. Nesting and mixing of series/parallel/net allow concise hierarchical connectivity. BHDL also supports in-place anonymous part creation (termed “lambda circuits”), avoiding the need to predefine components. For layout generation, BHDL provides relative, hierarchical layout combinators inspired by JITPCB and Pict: hstack, vstack, rotate, and at, enabling sub-circuits to be laid out and then composed into larger layouts with specified relative positions rather than absolute coordinates. The excerpt does not mention a Jupyter kernel integration.

The excerpt describes BHDL's layout and embedding features relevant to modular PCB design and layout generation. BHDL provides layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) with alignment and spacing options for hierarchical, functional composition of layouts; an example stacks three buttons vertically next to an ESP-01. The language supports concise in-place part creation combined with series/parallel/bus syntax, enabling reuse-free, template-style construction of repeated structures. BHDL circuits are first-class values in the host language: they can be bound to variables, passed to functions, and used in expressions. Host-language constructs (functions, conditionals, loops) can be used inside BHDL clauses (part, wire, layout), demonstrated by a parameterized TripleButton example that generates two versions via a mode parameter and a for-loop to create repeated parts. For physical mapping, BHDL includes a footprint clause and supports importing KiCad footprint files, e.g., for resistors. Placement control features include a padding parameter to control component density and a simulated-annealing detailed placer for placement refinement. The excerpt does not mention a Jupyter kernel.

## pqac-00000013

From text 'stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9'.

Julian Stirling, Kaspar Bumke, Joel Collins, Vimal Dhokia, and Richard Bowman. Hardops: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design. Feb 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188, doi:10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188. This article has 17 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes applying DevOps/DVCS and CI/CD practices to open-source hardware (HardOps). Key DVCS benefits include cryptographically signed commits, distributed branches, and merge requests with unique IDs, threaded discussion and issue links to control protected branches. A major challenge is CAD files: many are complex binary formats making diffs and merges difficult; even text-based CAD (e.g. KiCad.kicad_pcb) often needs specialist tools. Mitigations include centralised file locking, Git extensions, and external diff/merge tools, but the preferred route is CAD vendors providing comparison/merge functionality. OpenFlexure leverages OpenSCAD (CAD-as-code) enabling parametric designs and programmatic testing: unit tests, static analysis, and CI jobs generate STLs, renderings, documentation, and BOMs reproducibly. CI pipelines on DevOps platforms run automated quality checks, build artifacts, deploy websites, and can execute on external infrastructure for resource-intensive tasks or licensed software. Scheduled jobs and bots help manage issues and keep assets in sync, reducing manual overhead and ensuring a single source of truth for production files.


---

## pqac-00000014

From text 'stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 12-13'.

Julian Stirling, Kaspar Bumke, Joel Collins, Vimal Dhokia, and Richard Bowman. Hardops: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design. Feb 2022. URL: https://doi.org/10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188, doi:10.1080/0951192x.2022.2028188. This article has 17 citations and is from a domain leading peer-reviewed journal.

The excerpt describes HardOps as a decentralised alternative for hardware design using distributed version control (DVCS) and DevOps platforms. Users keep full local copies of designs and history, synchronise via SSH, and have cryptographically hashed records for traceability. Collaboration works by fetching and selectively merging changes; merge requests can be gated by review/sign-off. HardOps supports automation pipelines (e.g., clash analysis) but these rely on tools providing command-line interfaces so they can be scripted. Many CAD packages lack robust CLI and proprietary licensing can complicate automation (sequential jobs or multiple licenses may be required). The authors note that most CAD functionality can be automated via APIs and that command-line wrappers could be written, but significant work is needed to generalise HardOps beyond OpenSCAD and to scale from prototyping to production. They also emphasise that production-related structured data (quality control, production records) may be better handled in databases linked to the DVCS-managed design data, and that project-management federation between servers remains an open challenge.


---

## pqac-00000021

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a workflow for LLM-driven schematic generation that is guided by a domain knowledge graph (KG) encoding pin-role semantics and electrical constraints. Components in kg component.json receive normalized pin roles derived from automated datasheet extraction and expert validation (example: MGJ2D pin mappings). The KG defines four formal constraint types (e.g., supply pair, must be connected, driving pair, differential pair) used during fast-fail verification to catch topology and net-assignment errors. Data sources include aggregated manufacturer datasheets parsed by scripts; semantic normalization maps vendor-specific pin labels into a unified ontology of 36 pin-role semantics. Role-based verification enables generic checks (e.g., VDD and GND must be on distinct nets). The system supports multi-level feedback (Full/Weak/None) for iterative correction; Full, with pin-level localization, significantly improves pass rates. A custom KiCad component library (symbols, footprints, datasheets) is provided for SKiDL integration, enabling standardized component defs and accurate pin mappings for synthesis and downstream PCB workflows.

## pqac-00000022

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen targets text-to-PCB (text-to-schematic) by using SKiDL as the executable intermediate: SKiDL’s Python basis helps LLMs map functional requirements to package info and pin constraints. The authors build a domain-specific Knowledge Graph (KG) from IC datasheets to encode component types, pin-role semantics (R includes 36 pin role types), electrical properties, topological constraints and isolation boundaries; they formally define KG as KG = (C, R, A, Φ, I). The KG dramatically reduces context needs by compressing datasheet content — stated as a reduction from ~16k tokens per IC to ~300 tokens (30–70x less). Prompt engineering techniques include in-context learning (ICL) with multiple design examples and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to guide stepwise reasoning about connectivity and pin roles. Generation is followed by a multi-stage verification pipeline (Figure 2): Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in Electrical Rule Checking (ERC), and topology verification. The verification algorithm builds bipartite and topology graphs (BuildTopologyGraph(σgen, KG)), trims to critical subgraphs, and performs inter- and intra-connection checks to catch violations (e.g., VCC–GND shorts), combining verification KG and design rules.


---

## pqac-00000023

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen proposes a training-free LLM-driven pipeline for PCB schematic generation that uses an LLM as a SKiDL code proposal generator with domain-specific prompting, Chain-of-Thought, and In-Context Learning to synthesize executable design code without fine-tuning. To enforce physical correctness, the system builds an IC-domain knowledge graph (KG) from real-world datasheets capturing pin-role semantics, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints, and couples this KG with SI-driven multi-phase verification to produce interpretable error messages and iterative feedback. The authors curated a benchmark of 23 real-world circuits across digital, analog, and power domains to evaluate PCB generative ability and demonstrated end-to-end capability from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB layout files accounting for real-world IC packages. The paper highlights challenges specific to PCB schematic generation — vendor-specific schematic capture, scarce board-level datasets, and NP-hard multi-component/ routing/placement decisions — and positions PCBSchemaGen as addressing gaps left by prior work that focused mainly on IC-level digital/analog generation and lacked modeling of board-level packaging and pin constraints.

## pqac-00000024

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen frames text-to-PCB schematic generation as constraint-guided synthesis with multi-stage verification. Circuits are represented as bipartite graphs (components and nets), and topology verification is cast as a subgraph isomorphism problem; the paper uses the VF2 algorithm for efficient checking. Verification combines knowledge-graph (KG) information and design rules to detect pin/net and ERC violations, and the pipeline emits interpretable, localized error messages that are fed back to the LLM. The system supports three feedback message styles (concise, binary, and detailed with syntax/ERC/subgraph errors and precise pin/net/passive value info). It also implements subcircuit reuse via a library of validated modules to improve hierarchical/complex designs; ablation studies show retrieval helps success rates. The authors built a benchmark of 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks and a component library based on IC datasheets, intentionally including similar-package components to test LLM discrimination. Experiments evaluate various LLMs using prompt strategies (ICL and CoT) alongside the multi-stage verification, feedback loop, and subcircuit reuse, reporting main results and human expert validation.

## pqac-00000025

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-based pipeline that translates natural-language PCB schematic specifications into SKiDL code and ultimately KiCAD schematics/layouts. The system favors SKiDL because Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning. A careful prompt-design methodology is used to improve comprehension, connectivity awareness, and adaptability. To inject IC-level physical constraints without flooding the model, PCBSchemaGen encodes essential device rules from datasheets into a compact Knowledge Graph (KG), shrinking per-device token usage from ~16k to ~300 tokens. Generation accuracy is enforced via a multi-stage verification framework combining the KG, electrical design rules, and Subgraph Isomorphism topology checks; verification feedback is returned to the LLM for iterative refinement. The design also includes semantic pin-role labeling (mapping pins to one of 36 roles) and reuse of validated subcircuits. The pipeline steps: user NL input -> agent reads KG and datasheet-derived rules -> agent generates SKiDL -> KiCAD schematic and layout generation.

## pqac-00000026

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-based, constraint-guided framework for generating PCB schematics. The paper reports substantially higher success rates than prior baselines (a GNN and an earlier LLM) when aligned by component/pin counts. Table 3 breaks performance down by task difficulty: Easy (<10 pins) shows 93.3% for PCBSchemaGen versus 53.0% and 80% for baselines; Medium (10–50 pins) 92.7%; Hard (>100 pins) 78.1%. The authors emphasize that tasks require device-level knowledge retrieval/integration, strong abstraction over circuit connectivity, spatial reasoning, and circuit-level conceptualization. Error analysis highlights two dominant failure modes: (i) misunderstanding specific pin functions, and (ii) generating meaningless connections from an inability to synthesize abstract circuit topology. The paper uses representative tasks (e.g., a differential-to-single-end op-amp buffer and a SiC MOSFET half-bridge) to illustrate successes and failures. Among models evaluated, Gemini 3 is noted as the best performer, possibly due to stronger multimodal capabilities aiding topology understanding. The work positions PCBSchemaGen as advancing text-to-PCB schematic synthesis with constraint guidance and concrete performance gains over prior approaches.

## pqac-00000027

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen evaluates constraint-guided, LLM-based schematic generation for PCB design. The authors test a wide set of LLMs (DeepSeek-V3.2, Llama 3/4, MiniMax M2.1, Qwen 3 Max, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 series) with temperature 0.5 and include a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini ablation. The evaluation targets pin-function understanding, knowledge-graph and topology constraints, topology generation, and mixed analog/digital/power synthesis across a 23-task benchmark (Table 1; e.g., QFN-8×8 integrated half-bridge). The framework uses strategies from Section 3: prompt engineering (ICL, CoT), multi-stage verification and feedback, and subcircuit library reuse. Metrics use Pass@k (k=1,5) with n=15 trials per task. Results (summarized in Table 2) show state-of-the-art open-source models can match closed models on some easier/medium tasks, but generally underperform on complex designs (large networks >20 devices, >70 pins). PCBSchemaGen is time- and cost-efficient: best model consumes ≈23k tokens per task (~$0.07) and runs in ≈2.4 minutes versus ~1.5 hours for a human (~37× speedup).

## pqac-00000028

From text 'zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2'.

Huanghaohe Zou, Peng Han, Emad Nazerian, and Alex Q. Huang. Pcbschemagen: constraint-guided schematic design via llm for printed circuit boards (pcb). ArXiv, Jan 2602. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2602.00510. This article has 1 citations.

PCBSchemaGen is a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to generate PCB schematics by producing SKiDL-style Python code. The system emphasizes a decoupled architecture: an LLM-based proposal generator (with domain-specific prompting, CoT and ICL techniques) for SKiDL code, plus a verification pipeline built from a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) based verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological constraints. PCBSchemaGen targets the specific challenges of PCB schematic design — heterogeneous digital, analog, and power domains, real IC packages and pin constraints, and the scarcity of open schematic data — which make purely simulation-based verification (e.g., SPICE) insufficient. The paper reports experiments across 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains and claims improved design accuracy and computational efficiency. The authors highlight interpretability and reproducibility through rule-based verification and open-source their code, KG, and benchmark on GitHub.

## pqac-00000029

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench evaluates LLMs on board-level schematic design using a generative pipeline that mimics human engineering by decomposing tasks (e.g., module partitioning) and a verification engine with two strategies: static rule checking and dynamic transient (SPICE) simulation. Static checks abstract netlists into a topology graph and use pin property matching and protocol consistency algorithms to find connectivity faults, while dynamic verification compiles generated netlists into SPICE scripts to drive LTspice and performs electrical metric monitoring; "the probe voltage or current at each test point must precisely fall within the preset logic threshold window." The benchmark enforces preprocessing of datasheets into structured JSON. Evaluation uses three dimensions—static checks, dynamic simulations, and global correctness—and standardized sampling (T=0.1, top-p=0.95). Models tested include Claude Sonnet 4.5, DeepSeek-v3, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-max, and Grok-4. Static pass rates are relatively high (avg 71.84%; Claude 77.73%; Industrial 83.05%, Automotive 77.03%), dynamic pass rates lower (Claude 67.92%; Qwen3-Max 60.21%; Gemini 60.00%), and global full-design correctness is very low (Claude 8.15%; GPT-5.2 6.58%; others 4–5%), highlighting LLMs' physical-intuition gaps and hallucinations on ambiguous/multiplexed pins.


---

## pqac-00000030

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is a benchmark that evaluates large language models on board-level schematic design across static rule checking and dynamic (SPICE-like) behavior simulation. The paper reports considerably different results between static and dynamic evaluations: average static rule pass rates are around 71.84% while dynamic simulation pass rates drop to about 58.65%, and the overall correct-design success rates reported in TABLE V are very low (single-digit percentages for tested models). Key failure modes include context overload when handling very large, homogeneous component lists (many pins), entity role confusion and label conflicts across modules, and pin-multiplexing errors where LLMs assign mutually exclusive functions to the same MCU pin. The authors note models often produce functionally equivalent connections sufficient for simulation, but struggle with rigid physical constraints and global logical consistency. To mitigate these issues, HWE-Bench applies prompt-engineering interventions such as internal signal-chain inference and structured signal-chain or functional-group constraints to reduce component disorder and resolve pin-multiplexing conflicts. The benchmark highlights that circuit design non-uniqueness and strict physical rules remain major challenges for LLM-based EDA agents.


---

## pqac-00000031

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is an evaluation framework for LLMs on board-level schematic design that includes 300 real-world design tasks across 8 application domains and 2,914 IC datasheets. The benchmark emphasizes strict physical constraints and applies a novel functional group-based rule verification method for static testing. It builds a design constraint model that partitions MCU physical resources into functional bus groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) and a GPIO resource pool based on electrical attributes, and uses preset interface protocol contracts to enforce correct connectivity. A verification system introduces a pin-allocation simulation (‘pin locking’ / ‘pin-locking’ mechanism) that marks pins as ‘locked’ when a netlist connection passes attribute checks and emits LOCK CONFLICT warnings for reuse attempts, preventing resource contention and logical short circuits. HWE-Bench also integrates dynamic validation via SPICE simulation. Evaluation with this framework revealed critical limitations in current models’ physical reasoning, especially in MCU pin multiplexing scenarios. Future work aims to guide LLMs toward reliable reasoning and generation of practically deployable circuit designs.


---

## pqac-00000032

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is an execution-driven benchmark for evaluating large language models on board-level schematic design. The benchmark contains 300 real board-level tasks across 8 application domains sourced from GitHub and OSHWLab and is paired with a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets. For each task, an LLM must generate a schematic from functional requirements and provided datasheets; outputs undergo static electrical rule checking and dynamic verification via an industry-standard LTspice simulator. The framework emulates engineering workflows by using structured prompts that guide module decomposition, component allocation, and netlist generation. HWE-Bench deliberately avoids text-similarity scoring and instead verifies functional correctness through simulation and rule-based checks. The authors evaluated multiple state-of-the-art LLMs (DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4) and report that models show some documentation understanding but generally lack physical intuition, with the top-performing model achieving an overall pass rate of 8.15%. The paper’s contributions include task collection, standardized, machine-checkable task representations, an engineer-like test pipeline, and SOTA model evaluation.


---

## pqac-00000033

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is a benchmark and end-to-end evaluation pipeline for testing LLMs on board-level schematic design. The authors assembled a structured dataset of 300 circuit design tasks and 2,914 cleaned component datasheets, converting multimodal PDFs into JSON and using guided LLM extraction to produce component descriptions and pin definitions. The generative pipeline prompts LLMs sequentially for component allocation, module-level local netlist generation (using a strict plain-text syntax like netX: Component|Pin|Function to reduce formatting hallucinations), and system-level global netlist integration. Outputs are post-processed by scripts that strip Markdown and serialize to a predefined JSON schema. For verification, HWE-Bench uses dual-stage evaluation: static rule checking (rule-based label matching to verify topology and protocol alignment) and dynamic SPICE transient simulation (LLM netlists converted to SPICE and injected into a Python testbench against a human-prepared ground-truth SPICE circuit). The primary metric is the aggregate pass rate across static and dynamic checkpoints; success requires no physical damage and compliance of key node voltages/currents with safety thresholds under specified excitations.


---

## pqac-00000034

From text 'qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 2-3'.

HWE-Bench: Can Language Models Perform Board-level Schematic Designs? This article has 0 citations.

HWE-Bench is presented as a comprehensive, execution-driven benchmark to evaluate large language models on end-to-end board-level schematic and circuit design tasks. Motivated by limitations of prior text- and software-centric evaluations, it emphasizes the zero-fault-tolerance nature of physical board design and the insufficiency of discriminative or isolated subtask methods. The benchmark proposes a test pipeline that emulates human engineering cognitive pathways and guides models through multi-step design tasks. Core to HWE-Bench is a dual-verification evaluation: (1) expert-level static electrical rule checking and (2) dynamic physical simulations, together establishing a new standard for validating LLM-generated physical-level system netlists. The benchmark specifically targets the model’s ability to translate unstructured natural language specifications and multimodal datasheet constraints into autonomously generated, globally verified schematic designs, filling a capability gap left by previous benchmarks that focused on parsing, assistive text generation, or isolated routing/placement subtasks.


---

## pqac-00000035

From text 'song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3'.

Inpyo Song, Eunji Jeon, and Jangwon Lee. Pceval: a benchmark for evaluating physical computing capabilities of large language models. ArXiv, Dec 2601. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2601.02404, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2601.02404. This article has 0 citations.

PCEVAL is a benchmark for assessing LLMs on physical-computing tasks that combine hardware and software (Arduino projects). It is presented as the first benchmark enabling fully automatic evaluation of both logical and physical aspects: circuit generation and code generation, across varying project complexity. The framework decomposes tasks into four dimensions: Logical Circuit Generation, Physical Circuit Generation, Code from Logical Circuits, and Code from Physical Circuits. Outputs are validated automatically using standardized test procedures and circuit validation metrics within a simulation environment; metrics measure logical correctness and physical implementation feasibility (e.g., pin conflicts, extraneous components). The benchmark includes functional success criteria (example: "The test is successful when: Piezo buzzer runs when the button is pressed") and supports reproducible, automated validation without human graders. The authors evaluated 13 LLMs and report that models perform reasonably well on code generation and logical circuit design but struggle with breadboard-level physical layout, especially correct pin connections and avoiding circuit errors. The paper contrasts prior work that relied on manual expert evaluation and often ignored physical layout constraints, highlighting PCEVAL’s contribution toward automated, hardware-aware LLM evaluation.

The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a constraint-guided framework and benchmark for LLM-based printed-circuit schematic design. Table 3 compares the framework against baselines including a GNN and an LLM (Plettenberg et al., 2025; Hasan et al., 2026). Experimental notes highlight that stronger multi-modal and spatial reasoning (Gemini 3 performed best) and circuit-level conceptualization are important. The paper documents common failure modes: misunderstanding pin functions and producing meaningless connections due to incorrect abstract topology synthesis. Example tasks (Task 3: differential-to-single-ended op-amp buffer; Task 11: SiC MOSFET half-bridge with HF caps) are used to illustrate successes and failures, with concrete electrical constraints (e.g., Vin = Vin+ − Vin− = 2V, Vout = 3V). Metrics reported include pass@1-style overall percentages. This material is directly relevant for designing or comparing a benchmark like HWE-Bench for board-level schematic evaluation and for defining evaluation criteria, task difficulty, and common LLM failure modes.

## pqac-00000037

From text 'lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2'.

Richard Lin and Björn Hartmann. Opportunities and challenges for circuit board level hardware description languages. Preprint, Jan 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242, doi:10.48550/arxiv.2011.08242. This article has 2 citations.

The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks, a board-level hardware description language implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language. The authors position board-level HDLs as a way to raise abstraction and increase automation beyond traditional schematic capture. They emphasize generator-based, programmatic construction (a Chisel-like approach) so a generator defines methodology and can automatically size internal component values for each application. Object-oriented concepts such as type hierarchies and inheritance are proposed to raise design level: types can encode parameters (e.g., voltage and current ratings) to enable correctness checks and to support automated selection among compatible alternatives. Polymorphic Blocks is presented as a library-based flow that uses these ideas; the provided Blinky example shows block instantiation (self.Block(...)) and explicit connections (self.connect(...)). Subcomponents like IndicatorLed are themselves generators that can contain code to size parts (e.g., resistors) based on inputs. Overall, the paper highlights automated component sizing, generator reuse, and type-directed selection as core features for PCB-level HDLs.

## pqac-00000038

From text 'lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56'.

RC Lin. Human-centered circuit board design with flexible levels of abstraction and ambiguity. Unknown journal, 2021.

The excerpt describes a Polymorphic Circuit Blocks model and a Python-like hardware construction language (HCL) that supports abstraction and gradual refinement. A class hierarchy lets designers instantiate abstract, generic blocks (e.g., a step-down converter or LED) and later refine them into concrete subclasses; however, replacing an abstract block with a subclass can be unsafe without checks because physical constraints (like current limits) may differ. Ports and links carry typed electrical parameters: ports expose properties (voltage_out, current_limit, current_draw, voltage_limit), and links aggregate those to compute net-level parameters (total current, acceptable voltage range) and run assertions. The electronics layer models power, digital, and analog port types and bundles for multi-wire protocols. Libraries combine primitives and generator blocks — e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, inductors, capacitors, diodes, transistors, and higher-level blocks (pull-ups, decoupling caps) that translate port parameters to component ratings. Automated component selection and sizing examples include RC filter calculation from cutoff frequency and resistive divider selection from E24 values. The system exposes a generator HCL/Python DSL for composing blocks (example: class Blinky(Block): ...).

## pqac-00000039

Richard Lin, Rohit Ramesh, Connie Chi, Nikhil Jain, Ryan Nuqui, Prabal Dutta, and Björn Hartmann. Polymorphic blocks: unifying high-level specification and low-level control for circuit board design. Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology, Oct 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3379337.3415860, doi:10.1145/3379337.3415860. This article has 26 citations.

Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-based HDL implemented as a library of base classes with mypy annotations, enabling a typed DSL for circuit design. The system uses a hierarchical type/refinement model: abstract blocks can be interactively refined to concrete subclasses (for example, refining an abstract resistor to an SMD part), keeping high-level HDL while handling specifics via refinements. Some blocks act as generators: during lowering the generator is provided concrete parameter values and expands into internal elements. Generators execute once and in no guaranteed order, so they must handle parameter ranges via worst-case bounds. Parameter constraints are parsed into a directed constraint graph and evaluated lazily; constraint forms like assignments and subset bounds are recorded specially. The compiler incrementally lowers the high-level model, performs simplifications, and finally netlists the design as a tree walk, producing netlist files that can be imported into KiCad. The flow supports deterministic component naming tied to HDL variable names to enable netlist updates to in-progress layouts. The project is open-source on GitHub.

Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded domain specific language (HCL) for hierarchical circuit/block design. It uses classes and a library of base classes to represent reusable block templates, with Ports, Blocks, and GeneratorBlocks allowing programmatic construction of subcircuits. Generators are member functions that instantiate internal sub-blocks, ports, and parameters; they receive (concrete or bounded) parameter values during compilation and can "access solved values like digital logic thresholds, and use those to automatically size internal blocks like the resistor," enabling automated component selection and sizing. The DSL includes syntactic helpers such as implicit_connect (for common nets like power/ground) and chain (for linear signal-through topologies). A GUI visualizer supports refinement by letting users choose block subclass refinements interactively; refined subcircuits are generated and model checks run. The compiler lowers the high-level model via a tree walk performing refinement and generation, updates a constraint graph (constraints parsed into a directed graph), and requires generators to supply worst-case bounds because generators run once and in no particular order. The lowered design can be exported as a netlist for KiCad. The project is open-source on GitHub.

The system supports block-level polymorphism and a class hierarchy so users can write abstract blocks (e.g., a step-down converter) that are later refined into concrete subcircuits (e.g., specific buck converters tied to controller chips). Generators and subcircuit generators are a core mechanism (and the work cites approaches that embed generators in general-purpose languages), but this system augments generators with explicit electronics modeling to enable design support such as automated parts selection and correctness checks. The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks' layered model combining structural and class hierarchies to support both encapsulation and abstraction in PCB/schematic design. Structural hierarchy (hierarchy blocks, bridges) lets sub-sheets export or bridge ports to containing blocks, preserving internal parameters; bridges are two-ported blocks that map external-facing ports to flipped internal roles. The class hierarchy enables abstract parts and subclasses (is-a relationships), allowing delaying concrete specification and reuse. An electronics layer models pin/link types and part ratings; ports carry physical properties (voltage output, current limits, current draw, voltage limits) and parameters "flow" through connected ports into links, which aggregate net properties and include assertions to check correctness. The system includes generators (e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, plus inductor, capacitor, diode, and transistor generators from parts tables) and wraps primitives in higher-level library blocks. Block constraints and link assertions provide automated checks for compatibility and invariants. Together, these features support automated component selection and propagation of electrical parameters across hierarchical and typed block abstractions.

The excerpt is a bibliography pointing to multiple research and tool directions relevant to programmatic circuit creation and automated smart-device design. Key citations discuss circuit-board-level hardware description languages and their opportunities and challenges, and propose integrating code and visual schematics for interactive editing. Work on "Polymorphic Blocks" aims to bridge high-level specifications and low-level control in PCB design, while examples of custom HDLs for PCB capture show practical language-based capture approaches. Several prototyping and authoring tools are referenced—Fritzing, KiCad (and action plugins), AutoFritz (autocomplete for virtual breadboards), and VirtualComponent (mixed-reality tuning)—indicating emphasis on rapid iteration, mixed visual/code workflows, and support for amateurs and designers. Foundational and historical automation efforts (e.g., AUTO CARD) and studies on scaling hardware production highlight manufacturing and scaling challenges beyond prototyping. References to live programming and interactive visualization (e.g., "The Future of Programming will be Live", "Usable Live Programming", and Bifröst) suggest benefits of immediate feedback in embedded/hardware development. The excerpt describes three tools that automate and programmatically generate smart-device designs by encapsulating circuit and firmware information into higher-level representations. Amalgam embeds firmware functionality into web technologies (HTML/CSS/JS), enabling rapid prototyping and allowing designers to ‘‘transform web pages into fully functional electronic devices in a couple of minutes,’’ but it provides limited low-level configurability (low threshold, low ceiling). Appliancizer builds on Amalgam to also integrate circuit design data and automatically generate complete electronic designs (including PCB layout) and low-level code from static web prototypes; it uses "essential interface mapping" to map HTML controls to tangible controls, enabling circuit synthesis and device simulation, and can turn web apps (e.g., video player, smart thermostat) into physical devices without changing source code, showing a 6.5X speed improvement over Amalgam. TypedSchematics targets schematic reuse and scalability: it offers real-time validation of mismatches, an abstraction called Mats to remove repetitive ground/voltage connections, and a flow-based model for constraint checking; users produced thermostat schematics 2.8x faster than with Fusion 360. The work highlights trade-offs between ease-of-use, automation, configurability, and library scalability in programmatic circuit creation and end-to-end smart-device design automation.

The excerpt discusses board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) and the potential to apply programming-language concepts—specifically generator languages and type systems—to automate and simplify circuit board design. Such programmatic approaches can encapsulate low-level knowledge in libraries, increasing efficiency and accessibility for creating devices. A key challenge identified is user confidence: simulation and static modeling are incomplete, and community mechanisms (for example, marking subcircuits as “physically fabricated and tested”) may help vets reusable components and generators. The authors note that confidence in generators (which produce many instances) is different from confidence in individual fabricated subcircuits and may require additional processes. They highlight many open research questions spanning models, abstractions, and user interfaces, and recommend cross-disciplinary work across electrical engineering, programming languages, and HCI to develop powerful, usable systems. This context is directly relevant to efforts like JITX, Stanza, and other programmatic/design-automation approaches for creating smart devices, particularly around generator-based design, reusable libraries, testing/validation, and tooling/UI needs.

J Garza Guardado. “sudo make me a smart device”: accelerating and automating the end-to-end design of smart devices. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt is a bibliography listing works directly relevant to programmatic circuit creation and design automation for smart devices. It cites research on abstractions and toolchains for PCB design (e.g., "Polymorphic blocks", "Beyond schematic capture"), and on automated/embedded design generation ("Turning coders into makers: the promise of embedded design generation"). It also references programming-language work ([87] "The Stanza Language") and recent code-generation resources ([85] OpenAI GPT-3 demo), which are relevant to programmatic specification and synthesis of device behavior and firmware. Practical tool and component references appear (Atmega328, Multisim, Proteus, MCP9808, serial flash), as well as work on coupling simulation and hardware and PCB debugging pipelines ("Coupling simulation and hardware for interactive circuit debugging", "Pinpoint"), supporting iterative design, verification, and fabrication workflows. Collectively, these citations support topics around automating end-to-end smart-device design (specification languages, code/gen-assisted synthesis, board-level abstractions, simulation/debugging, and tooling). The excerpt does not appear to reference JITX explicitly.

The excerpt is a bibliography listing works relevant to programmatic and automated circuit design, PCB-level hardware description languages (HDLs), and tooling for prototyping and verification. It cites research on circuit-board HDLs and human-centered abstractions (e.g., "Opportunities and Challenges for Circuit Board Level Hardware Description Languages", "Beyond Schematic Capture", and "Polymorphic Blocks"), as well as practical examples of custom HDLs for PCB capture ("Using a Custom-Built HDL for Printed Circuit Board Design Capture"). Prototyping and mixed-reality aids are represented by "AutoFritz: Autocomplete for Prototyping Virtual Breadboard Circuits" and "VirtualComponent", while designer-focused tools include Fritzing and KiCad (plus "KiCad Action Plugins"). Xpedition Enterprise", "Ultra Librarian"), automated routing and layout research (e.g., AUTO CARD, interactive routing), and live programming concepts applied to development workflows ("The Future of Programming will be Live", "Usable Live Programming"). There are also entries on cross-domain verification and visualizing embedded behavior ("Bifröst"), indicating attention to integrated hardware/software design and tool-supported automation for creating smart devices.

## pqac-00000057

From text 'guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 56-60'.

J Garza Guardado. “sudo make me a smart device”: accelerating and automating the end-to-end design of smart devices. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt describes Appliancizer, a design-automation tool that lets designers create smart-device prototypes as web pages and then automatically produce corresponding physical devices (schematics/PCB and low-level code). It treats HTML as the hardware description language, mapping HTML GUI elements to reusable "tangible controls" (modules containing schematics, code, and hardware specs). Examples include mapping knobs and sensors to HTML sliders. Appliancizer emphasizes a higher-level approach: it simulates interface events rather than electrical circuit behavior and uses modular PCB schematic pieces that experts author and novices reuse. The tool contrasts with prior systems (JITPCB, EDG, TAC) that require new hardware description languages or target circuit-level descriptions; instead, Appliancizer leverages ubiquitous web technologies and MVC patterns to generate circuits from web prototypes. It builds on prior work (Amalgam) that extended the HTML DOM to the physical domain but adds automated PCB generation and deployment. These points are relevant when comparing programmatic circuit creation approaches (HDLs, web-based mappings, modular schematic libraries, automation of PCB/layout/code generation).

## pqac-00000058

From text 'guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 24-29'.

J Garza Guardado. “sudo make me a smart device”: accelerating and automating the end-to-end design of smart devices. Unknown journal, 2023.

The excerpt outlines the conventional multi-stage process for designing smart devices (device simulation, component selection, prototyping, low-level code, netlists/schematics, PCB layout/manufacture, and mechanical design) and observes that tools across these stages are largely disconnected. It emphasizes the need for device simulation and correct mapping between low-level code and hardware interfaces (GPIO, I2C, SPI, UART) and notes dependencies such as pin mapping and matching PCB schematics. The paper proposes fusing stages by encapsulating circuit data to enable new automation workflows. As an example, Amalgam integrates low-level hardware code into web programming: it embeds hardware components into HTML web components and places hardware interface specifications and pin assignments into CSS. Amalgam provides a nine-element module library and demonstrates reduced programming effort—reportedly one line of code per hardware component—illustrating a programmatic approach to bridging application logic and hardware configuration that could inform programmatic circuit generation and design-automation systems.

## pqac-00000059

From text 'lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 10-11'.

Richard Lin, Rohit Ramesh, Connie Chi, Nikhil Jain, Ryan Nuqui, Prabal Dutta, and Björn Hartmann. Polymorphic blocks: unifying high-level specification and low-level control for circuit board design. Proceedings of the 33rd Annual ACM Symposium on User Interface Software and Technology, Oct 2020. URL: https://doi.org/10.1145/3379337.3415860, doi:10.1145/3379337.3415860. This article has 26 citations.

Lin et al. describe Polymorphic Blocks, an approach that implements an HDL and compiler around a hierarchy block-diagram abstraction to provide design automation. The system lets designers compose high-level blocks while experienced engineers write re-usable generators—executable code that implements block designs—so complex subcircuits can be generated from high-level specifications and instantiated in hardware-proven examples. The approach is library-driven: good, complete part and subcircuit libraries are needed to maximize reuse, and many complex part details remain trapped in PDF datasheets. The paper suggests automating datasheet extraction (e.g., Tabula, DocParser) to populate libraries. They note potential integration with graphical, schematic-like interfaces to lower the need for programming experience, but also list current limitations: the electronics model only defines a few signal interfaces and lacks support for multiple grounds. The work targets intermediate-level designers and demonstrates a generator-based, programmatic path to circuit creation while highlighting scalability, tooling, and data requirements for broader adoption.
