answer.answer:
# Code-Based, Programmatic, and Design-as-Code Approaches to Electronics and PCB Design: A Review

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of software engineering practices with electronics design has given rise to a family of tools and frameworks that treat printed circuit board (PCB) schematics, netlists, and layouts as code artifacts amenable to version control, continuous integration (CI), and reproducible builds. Analogous to how CadQuery and OpenSCAD enable "code-CAD" for mechanical design, board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) and programmatic EDA tools promise to bring generator-based abstractions, type systems, parametric reuse, and automated verification to electronics (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2). Lin and Hartmann framed this space by noting that board-level HDLs can encapsulate specialized knowledge in reusable libraries, apply object-oriented type hierarchies with voltage and current parameters for correctness checks, and use generator constructs that automatically size components per application—analogous to the Chisel approach in chip design (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4). This review surveys the major tools and frameworks in this space, discusses LLM-based generative approaches, and assesses fitness for an open-hardware, CI-driven workflow suitable for a modular multi-powder dosing device.

## 2. Tool-by-Tool Analysis

The following table provides a comparative overview of the tools discussed:

| Tool | Programming Model/Language | Outputs (Gerber/BOM/Netlist) | Parametrization & Reuse | DRC/Verification | CI/Version-Control Fit | Maturity | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| atopile | Custom `.ato` HDL compiled by Python tooling into KiCad projects; textual electronics “design-as-code” | Netlist, KiCad schematic/PCB artifacts, BOM; Gerbers typically via downstream KiCad export | Strong module/library reuse and parameterized blocks; better than raw netlists for reusable subcircuits | Relies largely on compiler checks plus downstream KiCad ERC/DRC/manufacturing checks | Very good: text-first, Git-friendly, scriptable, suited to CI-generated outputs | Early-stage but active | Apache-2.0 |
| tscircuit | TypeScript/React/JSX model: PCB described as components in code (“React-for-PCB”) | Gerbers, BOM, pick-and-place/net connectivity outputs via toolchain | Strong software-style composition, props, package reuse, monorepo ergonomics | Tool-native checks plus downstream fab/export validation; less proven than KiCad-native DRC flows | Excellent: npm/TS ecosystem, deterministic builds, easy CI rendering | Early-stage / rapidly evolving | MIT |
| SKiDL | Python EDSL for schematic capture; instantiate `Part`, `Net`, connect in Python | Netlists directly; can feed KiCad flows, BOM generation, and downstream PCB/Gerber export | High: full Python for loops/functions/classes; reusable subcircuits and generators | Built-in ERC, syntax/runtime checks; often paired with KiCad/PCB tool DRC later (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | Excellent: plain text, Python testing, easy automation in CI | Mature open-source niche; widely cited in research/prototypes (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3) | MIT |
| BHDL | Embedded DSL in Racket; every circuit is a function; layout combinators and Jupyter REPL (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3) | KiCad-format PCB, BOM, Gerber via export/routing flow (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6) | Strong: first-class circuits, host-language loops/functions, hierarchical composition, series/parallel/bus combinators (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5) | Autoplacement, legalization, routability-aware placement; verification less mature than mainstream EDA DRC (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6) | Good for research/CI due to text + notebook flow, but ecosystem smaller than Python/KiCad | Research prototype | Open-source research code |
| Polymorphic Blocks | Python-embedded HDL/HCL with types, generators, constraint graph, GUI-assisted refinement (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) | Netlist export to KiCad/layout tools (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 56-60) | Very strong: abstract blocks, subclasses, generators, automated sizing/selection from constraints (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 56-60) | Strong semantic checking via typed ports/links and constraint propagation, but mostly research-grade (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 2-4, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5) | Good in principle: textual HDL and deterministic naming aid version control (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) | Research prototype | Open-source research code (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7) |
| JITX | Commercial programmatic PCB in Stanza; PCB generators, constraints, and layout automation | Netlist, BOM, placement/routing outputs, manufacturing files incl. Gerber from product flow | Very strong: libraries, generators, algorithmic placement/routing, reusable design patterns | Strong integrated rule/constraint-driven automation; one of the most capable code-native PCB systems | Good technically, but proprietary/runtime licensing complicates open CI compared with FOSS | Commercially mature | Proprietary/commercial |
| PCBmodE | Python + SVG/XML-driven PCB definition; board artwork and geometry as code/data | SVG-centric board descriptions; Gerber/manufacturing output supported through flow | Moderate: parametric geometry/artwork good; electrical abstraction weaker than HDL-style tools | Limited electrical verification compared with mainstream EDA; best for geometry-driven/simple boards | Good for Git because source is text/SVG, but narrower scope and smaller ecosystem | Older niche project; limited recent activity | Open-source (CC-BY-SA/GPL-style project assets/code mix) |
| gEDA/gaf | Traditional Unix-style open EDA suite; text schematics/netlists plus scriptable utilities | Netlist, BOM, Gerber via pcb/gerbv toolchain | Moderate: scriptable and library-based, but less ergonomic parametrization than newer HDL flows | Established ERC/DRC depending on subtool; manufacturing outputs proven | Good: text files and CLI tools suit CI, though workflow feels legacy | Mature but aging | GPL |
| Horizon EDA | GUI-first open-source EDA with structured text-backed project data and some scriptability | Gerber, drill, BOM, pick-and-place, netlist | Moderate: strong part/library reuse; less “full code CAD” than atopile/SKiDL/JITX | Strong native DRC/manufacturing outputs for open-source desktop EDA | Good: project files are Git-manageable and CLI/export automation is possible, though not as code-native as HDL tools | Active and increasingly capable | GPL-3.0 |
| Edea | Python library/tooling for generating or manipulating KiCad project files programmatically | KiCad schematics/PCB data, netlists, and downstream Gerber/BOM through KiCad | Moderate to strong: Python-based templating/manipulation enables reuse around KiCad artifacts | Usually delegates true ERC/DRC to KiCad after generation | Very good: ideal for repository automation, transformations, and CI wrappers around KiCad | Early-stage / tooling layer rather than full EDA | Open-source |
| KiCad Python / `kicad-cli` | Hybrid model: GUI editor plus Python API/action plugins and command-line automation | Native Gerber, drill, IPC netlist, BOM, STEP, position files, PDFs | Moderate: reuse via hierarchical sheets, templates, scripts, plugins, generated footprints/boards | Strong mainstream ERC/DRC and manufacturing checks; CLI-friendly in newer releases | Very good: central choice for open hardware CI/CD; scriptable exports/checks align with HardOps principles (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12) | Very mature, dominant open-source PCB tool | GPL-3.0 |


*Table: This table compares major code-based and scriptable PCB/electronics design approaches across programming model, outputs, verification, reuse, CI fit, maturity, and licensing. It is useful for selecting a reproducible, version-controlled workflow and distinguishing research prototypes from production-capable tools.*

### 2.1 atopile (.ato Language)

Atopile (github.com/atopile/atopile) introduces a custom `.ato` hardware description language compiled by a Python-based toolchain into KiCad project files. The programming model emphasizes textual, declarative circuit descriptions with module imports, parameterized components, and hierarchical composition. Designs are expressed in `.ato` files that compile to KiCad schematics and netlists; Gerber and BOM generation is delegated downstream to KiCad. The text-first nature makes atopile exceptionally Git-friendly, and its package manager supports reusable module libraries. DRC is handled by KiCad after compilation. Licensed under Apache-2.0, atopile is early-stage but represents one of the most complete "design-as-code" visions for electronics, with an active community and growing component ecosystem. Its philosophy aligns closely with the broader vision of board-level HDLs that raise abstraction while preserving fine-grained control (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4).

### 2.2 tscircuit (React-for-PCB)

tscircuit (github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit) models PCB designs as React/TypeScript component trees, where JSX elements describe resistors, capacitors, ICs, traces, and board outlines. This leverages the entire npm/TypeScript ecosystem for composition, testing, and CI. Circuits are defined declaratively using component props for values and footprints, then rendered to Gerber files, BOMs, netlists, and 3D previews. The React model naturally supports parametric reuse through props, higher-order components, and package publishing. DRC is handled internally with growing capability. Licensed under MIT, tscircuit is rapidly evolving and particularly appealing to web-developer communities, though its electronics library coverage is still growing.

### 2.3 SKiDL

SKiDL (github.com/devbisme/skidl) is a mature Python embedded DSL for schematic capture where designers instantiate `Part` objects, create `Net` connections, and call built-in ERC checks—all in Python (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3). Gutiérrez et al. noted that SKiDL and PySpice are Python tools focused on PCB design and netlist generation, though with some simulator limitations (gutierrez2023pythonframeworkfor pages 2-3). SKiDL's strength lies in leveraging full Python for loops, functions, classes, and conditional logic to generate parameterized, reusable circuit descriptions. It outputs netlists compatible with KiCad and includes built-in electrical rule checking. The PCBSchemaGen system specifically chose SKiDL as its intermediate representation because "Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning" (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4). SKiDL is MIT-licensed, widely cited in research, and represents the most mature purely code-based schematic capture tool.

### 2.4 BHDL (Board HDL)

BHDL is a declarative embedded DSL implemented in Racket, presented at DAC 2021, where every circuit element—even a resistor—is modeled as a function to enable hierarchical, modular composition (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2). BHDL provides concise connection syntax including `net`, `series`, `parallel`, and `bus` forms, plus layout combinators (`hstack`, `vstack`, `rotate`, `at`) inspired by VLSI placement tools (li2021bhdlalucid pages 2-3, li2021bhdlalucid pages 3-5). For layout, BHDL integrates an analytical global placer based on RePlace and a simulated-annealing detailed placer with rotation support, implemented in Julia (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). The system exports KiCad-format PCB files, Gerber, and BOMs, and supports REPL-style development via a Jupyter kernel (li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). As a research prototype, BHDL demonstrates the power of functional composition for PCB design but has a smaller ecosystem than Python-based alternatives.

### 2.5 Polymorphic Blocks

Polymorphic Blocks, developed by Lin et al. (2020), is a Python-embedded HDL that extends hierarchical block diagrams with polymorphism, typed ports carrying electrical parameters (voltage, current, thresholds), and generator blocks that programmatically instantiate subcircuits (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 2-4). Ports and links propagate parameters through a directed constraint graph and include assertions for correctness checking; generators can access solved values to automatically size components such as resistors from the E24 series (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 56-60, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5). The compiler lowers designs via refinement and generation steps and exports netlists for KiCad import, with deterministic component naming to support layout updates (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7). The project is open-source on GitHub and demonstrates how type systems and constraint propagation can provide meaningful automation for intermediate-level designers (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 5-7, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 10-11).

### 2.6 JITX (Stanza-Based Programmatic PCB)

JITX (jitx.com) is a commercial programmatic PCB design platform built on the Stanza programming language. Referenced in multiple academic works as JITPCB (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 27-30, li2021bhdlalucid pages 6-6), JITX embeds circuit construction primitives in a generator-style programming model, enabling parameterized design families with algorithmic placement, routing, and constraint-driven automation. It produces complete manufacturing outputs including Gerber files, BOMs, and pick-and-place data. JITX's integrated rule and constraint system is among the most capable code-native PCB verification flows. However, its proprietary licensing creates friction for open-hardware workflows, and the requirement to learn Stanza represents a barrier compared with Python-based alternatives (guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 129-132). JITX is commercially mature and targets professional engineering teams requiring high automation.

### 2.7 PCBmodE

PCBmodE (github.com/boldport/pcbmode) takes a unique approach by defining PCB geometry and artwork through JSON configuration files and SVG graphics. This makes it excellent for artistic or geometry-driven board designs where visual aesthetics matter. It produces Gerber manufacturing files from SVG-based board descriptions. However, its electrical abstraction is weaker than HDL-style tools—it lacks netlist-level schematic capture, DRC, or ERC capabilities. The project has seen limited recent activity but remains interesting as a purely text/data-driven PCB definition approach that is naturally Git-friendly.

### 2.8 gEDA/gaf

The gEDA project provides a traditional Unix-style open-source EDA suite with text-based schematics (gschem), scriptable netlisting utilities, and the PCB layout tool with Gerber export via gerbv. Files are text-based and CLI tools support scripted workflows, making gEDA compatible with version control and CI pipelines. However, the toolchain feels legacy compared with modern alternatives, and parametric design support is less ergonomic than newer HDL-based flows. Licensed under GPL, gEDA remains functional but has largely been superseded by KiCad for new open-hardware projects.

### 2.9 Horizon EDA

Horizon EDA (github.com/horizon-eda/horizon) is a GUI-first open-source EDA tool with strong DRC, manufacturing-output capabilities, and structured text-backed project data. While not a "code-CAD" tool per se, its project files are Git-manageable and it supports some scriptability and CLI-driven export. Horizon EDA excels at part and library management and is licensed under GPL-3.0. It bridges the gap between GUI-first design and version-controllable workflows.

### 2.10 Edea

Edea (github.com/edea-dev/edea) is a Python library for programmatically generating or manipulating KiCad project files. It operates as a tooling layer rather than a full EDA suite, enabling scripts to create, modify, or template KiCad schematics and PCB layouts. This makes it ideal for CI pipeline integration—for example, automated variant generation or BOM manipulation—while delegating ERC/DRC to KiCad itself. Edea is open-source and fits well into workflows where KiCad is the primary design tool but automation is needed around it.

### 2.11 KiCad Python Scripting and kicad-cli

KiCad, the dominant open-source PCB tool (GPL-3.0), offers extensive automation through its Python scripting API, action plugins, and the newer `kicad-cli` command-line interface. The Python API enables programmatic creation and manipulation of schematics, footprints, and board layouts; action plugins extend the GUI with custom functionality (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboarda pages 97-100, lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 97-100). The `kicad-cli` tool (introduced in KiCad 7+) supports headless export of Gerber files, drill files, BOMs, PDFs, STEP models, and schematic/PCB renderings—making it the key enabler for CI pipelines. KiCad's text-based file formats (`.kicad_sch`, `.kicad_pcb`) are Git-manageable, though diffs can be complex (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9). The HardOps framework demonstrated that CI pipelines can automate quality checks, artifact generation, and documentation for open-hardware projects, with KiCad as a central tool (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12).

## 3. LLM-Based and AI/Generative Approaches

Recent research has explored using large language models for text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB generation. The following table summarizes the key systems:

| System | Approach | Intermediate Representation | Verification Method | Benchmark Size | Best Reported Performance | Key Limitations | Year/Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCBSchemaGen (Zou et al.) | Training-free LLM pipeline for natural-language-to-schematic generation using domain-specific prompting, CoT, ICL, KG-guided constraints, and iterative feedback (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | SKiDL Python code, then KiCad schematic/layout artifacts (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4) | Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in ERC, knowledge-graph pin-role checks, topology verification via subgraph isomorphism/VF2, iterative localized feedback (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26) | 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks across digital, analog, and power domains (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | Hard-task success reported at 78.1%; easy-task success 93.3%; best model ~23k tokens/task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 min/task (~37× human speed) (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7) | Failures from misunderstanding pin functions and generating incorrect abstract topologies; depends on curated KG/component library (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 24-26) | 2025 research prototype / open-source benchmark+code (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) |
| HWE-Bench (Qiu et al.) | Execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design; prompts models through module decomposition, component allocation, local netlists, and global integration (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5) | Structured plain-text/JSON netlists derived from NL specs and datasheets; converted to SPICE/test representations for checking (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 2-3) | Dual verification: static rule checking on topology/protocol/pin properties plus dynamic LTspice transient simulation with threshold checks; pin-locking/resource-conflict simulation (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5) | 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8) | Best overall full-design pass rate only 8.15%; average static pass ~71.84%, dynamic ~58.65% (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2) | Major failures in context overload, role confusion, multiplexed-pin conflicts, and poor global physical reasoning; models often pass local/static checks but fail full design correctness (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8) | 2025 benchmark/evaluation framework |
| PCEval (Song et al.) | Benchmark for physical-computing LLM tasks spanning circuit + code generation for Arduino-like projects; evaluates both logical and physical design ability (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Logical circuit descriptions and physical circuit layouts with associated code artifacts in simulation (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Automated simulation-based validation of logical correctness and physical feasibility, including pin-conflict and extraneous-component checks (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Not stated in gathered excerpt; covers multiple project complexities and 13 LLMs (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | No single percentage cited in gathered excerpt; qualitative result is that models do reasonably on code/logical circuit generation but struggle on breadboard-level physical layout (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Weakness on physical wiring/layout correctness despite better performance on code and logical circuits; oriented to physical-computing tasks rather than full PCB CAD (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | 2025 benchmark |
| General LLM + SKiDL approaches | Use an LLM to synthesize executable Python-based schematic code from text, leveraging SKiDL because it is concise, code-native, and better aligned with LLMs than vendor schematic formats (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | SKiDL Python as the central executable IR; downstream conversion to KiCad schematics/PCB files (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | Typically combines SKiDL/ERC checks with downstream KiCad generation; stronger systems add datasheet-derived knowledge graphs, topological checks, and iterative feedback loops (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3) | No single standard benchmark; examples include PCBSchemaGen's 23-task suite and related evaluation frameworks like HWE-Bench/PCEval (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3) | Most convincing evidence comes from PCBSchemaGen rather than generic SKiDL prompting; generic approaches remain highly variable and dataset-constrained (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | Limited by scarce open PCB data, vendor-specific capture formats, package/pin constraints, and insufficient physical verification if using only syntax/ERC or SPICE (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) | 2023-2025 emerging pattern rather than a single mature tool (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2) |


*Table: This table compares the main recent LLM-based text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB research systems, their intermediate representations, verification strategies, benchmark scales, and limitations. It is useful for distinguishing promising constraint-guided approaches from broader benchmarks that reveal current reliability gaps.*

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al.) is a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses LLMs to generate SKiDL Python code from natural-language specifications. It builds a knowledge graph from IC datasheets encoding pin-role semantics (36 role types) and topological constraints, compressing per-device context from ~16k tokens to ~300 tokens (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 3-4). Multi-stage verification combines SKiDL syntax/ERC checks with topology verification via the VF2 subgraph isomorphism algorithm (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 5-6). On a 23-task benchmark spanning digital, analog, and power domains, PCBSchemaGen achieved 93.3% success on easy tasks and 78.1% on hard tasks, with a ~37× speedup over human designers (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 7-8, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 6-7, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2).

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al.) provides a more rigorous evaluation with 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains paired with 2,914 IC datasheets. It applies dual verification: static electrical rule checking and dynamic LTspice transient simulation (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 4-5). The results are sobering: even the best model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieved only 8.15% overall correct-design pass rate, despite average static pass rates of ~72% (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 1-2). Key failure modes include context overload with large component lists, MCU pin-multiplexing errors, and inability to maintain global logical consistency (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 8-8).

**PCEval** (Song et al.) benchmarks LLMs on physical computing tasks combining circuit and code generation for Arduino projects. Models performed reasonably on logical circuit design and code generation but struggled with physical breadboard-level layout, particularly pin-conflict avoidance (song2601pcevalabenchmark pages 1-3).

These results indicate that while LLM-assisted schematic generation shows promise for constrained subtasks—especially when augmented with knowledge graphs, iterative feedback, and domain-specific verification—full end-to-end autonomous PCB design remains unreliable for production use.

## 4. Trade-offs: GUI-Based EDA vs. Code-Based EDA vs. AI/Generative EDA

**GUI-based EDA** (KiCad, Altium, EAGLE) excels at interactive layout optimization, complex trace routing, and visual inspection. Experienced designers achieve superior placement and routing quality through direct manipulation. However, GUI tools produce binary or semi-structured files that resist meaningful Git diffs, make parametric variant generation manual, and are difficult to drive from CI pipelines without significant scripting effort (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 12-13).

**Code-based EDA** (atopile, SKiDL, tscircuit, BHDL, JITX) treats electronics design as a programming activity with all attendant benefits: plain-text source control, meaningful diffs and code review, automated testing, parameterized reuse through functions and modules, and deterministic CI-generated outputs (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9). The trade-off is that placement and routing often require handoff to downstream tools (KiCad, FreeRouting) or are handled by less mature auto-placers (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6). Lin and Hartmann identified confidence in code-generated hardware as a key challenge: unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly, so community mechanisms for marking subcircuits as "physically fabricated and tested" are important (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 4-4).

**AI/Generative EDA** offers the most ambitious vision—natural-language to manufactured board—but current capabilities fall short. The HWE-Bench results (8.15% full-design pass rate) demonstrate that LLMs lack the physical intuition and global consistency needed for reliable circuit design (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 6-8). Targeted applications such as subcircuit suggestion, component selection, or datasheet extraction are more immediately practical (lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 10-11, guardado2023“sudomakeme pages 20-24).

## 5. Recommendations for an Open-Hardware, CI-Rendered Modular Dosing Device

For a modular multi-powder dosing device—likely involving motor drivers, load-cell amplifiers, microcontroller modules, and communication interfaces as repeated building blocks—the following architecture is recommended:

**Primary design tool: KiCad + atopile or SKiDL for schematic generation.** atopile's `.ato` files provide the cleanest "design-as-code" experience with native KiCad integration, Git-friendliness, and a growing package ecosystem. SKiDL offers a mature, well-documented alternative with the advantage of full Python ecosystem access and demonstrated compatibility with LLM-assisted generation workflows (zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 4-5, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 2-3). Both produce KiCad-compatible outputs.

**CI pipeline: kicad-cli + GitHub/GitLab CI.** Following HardOps principles (stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 7-9, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 3-4, stirling2022hardopsutilisingthe pages 9-12), the pipeline should run ERC/DRC checks, generate Gerber/drill files, render board previews (SVG/PDF), produce BOMs, and publish release artifacts on each commit. The `kicad-cli` tool enables all of these operations headlessly. Docker containers with KiCad pre-installed ensure reproducible builds.

**Modular reuse:** The dosing device's repeated channel modules (each with motor driver, load cell, valve control) should be defined as parameterized blocks—either atopile modules or SKiDL generator functions—instantiated N times with channel-specific parameters. Polymorphic Blocks' approach of abstract block types with typed ports and constraint propagation provides a strong conceptual model for this (lin2021humancenteredcircuitboard pages 54-56, lin2020polymorphicblocksunifying pages 4-5).

**Edea** can serve as a CI-layer tool for KiCad file manipulation tasks such as variant generation, BOM post-processing, or automated schematic transformations.

**Layout:** For a production device, interactive KiCad layout with replication of channel modules (using hierarchical sheets and "connection room" replication) remains preferable to fully automated placement, which is not yet sufficiently mature in open-source tools for mixed-signal designs (li2021bhdlalucid pages 5-6).

**Licensing alignment:** atopile (Apache-2.0), SKiDL (MIT), KiCad (GPL-3.0), Edea (open-source), and tscircuit (MIT) all support open-hardware distribution. JITX's proprietary model is less suitable for community-driven projects.

## 6. Conclusion

The design-as-code paradigm for electronics has matured substantially since 2020, with tools spanning custom HDLs (atopile), Python eDSLs (SKiDL, Polymorphic Blocks), functional languages (BHDL in Racket), React/TypeScript frameworks (tscircuit), and commercial platforms (JITX). These tools share the vision of raising abstraction through generators, type systems, and reusable libraries while producing version-controllable, CI-amenable artifacts (lin2020opportunitiesandchallenges pages 1-2, li2021bhdlalucid pages 1-2). LLM-based approaches show promise for constrained subtasks but remain unreliable for full-design autonomy (qiu2603hwebenchcanlanguage pages 5-6, zou2602pcbschemagenconstraintguidedschematic pages 1-2). For an open-hardware modular dosing device, a hybrid workflow combining code-generated schematics (atopile or SKiDL), KiCad-based layout/DRC, and CI automation via `kicad-cli` and HardOps principles offers the best balance of reproducibility, manufacturability, and community accessibility.

# Code-Based, Programmatic, and Design-as-Code Approaches to Electronics and PCB Design: A Review

## 1. Introduction

The convergence of software engineering practices with electronics design has given rise to a family of tools and frameworks that treat printed circuit board (PCB) schematics, netlists, and layouts as code artifacts amenable to version control, continuous integration (CI), and reproducible builds. Analogous to how CadQuery and OpenSCAD enable "code-CAD" for mechanical design, board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) and programmatic EDA tools promise to bring generator-based abstractions, type systems, parametric reuse, and automated verification to electronics (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000037). Lin and Hartmann framed this space by noting that board-level HDLs can encapsulate specialized knowledge in reusable libraries, apply object-oriented type hierarchies with voltage and current parameters for correctness checks, and use generator constructs that automatically size components per application—analogous to the Chisel approach in chip design (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000000). This review surveys the major tools and frameworks in this space, discusses LLM-based generative approaches, and assesses fitness for an open-hardware, CI-driven workflow suitable for a modular multi-powder dosing device.

## 2. Tool-by-Tool Analysis

The following table provides a comparative overview of the tools discussed:

| Tool | Programming Model/Language | Outputs (Gerber/BOM/Netlist) | Parametrization & Reuse | DRC/Verification | CI/Version-Control Fit | Maturity | License |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| atopile | Custom `.ato` HDL compiled by Python tooling into KiCad projects; textual electronics “design-as-code” | Netlist, KiCad schematic/PCB artifacts, BOM; Gerbers typically via downstream KiCad export | Strong module/library reuse and parameterized blocks; better than raw netlists for reusable subcircuits | Relies largely on compiler checks plus downstream KiCad ERC/DRC/manufacturing checks | Very good: text-first, Git-friendly, scriptable, suited to CI-generated outputs | Early-stage but active | Apache-2.0 |
| tscircuit | TypeScript/React/JSX model: PCB described as components in code (“React-for-PCB”) | Gerbers, BOM, pick-and-place/net connectivity outputs via toolchain | Strong software-style composition, props, package reuse, monorepo ergonomics | Tool-native checks plus downstream fab/export validation; less proven than KiCad-native DRC flows | Excellent: npm/TS ecosystem, deterministic builds, easy CI rendering | Early-stage / rapidly evolving | MIT |
| SKiDL | Python EDSL for schematic capture; instantiate `Part`, `Net`, connect in Python | Netlists directly; can feed KiCad flows, BOM generation, and downstream PCB/Gerber export | High: full Python for loops/functions/classes; reusable subcircuits and generators | Built-in ERC, syntax/runtime checks; often paired with KiCad/PCB tool DRC later (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000023) | Excellent: plain text, Python testing, easy automation in CI | Mature open-source niche; widely cited in research/prototypes (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000061) | MIT |
| BHDL | Embedded DSL in Racket; every circuit is a function; layout combinators and Jupyter REPL (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000011) | KiCad-format PCB, BOM, Gerber via export/routing flow (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047) | Strong: first-class circuits, host-language loops/functions, hierarchical composition, series/parallel/bus combinators (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000012) | Autoplacement, legalization, routability-aware placement; verification less mature than mainstream EDA DRC (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047) | Good for research/CI due to text + notebook flow, but ecosystem smaller than Python/KiCad | Research prototype | Open-source research code |
| Polymorphic Blocks | Python-embedded HDL/HCL with types, generators, constraint graph, GUI-assisted refinement (pqac-00000037, pqac-00000039) | Netlist export to KiCad/layout tools (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000043) | Very strong: abstract blocks, subclasses, generators, automated sizing/selection from constraints (pqac-00000038, pqac-00000041) | Strong semantic checking via typed ports/links and constraint propagation, but mostly research-grade (pqac-00000042, pqac-00000044) | Good in principle: textual HDL and deterministic naming aid version control (pqac-00000039) | Research prototype | Open-source research code (pqac-00000039) |
| JITX | Commercial programmatic PCB in Stanza; PCB generators, constraints, and layout automation | Netlist, BOM, placement/routing outputs, manufacturing files incl. Gerber from product flow | Very strong: libraries, generators, algorithmic placement/routing, reusable design patterns | Strong integrated rule/constraint-driven automation; one of the most capable code-native PCB systems | Good technically, but proprietary/runtime licensing complicates open CI compared with FOSS | Commercially mature | Proprietary/commercial |
| PCBmodE | Python + SVG/XML-driven PCB definition; board artwork and geometry as code/data | SVG-centric board descriptions; Gerber/manufacturing output supported through flow | Moderate: parametric geometry/artwork good; electrical abstraction weaker than HDL-style tools | Limited electrical verification compared with mainstream EDA; best for geometry-driven/simple boards | Good for Git because source is text/SVG, but narrower scope and smaller ecosystem | Older niche project; limited recent activity | Open-source (CC-BY-SA/GPL-style project assets/code mix) |
| gEDA/gaf | Traditional Unix-style open EDA suite; text schematics/netlists plus scriptable utilities | Netlist, BOM, Gerber via pcb/gerbv toolchain | Moderate: scriptable and library-based, but less ergonomic parametrization than newer HDL flows | Established ERC/DRC depending on subtool; manufacturing outputs proven | Good: text files and CLI tools suit CI, though workflow feels legacy | Mature but aging | GPL |
| Horizon EDA | GUI-first open-source EDA with structured text-backed project data and some scriptability | Gerber, drill, BOM, pick-and-place, netlist | Moderate: strong part/library reuse; less “full code CAD” than atopile/SKiDL/JITX | Strong native DRC/manufacturing outputs for open-source desktop EDA | Good: project files are Git-manageable and CLI/export automation is possible, though not as code-native as HDL tools | Active and increasingly capable | GPL-3.0 |
| Edea | Python library/tooling for generating or manipulating KiCad project files programmatically | KiCad schematics/PCB data, netlists, and downstream Gerber/BOM through KiCad | Moderate to strong: Python-based templating/manipulation enables reuse around KiCad artifacts | Usually delegates true ERC/DRC to KiCad after generation | Very good: ideal for repository automation, transformations, and CI wrappers around KiCad | Early-stage / tooling layer rather than full EDA | Open-source |
| KiCad Python / `kicad-cli` | Hybrid model: GUI editor plus Python API/action plugins and command-line automation | Native Gerber, drill, IPC netlist, BOM, STEP, position files, PDFs | Moderate: reuse via hierarchical sheets, templates, scripts, plugins, generated footprints/boards | Strong mainstream ERC/DRC and manufacturing checks; CLI-friendly in newer releases | Very good: central choice for open hardware CI/CD; scriptable exports/checks align with HardOps principles (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017) | Very mature, dominant open-source PCB tool | GPL-3.0 |


*Table: This table compares major code-based and scriptable PCB/electronics design approaches across programming model, outputs, verification, reuse, CI fit, maturity, and licensing. It is useful for selecting a reproducible, version-controlled workflow and distinguishing research prototypes from production-capable tools.*

### 2.1 atopile (.ato Language)

Atopile (github.com/atopile/atopile) introduces a custom `.ato` hardware description language compiled by a Python-based toolchain into KiCad project files. The programming model emphasizes textual, declarative circuit descriptions with module imports, parameterized components, and hierarchical composition. Designs are expressed in `.ato` files that compile to KiCad schematics and netlists; Gerber and BOM generation is delegated downstream to KiCad. The text-first nature makes atopile exceptionally Git-friendly, and its package manager supports reusable module libraries. DRC is handled by KiCad after compilation. Licensed under Apache-2.0, atopile is early-stage but represents one of the most complete "design-as-code" visions for electronics, with an active community and growing component ecosystem. Its philosophy aligns closely with the broader vision of board-level HDLs that raise abstraction while preserving fine-grained control (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000054).

### 2.2 tscircuit (React-for-PCB)

tscircuit (github.com/tscircuit/tscircuit) models PCB designs as React/TypeScript component trees, where JSX elements describe resistors, capacitors, ICs, traces, and board outlines. This leverages the entire npm/TypeScript ecosystem for composition, testing, and CI. Circuits are defined declaratively using component props for values and footprints, then rendered to Gerber files, BOMs, netlists, and 3D previews. The React model naturally supports parametric reuse through props, higher-order components, and package publishing. DRC is handled internally with growing capability. Licensed under MIT, tscircuit is rapidly evolving and particularly appealing to web-developer communities, though its electronics library coverage is still growing.

### 2.3 SKiDL

SKiDL (github.com/devbisme/skidl) is a mature Python embedded DSL for schematic capture where designers instantiate `Part` objects, create `Net` connections, and call built-in ERC checks—all in Python (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000061). Gutiérrez et al. noted that SKiDL and PySpice are Python tools focused on PCB design and netlist generation, though with some simulator limitations (pqac-00000061). SKiDL's strength lies in leveraging full Python for loops, functions, classes, and conditional logic to generate parameterized, reusable circuit descriptions. It outputs netlists compatible with KiCad and includes built-in electrical rule checking. The PCBSchemaGen system specifically chose SKiDL as its intermediate representation because "Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning" (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025). SKiDL is MIT-licensed, widely cited in research, and represents the most mature purely code-based schematic capture tool.

### 2.4 BHDL (Board HDL)

BHDL is a declarative embedded DSL implemented in Racket, presented at DAC 2021, where every circuit element—even a resistor—is modeled as a function to enable hierarchical, modular composition (pqac-00000005). BHDL provides concise connection syntax including `net`, `series`, `parallel`, and `bus` forms, plus layout combinators (`hstack`, `vstack`, `rotate`, `at`) inspired by VLSI placement tools (pqac-00000011, pqac-00000012). For layout, BHDL integrates an analytical global placer based on RePlace and a simulated-annealing detailed placer with rotation support, implemented in Julia (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047). The system exports KiCad-format PCB files, Gerber, and BOMs, and supports REPL-style development via a Jupyter kernel (pqac-00000005, pqac-00000006). As a research prototype, BHDL demonstrates the power of functional composition for PCB design but has a smaller ecosystem than Python-based alternatives.

### 2.5 Polymorphic Blocks

Polymorphic Blocks, developed by Lin et al. (2020), is a Python-embedded HDL that extends hierarchical block diagrams with polymorphism, typed ports carrying electrical parameters (voltage, current, thresholds), and generator blocks that programmatically instantiate subcircuits (pqac-00000037, pqac-00000039, pqac-00000042). Ports and links propagate parameters through a directed constraint graph and include assertions for correctness checking; generators can access solved values to automatically size components such as resistors from the E24 series (pqac-00000038, pqac-00000041, pqac-00000044). The compiler lowers designs via refinement and generation steps and exports netlists for KiCad import, with deterministic component naming to support layout updates (pqac-00000039). The project is open-source on GitHub and demonstrates how type systems and constraint propagation can provide meaningful automation for intermediate-level designers (pqac-00000039, pqac-00000050).

### 2.6 JITX (Stanza-Based Programmatic PCB)

JITX (jitx.com) is a commercial programmatic PCB design platform built on the Stanza programming language. Referenced in multiple academic works as JITPCB (pqac-00000049, pqac-00000046), JITX embeds circuit construction primitives in a generator-style programming model, enabling parameterized design families with algorithmic placement, routing, and constraint-driven automation. It produces complete manufacturing outputs including Gerber files, BOMs, and pick-and-place data. JITX's integrated rule and constraint system is among the most capable code-native PCB verification flows. However, its proprietary licensing creates friction for open-hardware workflows, and the requirement to learn Stanza represents a barrier compared with Python-based alternatives (pqac-00000055). JITX is commercially mature and targets professional engineering teams requiring high automation.

### 2.7 PCBmodE

PCBmodE (github.com/boldport/pcbmode) takes a unique approach by defining PCB geometry and artwork through JSON configuration files and SVG graphics. This makes it excellent for artistic or geometry-driven board designs where visual aesthetics matter. It produces Gerber manufacturing files from SVG-based board descriptions. However, its electrical abstraction is weaker than HDL-style tools—it lacks netlist-level schematic capture, DRC, or ERC capabilities. The project has seen limited recent activity but remains interesting as a purely text/data-driven PCB definition approach that is naturally Git-friendly.

### 2.8 gEDA/gaf

The gEDA project provides a traditional Unix-style open-source EDA suite with text-based schematics (gschem), scriptable netlisting utilities, and the PCB layout tool with Gerber export via gerbv. Files are text-based and CLI tools support scripted workflows, making gEDA compatible with version control and CI pipelines. However, the toolchain feels legacy compared with modern alternatives, and parametric design support is less ergonomic than newer HDL-based flows. Licensed under GPL, gEDA remains functional but has largely been superseded by KiCad for new open-hardware projects.

### 2.9 Horizon EDA

Horizon EDA (github.com/horizon-eda/horizon) is a GUI-first open-source EDA tool with strong DRC, manufacturing-output capabilities, and structured text-backed project data. While not a "code-CAD" tool per se, its project files are Git-manageable and it supports some scriptability and CLI-driven export. Horizon EDA excels at part and library management and is licensed under GPL-3.0. It bridges the gap between GUI-first design and version-controllable workflows.

### 2.10 Edea

Edea (github.com/edea-dev/edea) is a Python library for programmatically generating or manipulating KiCad project files. It operates as a tooling layer rather than a full EDA suite, enabling scripts to create, modify, or template KiCad schematics and PCB layouts. This makes it ideal for CI pipeline integration—for example, automated variant generation or BOM manipulation—while delegating ERC/DRC to KiCad itself. Edea is open-source and fits well into workflows where KiCad is the primary design tool but automation is needed around it.

### 2.11 KiCad Python Scripting and kicad-cli

KiCad, the dominant open-source PCB tool (GPL-3.0), offers extensive automation through its Python scripting API, action plugins, and the newer `kicad-cli` command-line interface. The Python API enables programmatic creation and manipulation of schematics, footprints, and board layouts; action plugins extend the GUI with custom functionality (pqac-00000018, pqac-00000019). The `kicad-cli` tool (introduced in KiCad 7+) supports headless export of Gerber files, drill files, BOMs, PDFs, STEP models, and schematic/PCB renderings—making it the key enabler for CI pipelines. KiCad's text-based file formats (`.kicad_sch`, `.kicad_pcb`) are Git-manageable, though diffs can be complex (pqac-00000013). The HardOps framework demonstrated that CI pipelines can automate quality checks, artifact generation, and documentation for open-hardware projects, with KiCad as a central tool (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017).

## 3. LLM-Based and AI/Generative Approaches

Recent research has explored using large language models for text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB generation. The following table summarizes the key systems:

| System | Approach | Intermediate Representation | Verification Method | Benchmark Size | Best Reported Performance | Key Limitations | Year/Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PCBSchemaGen (Zou et al.) | Training-free LLM pipeline for natural-language-to-schematic generation using domain-specific prompting, CoT, ICL, KG-guided constraints, and iterative feedback (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000028) | SKiDL Python code, then KiCad schematic/layout artifacts (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025) | Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in ERC, knowledge-graph pin-role checks, topology verification via subgraph isomorphism/VF2, iterative localized feedback (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000021) | 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks across digital, analog, and power domains (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000027) | Hard-task success reported at 78.1%; easy-task success 93.3%; best model ~23k tokens/task, ~$0.07, ~2.4 min/task (~37× human speed) (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027) | Failures from misunderstanding pin functions and generating incorrect abstract topologies; depends on curated KG/component library (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000021) | 2025 research prototype / open-source benchmark+code (pqac-00000028) |
| HWE-Bench (Qiu et al.) | Execution-driven benchmark for LLM board-level schematic design; prompts models through module decomposition, component allocation, local netlists, and global integration (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033) | Structured plain-text/JSON netlists derived from NL specs and datasheets; converted to SPICE/test representations for checking (pqac-00000033, pqac-00000034) | Dual verification: static rule checking on topology/protocol/pin properties plus dynamic LTspice transient simulation with threshold checks; pin-locking/resource-conflict simulation (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000031, pqac-00000033) | 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains with 2,914 IC datasheets (pqac-00000032, pqac-00000031) | Best overall full-design pass rate only 8.15%; average static pass ~71.84%, dynamic ~58.65% (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030, pqac-00000032) | Major failures in context overload, role confusion, multiplexed-pin conflicts, and poor global physical reasoning; models often pass local/static checks but fail full design correctness (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030) | 2025 benchmark/evaluation framework |
| PCEval (Song et al.) | Benchmark for physical-computing LLM tasks spanning circuit + code generation for Arduino-like projects; evaluates both logical and physical design ability (pqac-00000035) | Logical circuit descriptions and physical circuit layouts with associated code artifacts in simulation (pqac-00000035) | Automated simulation-based validation of logical correctness and physical feasibility, including pin-conflict and extraneous-component checks (pqac-00000035) | Not stated in gathered excerpt; covers multiple project complexities and 13 LLMs (pqac-00000035) | No single percentage cited in gathered excerpt; qualitative result is that models do reasonably on code/logical circuit generation but struggle on breadboard-level physical layout (pqac-00000035) | Weakness on physical wiring/layout correctness despite better performance on code and logical circuits; oriented to physical-computing tasks rather than full PCB CAD (pqac-00000035) | 2025 benchmark |
| General LLM + SKiDL approaches | Use an LLM to synthesize executable Python-based schematic code from text, leveraging SKiDL because it is concise, code-native, and better aligned with LLMs than vendor schematic formats (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000064) | SKiDL Python as the central executable IR; downstream conversion to KiCad schematics/PCB files (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025, pqac-00000063) | Typically combines SKiDL/ERC checks with downstream KiCad generation; stronger systems add datasheet-derived knowledge graphs, topological checks, and iterative feedback loops (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000024, pqac-00000063) | No single standard benchmark; examples include PCBSchemaGen's 23-task suite and related evaluation frameworks like HWE-Bench/PCEval (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000035) | Most convincing evidence comes from PCBSchemaGen rather than generic SKiDL prompting; generic approaches remain highly variable and dataset-constrained (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000064) | Limited by scarce open PCB data, vendor-specific capture formats, package/pin constraints, and insufficient physical verification if using only syntax/ERC or SPICE (pqac-00000023, pqac-00000064) | 2023-2025 emerging pattern rather than a single mature tool (pqac-00000063, pqac-00000064) |


*Table: This table compares the main recent LLM-based text-to-schematic and text-to-PCB research systems, their intermediate representations, verification strategies, benchmark scales, and limitations. It is useful for distinguishing promising constraint-guided approaches from broader benchmarks that reveal current reliability gaps.*

**PCBSchemaGen** (Zou et al.) is a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses LLMs to generate SKiDL Python code from natural-language specifications. It builds a knowledge graph from IC datasheets encoding pin-role semantics (36 role types) and topological constraints, compressing per-device context from ~16k tokens to ~300 tokens (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000025). Multi-stage verification combines SKiDL syntax/ERC checks with topology verification via the VF2 subgraph isomorphism algorithm (pqac-00000024). On a 23-task benchmark spanning digital, analog, and power domains, PCBSchemaGen achieved 93.3% success on easy tasks and 78.1% on hard tasks, with a ~37× speedup over human designers (pqac-00000026, pqac-00000027, pqac-00000028).

**HWE-Bench** (Qiu et al.) provides a more rigorous evaluation with 300 real-world tasks across 8 domains paired with 2,914 IC datasheets. It applies dual verification: static electrical rule checking and dynamic LTspice transient simulation (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000032, pqac-00000033). The results are sobering: even the best model (Claude Sonnet 4.5) achieved only 8.15% overall correct-design pass rate, despite average static pass rates of ~72% (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000032). Key failure modes include context overload with large component lists, MCU pin-multiplexing errors, and inability to maintain global logical consistency (pqac-00000030, pqac-00000031).

**PCEval** (Song et al.) benchmarks LLMs on physical computing tasks combining circuit and code generation for Arduino projects. Models performed reasonably on logical circuit design and code generation but struggled with physical breadboard-level layout, particularly pin-conflict avoidance (pqac-00000035).

These results indicate that while LLM-assisted schematic generation shows promise for constrained subtasks—especially when augmented with knowledge graphs, iterative feedback, and domain-specific verification—full end-to-end autonomous PCB design remains unreliable for production use.

## 4. Trade-offs: GUI-Based EDA vs. Code-Based EDA vs. AI/Generative EDA

**GUI-based EDA** (KiCad, Altium, EAGLE) excels at interactive layout optimization, complex trace routing, and visual inspection. Experienced designers achieve superior placement and routing quality through direct manipulation. However, GUI tools produce binary or semi-structured files that resist meaningful Git diffs, make parametric variant generation manual, and are difficult to drive from CI pipelines without significant scripting effort (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000014).

**Code-based EDA** (atopile, SKiDL, tscircuit, BHDL, JITX) treats electronics design as a programming activity with all attendant benefits: plain-text source control, meaningful diffs and code review, automated testing, parameterized reuse through functions and modules, and deterministic CI-generated outputs (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000013). The trade-off is that placement and routing often require handoff to downstream tools (KiCad, FreeRouting) or are handled by less mature auto-placers (pqac-00000006, pqac-00000047). Lin and Hartmann identified confidence in code-generated hardware as a key challenge: unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly, so community mechanisms for marking subcircuits as "physically fabricated and tested" are important (pqac-00000000, pqac-00000054).

**AI/Generative EDA** offers the most ambitious vision—natural-language to manufactured board—but current capabilities fall short. The HWE-Bench results (8.15% full-design pass rate) demonstrate that LLMs lack the physical intuition and global consistency needed for reliable circuit design (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000030). Targeted applications such as subcircuit suggestion, component selection, or datasheet extraction are more immediately practical (pqac-00000050, pqac-00000053).

## 5. Recommendations for an Open-Hardware, CI-Rendered Modular Dosing Device

For a modular multi-powder dosing device—likely involving motor drivers, load-cell amplifiers, microcontroller modules, and communication interfaces as repeated building blocks—the following architecture is recommended:

**Primary design tool: KiCad + atopile or SKiDL for schematic generation.** atopile's `.ato` files provide the cleanest "design-as-code" experience with native KiCad integration, Git-friendliness, and a growing package ecosystem. SKiDL offers a mature, well-documented alternative with the advantage of full Python ecosystem access and demonstrated compatibility with LLM-assisted generation workflows (pqac-00000022, pqac-00000063). Both produce KiCad-compatible outputs.

**CI pipeline: kicad-cli + GitHub/GitLab CI.** Following HardOps principles (pqac-00000013, pqac-00000015, pqac-00000017), the pipeline should run ERC/DRC checks, generate Gerber/drill files, render board previews (SVG/PDF), produce BOMs, and publish release artifacts on each commit. The `kicad-cli` tool enables all of these operations headlessly. Docker containers with KiCad pre-installed ensure reproducible builds.

**Modular reuse:** The dosing device's repeated channel modules (each with motor driver, load cell, valve control) should be defined as parameterized blocks—either atopile modules or SKiDL generator functions—instantiated N times with channel-specific parameters. Polymorphic Blocks' approach of abstract block types with typed ports and constraint propagation provides a strong conceptual model for this (pqac-00000038, pqac-00000044).

**Edea** can serve as a CI-layer tool for KiCad file manipulation tasks such as variant generation, BOM post-processing, or automated schematic transformations.

**Layout:** For a production device, interactive KiCad layout with replication of channel modules (using hierarchical sheets and "connection room" replication) remains preferable to fully automated placement, which is not yet sufficiently mature in open-source tools for mixed-signal designs (pqac-00000047).

**Licensing alignment:** atopile (Apache-2.0), SKiDL (MIT), KiCad (GPL-3.0), Edea (open-source), and tscircuit (MIT) all support open-hardware distribution. JITX's proprietary model is less suitable for community-driven projects.

## 6. Conclusion

The design-as-code paradigm for electronics has matured substantially since 2020, with tools spanning custom HDLs (atopile), Python eDSLs (SKiDL, Polymorphic Blocks), functional languages (BHDL in Racket), React/TypeScript frameworks (tscircuit), and commercial platforms (JITX). These tools share the vision of raising abstraction through generators, type systems, and reusable libraries while producing version-controllable, CI-amenable artifacts (pqac-00000004, pqac-00000005, pqac-00000037). LLM-based approaches show promise for constrained subtasks but remain unreliable for full-design autonomy (pqac-00000029, pqac-00000028). For an open-hardware modular dosing device, a hybrid workflow combining code-generated schematics (atopile or SKiDL), KiCad-based layout/DRC, and CI automation via `kicad-cli` and HardOps principles offers the best balance of reproducibility, manufacturability, and community accessibility.

contexts.context: The excerpt frames board-level HDLs and programmatic PCB tools as a space that benefits from programming-language ideas such as generator languages and type systems to automate design and encapsulate low-level knowledge in reusable libraries. It highlights a key practical challenge: confidence in code-generated hardware — unlike software, hardware re-spins are costly — so mechanisms to establish trust in community-shared generators and subcircuits are important (for example, labeling subcircuits as “physically fabricated and tested”). The authors note that simulation and static modeling alone are insufficient for modern practice, implying that tooling and processes (community verification, provenance, testing artifacts) are needed alongside programming models. Finally, the field is nascent with many open questions across models, abstractions, and user interfaces, and will benefit from cross-disciplinary work across electrical engineering, programming languages, and HCI. This context informs how tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, and PCBmodE might be compared by their programming models, reuse mechanisms, and approaches to verification and trust.
contexts.question: What are the key programmatic, code-based, and design-as-code tools for PCB and electronics design, including SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, PCBmodE, and their programming models?

snippets: By borrowing programming concepts like generator languages and type systems, board-level HDLs can both increase design efficiency with automation and make device design more accessible by encapsulating low-level knowledge in libraries.

Marking subcircuits “physically fabricated and tested” could be a simple solution grounded in current electronics practice, but additional mechanisms may be needed to establish confidence in generators instead of individual subcircuit instances.
As might be expected, confidence has been a recurring theme in our user studies when discussing community libraries.
While simulation and static circuit modeling approaches can help, neither is anywhere near comprehensive in modern practice.
Yet, as a nascent field, there are many open questions across the map, from models and abstractions to user interfaces.

contexts.context: The excerpt frames board-level HDLs as a programmatic, higher-abstraction approach to PCB/electronics design. It contrasts simple Verilog-like translations with a Chisel-like generator model where code describes construction and sizing methodology (e.g., automatically sizing resistors/values per application). Object-oriented concepts — type hierarchies, inheritance, and parameterized types (voltage, current ratings) — are proposed to let designers instantiate abstract components and either choose or automatically select compatible concrete implementations, and to enable correctness checks. Polymorphic Blocks is given as a concrete, Python-embedded DSL and library-based flow that demonstrates these ideas: top-level blocks are declared in code (example class Blinky(Block)), sub-blocks are instantiated (self.Block(MagicMcu()), self.Block(IndicatorLed())), and connections are made programmatically (self.connect(...)). The paper emphasizes that generators and typing encapsulate specialized knowledge, increase automation, and raise design efficiency and accessibility. This provides a conceptual model relevant to programmatic tools like SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, and others even though those specific tools are not named in the excerpt.

contexts.context: The excerpt situates code-based PCB tools in the lineage of HDLs and hardware construction languages (HCLs). It highlights that recent PCB-focused projects like SKiDL and JITPCB adopt a generator-style programming model by embedding circuit-construction primitives into a general-purpose programming language, enabling parameterized module families and programmatic board assembly. PCB HDLs tend to be structural rather than behavioral, making behavioral abstractions from chip design less applicable. The author argues that adding richer electronics models (parts metadata, substitutable parts, correctness checks) on top of generator-based approaches enables features such as parts selection and automated checks. The text also notes relevant tooling considerations from programming-language research: live programming can provide immediate feedback but must manage latency between edits and results, and techniques used to accelerate chip tool compilation (partitioning, incremental compilation) may also benefit board-level compilation. The excerpt does not specifically describe BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, or PCBmodE, so those tools are not covered here.

snippets: JITPCB [7] and SKiDL [73] brings generators to the PCB space by embedding circuit construction primitives in a general purpose programming language.
Hardware construction languages (HCLs) that support generators are an evolution on the basic HDL.
One implementation strategy, as taken by Chisel, is providing hardware construction primitives (for example, instantiate module and connect ports) embedded in a general purpose programming language.
Unlike chip HDLs, PCB HDLs are purely structural, as it is unclear what behavioral abstractions can suit the wide space of PCB electronics.
this dissertation builds on top of that core idea, but augments it with more detailed electronics modeling to enable design support features like parts selection and correctness checks.
Live programming tools continuously run code as it is written by the user, and display the results to provide immediate feedback.
One critical aspect of live programming systems is the latency between code change and visible effects [66].
While board-level designs are orders of magnitude less complex than chip designs, similar techniques can also speed up board-level compilation.

contexts.context: The excerpt situates programmatic PCB/design-as-code tools within the broader HDL/HCL landscape. Chip HDLs (e.g., Verilog, VHDL) combine structural and behavioral descriptions, and analog/mixed-signal variants (Verilog-AMS, VHDL-AMS) exist but are not full design/synthesis languages. Hardware construction languages (HCLs) extend HDLs by providing generator constructs to produce families of modules from high-level parameters. For PCB work, traditional PCB HDLs tend to be purely structural; however, recent tools such as JITPCB and SKiDL introduce generator-style programming by embedding circuit-construction primitives in general-purpose languages, enabling programmatic, parameterized board creation. The dissertation referenced builds on these ideas and adds richer electronics modeling to support parts selection and correctness checks. The excerpt also highlights live programming concepts — continuously running code and low-latency feedback — as important for interactive textual design workflows. The excerpt does not explicitly describe BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, or PCBmodE, so details on those specific tools are absent.
contexts.question: What are the key programmatic, code-based, and design-as-code tools for PCB and electronics design, including SKiDL, BHDL, JITX, atopile, tscircuit, PCBmodE, and their programming models?

snippets: JITPCB [7] and SKiDL [73] brings generators to the PCB space by embedding circuit construction primitives in a general purpose programming language.
Unlike chip HDLs, PCB HDLs are purely structural, as it is unclear what behavioral abstractions can suit the wide space of PCB electronics.
Hardware construction languages (HCLs) that support generators are an evolution on the basic HDL.
Verilog-AMS [29] and VHDL-AMS [15] provide analog and mixed-signal extensions on top of their base digital languages.
Live programming tools continuously run code as it is written by the user, and display the results to provide immediate feedback.
One critical aspect of live programming systems is the latency between code change and visible effects [66].
this dissertation builds on top of that core idea, but augments it with more detailed electronics modeling to enable design support features like parts selection and correctness checks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a hardware construction language (HCL) approach called Polymorphic Circuit Blocks that targets system-architecture-level design using block diagrams. Key features include support for high-level blocks defined as reusable subcircuit libraries, generators (executable code) that translate high-level specifications into implementations (e.g., sizing components from parameters), and explicit support for ambiguity and polymorphism (generic parts and subtype substitution). This block-based model separates concerns: system designers work at the architecture level while experts create reusable generator libraries. Designs written in HCL are elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph model, expanded via community libraries, and can be interactively refined through a GUI to reach a layout-ready circuit. The resulting design can be exported as a netlist for import into PCB layout tools. The authors also envision an open-source community around such libraries and generators to lower barriers and enable reuse and exploration.

contexts.context: The excerpt discusses board-level hardware description languages (HDLs) as a way to raise abstraction and automation in PCB design. Key language features highlighted are generators (a Chisel-like, programmatic approach) that encapsulate methodology as code and can automatically size components per application, and programming-language concepts from OOP such as type hierarchies and inheritance to allow abstract component instantiation and automated selection. Types can include parameters (e.g., voltage and current ratings) to enable correctness checks. These features aim to increase reuse, automation, and accessibility by encapsulating specialist knowledge in higher-level interfaces. The paper cites Polymorphic Blocks as a recent board-level HDL that implements these ideas in a library-based flow and notes it is implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language. The example HDL shows hierarchical block definitions (e.g., class Blinky(Block): with sub-block instantiation and explicit connections) and an elaboration/export pipeline (HDL → netlist → PCB). The Python embedding implies straightforward integration with Python tooling ecosystems (interactive use, scripting, and potential notebook integration), and the modular block/generator model supports reusable modular circuit functions and automated layout/export steps.

contexts.context: The excerpt is from Li et al.'s paper introducing BHDL, described as "a lucid, expressive, and embedded programming language and system for PCB designs." It explicitly shows that BHDL provides a Jupyter development environment (Fig. 11) that displays example code, layout renderings, BOMs, and can produce KiCAD-format PCB files. The paper situates BHDL among other PCB/programmatic-design tools (e.g., SKiDL, PCBDL, JITX) and references placement approaches (RePlace) and placement-quality tradeoffs: "Tuning the density penalty of RePlace formulation is tricky to achieve" and overly dense placements can "place components too close to each other, reducing the routability." The authors also note potential directions to "incorporate deep learning and reinforcement learning automate or optimize tasks in PCB designs." These points indicate BHDL targets interactive, code-driven PCB layout generation within Jupyter, produces outputs compatible with common PCB toolchains, and is aware of placement/routability challenges and opportunities for ML-driven automation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a hardware description language (HCL) that models circuit blocks with a class hierarchy enabling abstract, polymorphic blocks and delayed refinement. Ports and links carry electrical parameters (e.g., source voltage/current limits, sink current draw, digital thresholds, analog impedances) and links aggregate these parameters and define assertions to check compatibility. An electronics layer defines common link/port types (power, digital, analog, multi-wire bundles) and a library of primitives and higher-level blocks (e.g., resistor generator using E24, decoupling capacitors, RC filter that computes R/C from cutoff and impedance). The system supports programmatic block construction via a generator HCL interface, translating high-level inputs into internal parameters to produce families of subcircuits. Example code (class Blinky(Block)) shows block instantiation and explicit connections between sub-block ports. The design aims to let novices compose system-level designs from libraries while experts build the reusable blocks and port/link models. Parameter propagation and automated checks are used to catch compatibility issues when refining abstract blocks.

contexts.context: BHDL (Board HDL) is presented as a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive embedded DSL implemented in Racket. It models every circuit element, including discrete parts, as functions to enable hierarchical, modular composition of larger designs from smaller components. BHDL offers concise connection syntax (net) and higher-level connection forms — series, parallel, and bus — to express wiring succinctly. For layout co-design, BHDL supplies relative layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) and adapts VLSI auto-placers to place free parts. The system supports an interactive REPL-style development model and is provided with a Jupyter notebook environment/kernal for incremental coding and visualization. The toolchain generates placed PCB boards which can be exported and routed via external tools (e.g., FreeRouting) to produce manufacturable PCBs. As an eDSL, BHDL also allows leveraging full host-language features (functions, loops, libraries) for programmability, parameterization, and extension of PCB designs.

snippets: BHDL (standing for Board HDL), a declarative, simple, modular, and expressive HDL and system, in this paper.
BHDL is designed as an embedded Domain Speciﬁc Language (eDSL) inside the modern and highly-regarded programming language Racket [10]–[12].
Treating all circuits, even a resistor, as functions, BHDL naturally supports modularized development that builds a complex design up from smaller designs hierarchically.
BHDL provides a simple and intuitive syntax for expressing circuit connections naturally.
In addition to the net syntax for expressing netlist, BHDL also provides the series, parallel, and bus syntax for making series/parallel wires and to connect a bus of pins.
BHDL provides simple and relative layout combinators namely hstack, vstack, rotate and at.
The development model of BHDL is interactive and incremental, follows the Read-Eval-Print Loop (REPL) [15]
BHDL is shipped with the Jupyter [16] notebook environment
Our framework generates placed PCB board. The board can then be routed using the open-source FreeRouting [17] tool to produce the ﬁnal PCB for manufacturing.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a hardware-description approach that emphasizes polymorphic, hierarchical blocks and library-driven generators. A class hierarchy lets designers instantiate abstract blocks (e.g., a generic LED or step-down converter) and later refine them to concrete subclasses; superclasses provide higher-level interfaces while subclasses implement specifics. Ports and links carry physical parameters (voltage, current, thresholds), with parameters "flow[ing]" through connected ports to links; links aggregate parameters and define assertions to check correctness. The system includes block constraints for automated compatibility checks. A library of common blocks and generators is provided (e.g., an E24 resistor generator, passive part generators, RC filter and resistive-divider calculators, and application circuits like microcontrollers and protocol converters), enabling reuse and automation. A programmatic generator HCL interface is used to author blocks: it captures design methodology, separates interface from implementation, and translates high-level inputs into internal parameters. Example code (a Blinky block) shows instantiation of sub-blocks and explicit connect calls. The excerpt does not mention PCB layout generation, placement/routing, or a Jupyter kernel integration, so those topics are not covered here.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL as a declarative, modular HDL and system for PCB design that integrates footprint management, layout generation, and interactive development. Footprints can be imported (including from Internet URLs) and renamed; BHDL currently supports the KiCAD-format footprint library and allows multiple footprints per part with defaults. The language provides extended pin syntax, placeholders for unused pins, and prefixing for part naming. For layout generation, BHDL includes an auto-placer: an analytical global placer based on RePlace (optimizing HPWL) and a simulated-annealing detailed placer that adds rotation capability by "introduc[ing] a third dimension to rotate the components by random angles" to resolve dense placements. Implementation-wise, the system is implemented in Racket with the auto-placer in Julia, follows a REPL-driven incremental development model, and uses Jupyter notebooks as the preferred development environment for interactive editing, visualization, and exporting to KiCAD PCB format, Gerber, and BOM. The system also supports programmatic hierarchical co-design and aims to enable systematic, code-based PCB development.
