contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL's connection primitives and layout facilities relevant to modular PCB design. Circuits can be called as functions and their arguments must match the pin clause, enabling modular circuit functions and signal wiring at invocation. BHDL offers multiple wire-connection syntaxes: net (list of pins forming a netlist), series (connect left/right pins of listed parts in sequence), parallel (connect components in parallel), and bus (connect corresponding pins across entities). The bus syntax supports variants and uses underscores as placeholders for missing pins; examples show use for bridging Arduino form factors. Nesting and mixing of series/parallel/net allow concise hierarchical connectivity. BHDL also supports in-place anonymous part creation (termed “lambda circuits”), avoiding the need to predefine components. For layout generation, BHDL provides relative, hierarchical layout combinators inspired by JITPCB and Pict: hstack, vstack, rotate, and at, enabling sub-circuits to be laid out and then composed into larger layouts with specified relative positions rather than absolute coordinates. The excerpt does not mention a Jupyter kernel integration.

parallel: The parallel syntax is used to specify parts   connected in  parallel, such as the two resistors in Fig. 4c.
bus: The bus syntax is used to connect corresponding pins of two or more entities such as the MOSI, MISO, and CLK pins of two SPI ports in Fig. 4d.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes an HCL-based approach ("Polymorphic Circuit Blocks") for high-level PCB/system design. The language supports system-architecture-level block diagrams and reusable subcircuit libraries, and it introduces generators—executable code that translate high-level specifications into concrete implementations (e.g., sizing a resistor from input parameters). The model allows ambiguity (generic parts) and block-level polymorphism so abstract blocks can be refined or substituted with compatible specific implementations. Designs written in the HCL are elaborated into a hierarchical block-graph, expanded via community libraries, and then interactively refined in a GUI where propagated parameters are checked for correctness. The workflow culminates in exporting a netlist that can be imported into conventional board-layout tools. The authors implemented this end-to-end system as Polymorphic Circuit Blocks and anticipate an open-source community to share libraries and lower barriers to electronics design. The text notes mockups were used initially and that full implementation and evaluation follow in later chapters.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL's layout and embedding features relevant to modular PCB design and layout generation. BHDL provides layout combinators (hstack, vstack, rotate, at) with alignment and spacing options for hierarchical, functional composition of layouts; an example stacks three buttons vertically next to an ESP-01. The language supports concise in-place part creation combined with series/parallel/bus syntax, enabling reuse-free, template-style construction of repeated structures. BHDL circuits are first-class values in the host language: they can be bound to variables, passed to functions, and used in expressions. Host-language constructs (functions, conditionals, loops) can be used inside BHDL clauses (part, wire, layout), demonstrated by a parameterized TripleButton example that generates two versions via a mode parameter and a for-loop to create repeated parts. For physical mapping, BHDL includes a footprint clause and supports importing KiCad footprint files, e.g., for resistors. Placement control features include a padding parameter to control component density and a simulated-annealing detailed placer for placement refinement. The excerpt does not mention a Jupyter kernel.

contexts.context: The paper introduces "HardOps": applying software DevOps toolchains to open hardware development. It argues that distributed version control (e.g., Git) and Git-based platforms (GitHub, GitLab, CERN's Open Hardware Repository) are better suited than centralized PDM for open, collaborative hardware projects because they give contributors control of copies, branches, and a rich design history. HardOps adapts DevOps practices — version control, project management, and automated workflows — to capture and control design data and to automate computational tasks needed for design and manufacturing. The authors present the OpenFlexure Microscope as a case study of implementing HardOps. The paper also notes the growing automation trend in DevOps (automated tests, deployments, releases) and the maturing legal/standards context for open hardware (e.g., CERN open hardware license, DIN definition). While the excerpt outlines the conceptual fit between DevOps and hardware and highlights tooling choices and benefits, it does not provide detailed, tool-level examples (e.g., specific CI jobs for KiCad) in these pages.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

snippets: HardOps: utilising the software development toolchain for hardware design
Distributed version control for software is often coupled with ‘Developer Operations’ or DevOps tools to automate critical processes and facilitate communication.
This paper explores how DevOps workflows can be adapted for open development of hardware.
An example of how DevOps for hardware (HardOps) can be implemented is provided by the OpenFlexure Microscope project.
Distributed version control systems such as Git allow distributed teams to work on their own branch of development
Git-based developer operations (DevOps) platforms such as GitHub and GitLab are seeing an increasing number of hardware projects
HardOps utilises DevOps toolchains to capture and control the design process, and to automate computational tasks necessary for design and manufacturing.

Traditionally, Product Data Management (PDM) systems are used to store these data. However, the centralisation of PDM provides problems for open development as one entity has exclusive control of the data and of access to this data.
This shift has lead to an increase in automation by software teams to run tests, deploy and to release code continuously.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a HardOps-style CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware (OpenFlexure Microscope) that automates CAD/assembly outputs and documentation. The pipeline uses open-source tools (Python, OpenSCAD, Inkscape, ImageMagick, etc.) run inside Ubuntu Docker images to render illustrations and auto-generate STL files, assembly instructions, and bills of materials whenever the repository is updated. Prepare-and-verify CI pipelines create digital production assets, enabling production and quality teams to trial parts, record QA/QC, and feed results back into the DVCS issue/merge-request workflow. The process links issues, merge requests, and commits so design changes and production feedback remain auditable; problems discovered in distributed production are tracked as issues tied to the relevant merge request or commit. The paper also notes potential PLM integrations (automated BOM passing, manufacturing/QA tracking) are desirable but not yet implemented, and highlights that automation complements but does not replace quality personnel. The excerpt is directly relevant to version control and CI/CD for hardware pipelines but does not provide KiCad-specific automation details.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

contexts.context: The excerpt is a bibliography listing practical tools, libraries, and research relevant to automating and integrating PCB design into DevOps workflows. It includes canonical KiCad references and a GitHub collection of KiCad action plugins (useful for scripting and CI tasks), plus component library sources (Ultra Librarian) for automating part lookup. Several entries cover HDL or programmatic PCB capture (a custom-built HDL, and work on "Circuit Board Level Hardware Description Languages") and interactive approaches that bridge code and schematics ("Polymorphic Blocks", "Weaving Schematics and Code"), which are directly relevant to source-controlled, code-driven hardware descriptions. There are also tooling/UX research projects (AutoFritz, VirtualComponent) and practical layout techniques ("connection room" replication) that inform automation and repeatable layouts. Commercial EDA references (Mentor Xpedition) and historical automation work are listed as well. These citations point to repositories, plugin collections, HDL approaches, and component/part automation resources that could be used when designing version control strategies, CI/CD pipelines, and KiCad automation for open-source hardware projects.

It explicitly references KiCad and a KiCad Action Plugins repository, indicating available automation/extension points for KiCad. Multiple entries by Richard Lin and collaborators address hardware description languages, higher-level abstractions, polymorphic blocks, and interactive weaving of schematics and code—papers that discuss unifying high-level specification with low-level PCB control and tools beyond traditional schematic capture. There are also references to prototyping and autocomplete tools (AutoFritz, Fritzing) and commercial tooling/resources such as Mentor’s Xpedition materials on reusable circuit blocks and schematic analysis, plus component library services (Ultra Librarian). Older works on automated PCB design and interactive routing are cited as well. Notably, the excerpt does not explicitly mention version control systems, CI/CD pipelines, or DevOps practices for hardware, but the cited papers and tools (KiCad plugins, HDL-like approaches, reusable blocks, and component libraries) suggest points where automation and integration could be applied in an open-source hardware DevOps workflow.

contexts.context: The excerpt is a bibliography listing EDA tools, automation projects, and research papers relevant to automated and user-focused hardware design. It references major commercial and open EDA tools (Altium Designer, EAGLE, KiCad, Allegro PCB Designer) and supporting libraries/frameworks (Eclipse Layout Kernel). Several entries point to automation and programmatic PCB generation or functional EDA platforms (JITPCB, EDASolver, circuito.io, Gumstix Geppetto, uConfig). Research papers and project titles describe interfaces and tooling for integrating PCBs with software tooling and automated checking (BoardLab, The Toastboard), mixed-reality and learning platforms for electronics (VirtualComponent, LightUp), and generative/design-assist approaches for novices (Trigger-Action-Circuits). There are also references to simulation and design tools that can be integrated into test flows (WEBENCH Power Designer, LabVIEW) and hardware description/synthesis frameworks (Verilog-AMS, VHDL-AMS, BAG). Overall, the bibliography provides a set of concrete tools, libraries, and prior work that could be leveraged when building version-controlled workflows, automation scripts, and CI/CD processes around KiCad and open-source hardware, though it does not itself describe CI/CD procedures.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HardOps as a decentralised alternative for hardware design using distributed version control (DVCS) and DevOps platforms. Users keep full local copies of designs and history, synchronise via SSH, and have cryptographically hashed records for traceability. Collaboration works by fetching and selectively merging changes; merge requests can be gated by review/sign-off. HardOps supports automation pipelines (e.g., clash analysis) but these rely on tools providing command-line interfaces so they can be scripted. Many CAD packages lack robust CLI and proprietary licensing can complicate automation (sequential jobs or multiple licenses may be required). The authors note that most CAD functionality can be automated via APIs and that command-line wrappers could be written, but significant work is needed to generalise HardOps beyond OpenSCAD and to scale from prototyping to production. They also emphasise that production-related structured data (quality control, production records) may be better handled in databases linked to the DVCS-managed design data, and that project-management federation between servers remains an open challenge.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

snippets: HardOps provides a true alternative.
Users on an internal server can still collaborate with users on other servers via the DVCS.
Each organisation, and indeed each worker at the organisation, maintains their own copy of the full design and its history.
As all parties have a cryptography hashed record of all modifications and their authors, the source of any design changes are traceable.
Automation pipelines could be set up to automatically detect certain problemsfor example, in the above example running clash analysis would identify a problem.
HardOps automation pipelines rely on programs providing a command line interface so their functionality can be accessed by scripting.
For proprietary CAD packages licencing may be problematic requiring either sequential execution of jobs or multiple licenses.
Also many CAD packages lack effective command-line execution increasing the complexity of creating these pipelines.
However, the functionality of most CAD packages can often be automated via software APIs, command-line software could be written to access these APIs.
HardOps has only been implemented for prototyping and very limited production runs.
DVCS is appropriate for managing the design and documentation of a product, and HardOps builds on this to provide automation and project management.
The key difference between the proposed HardOps paradigm and the more traditional PDM/PLM is that decentralisation is core to the model.

contexts.context: The paper describes an approach that pairs a high-level hierarchy/block abstraction with an HDL and compiler to automate board design: "we implemented an HDL and compiler ... capable of providing meaningful design automation." Designers compose systems from high-level blocks while experts implement those blocks as reusable generators. Generated blocks and the broader design model can be exposed within a graphical, schematic-like interface, suggesting hybrid text/GUI workflows. The authors emphasize a library-based approach but note libraries are incomplete: "our current library includes many common parts and subcircuits, it is far from complete." Complex part data is often trapped in PDFs and the authors suggest using tools like Tabula and DocParser to extract parametric data. The foundational abstractions (hierarchy blocks, links, parameters) were useful, but the electronics model has limitations (few signal interfaces, no multiple-ground support). KiCad is listed among referenced EDA tools. The paper also notes the need for better integration between HDL and block diagrams, quality indicators for reused parts, and a more polished/scalable system and libraries—points relevant to versioning, CI/CD, and automation for open-source hardware workflows.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

contexts.context: The excerpt describes applying DevOps/DVCS and CI/CD practices to open-source hardware (HardOps). Key DVCS benefits include cryptographically signed commits, distributed branches, and merge requests with unique IDs, threaded discussion and issue links to control protected branches. A major challenge is CAD files: many are complex binary formats making diffs and merges difficult; even text-based CAD (e.g. KiCad.kicad_pcb) often needs specialist tools. Mitigations include centralised file locking, Git extensions, and external diff/merge tools, but the preferred route is CAD vendors providing comparison/merge functionality. OpenFlexure leverages OpenSCAD (CAD-as-code) enabling parametric designs and programmatic testing: unit tests, static analysis, and CI jobs generate STLs, renderings, documentation, and BOMs reproducibly. CI pipelines on DevOps platforms run automated quality checks, build artifacts, deploy websites, and can execute on external infrastructure for resource-intensive tasks or licensed software. Scheduled jobs and bots help manage issues and keep assets in sync, reducing manual overhead and ensuring a single source of truth for production files.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

In the case of the OpenFlexure project, all designs are created in the constructive solid geometry language OpenSCAD, and as such the design itself is computer code.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HardOps as a DevOps-based approach for hardware design that centralises data and standardises workflows, lowering collaboration barriers and enabling automation for SMEs via open, hosted DevOps platforms. While cost savings are noted, scaling to larger projects introduces expenses for compute infrastructure and bespoke integrations. A major limitation is usability: interfaces inherited from software DevOps include many features irrelevant to hardware, lack previews for hardware files, and rely on DVCS tooling (e.g., Git) that is primarily command-line and requires programming skills. The authors argue that, if HardOps is paired with a standardized CAD package interface, it could deliver many PLM-like benefits to distributed teams and organizations that cannot afford traditional PLM systems. Overall, the passage highlights opportunities for automation and cross-team information flow, but flags practical barriers—infrastructure costs at scale and poor user experience with existing DVCS/DevOps tools—that would affect adoption of version control, CI/CD-style automation, and CAD (e.g., KiCad) integrations for open-source hardware workflows.
contexts.question: HardOps DevOps for hardware design, version control, CI/CD pipeline for open-source hardware, KiCad automation

snippets: As open source and free-to-use hosted DevOps    platforms exist, this opens up key automation to SMEs and start-ups that are priced out of PDM/PLM.
However, cost-saving  is a secondary benefit.
As   HardOps scales to larger more complex projects, costs will arise for dedicated computing infrastructure for automation and the development of company specific integrations.

Despite the decentralised nature of HardOps, it still brings the core benefit of a centralised system – joined    up, standardised workflows and efficient flow of infor­ mation between different teams.
one key area where HardOps falls down is on the usability of the interface.
As HardOps is built upon DevOps plat­forms, numerous features in the interface are not rele­vant to hardware, and previews for many hardware    files are also lacking.
However, the biggest barrier to HardOps adoption is the software used to interact with the DVCS.
As DVCS, such as Git, have been designed for software development, the standard tools are complex command-line applications that require programming skills to operate.

If bolstered by a standardised interface to CAD packages, HardOps has the potential to bring many of the benefits of PLM to both distributed design teams, and to organisa­tions that are too small to invest in costly PLM.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes HardOps as applying DevOps practices and toolchains to hardware development, using the OpenFlexure Project as a worked example. HardOps presents a six-stage workflow (Plan; Design and Document; Prepare and Verify; Distributed Production; Physical Testing; Feedback) and emphasizes using distributed version control systems (DVCS) alongside project management and automation tools. Task automation is organised into CI pipelines — server-run scripts for static analysis, building and testing — with GitLab given as an example platform. The text contrasts HardOps with traditional PLM/PDM approaches, highlighting that DevOps/HardOps are distributed (each user keeps a full copy and history) rather than centralised. It also notes that certain types of design (gateware: FPGAs and ASICs, written in HDLs) are more naturally amenable to source-control and automation because they behave more like software. The excerpt does not explicitly mention KiCad or PCB-specific automation, but the described principles (DVCS, CI pipelines, reproducible builds/tests, and distributed collaboration) are directly applicable to automating KiCad workflows for open-source hardware projects.

snippets: This paper explores the workflow developed for the OpenFlexure Project to demonstrate how HardOps can be implemented in practice.
The HardOps workflow is presented in six stages: Plan, Design and Document, Prepare and Verify, Distributed Production, Physical Testing, and Feedback.
DevOps toolchains generally include version control software, project management tools, and tools to automate computational tasks in a reproducible manner.
Task automation  is organised into ‘Continuous Integration’ (CI) pipelines.
A CI pipeline is a set of scripts that are run on a server that perform different functions such as code analysis, compiling executable programs, and testing the software functionality.
Due to the public availability of open-source hardware designs, they are ideal for demonstrating the HardOps workflow.
HardOps is a paradigm which unlocks the huge benefit of DVCS for hardware development.
FPGAs and ASICs are ‘gateware’ sitting somewhere between firmware and hardware.
Gateware is generally written in hardware description languages (HDLs) that can be simulated and are compiled to binary bitstreams that configure the FPGAs.
While full consideration of these aspects is important for efficiency and ease of use, there is one key and fundamental difference between PLM and DevOps: centralisation.
Figure 1. A graphical representation of a CI pipeline produced by the DevOps platform GitLab.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a workflow for LLM-driven schematic generation that is guided by a domain knowledge graph (KG) encoding pin-role semantics and electrical constraints. Components in kg component.json receive normalized pin roles derived from automated datasheet extraction and expert validation (example: MGJ2D pin mappings). The KG defines four formal constraint types (e.g., supply pair, must be connected, driving pair, differential pair) used during fast-fail verification to catch topology and net-assignment errors. Data sources include aggregated manufacturer datasheets parsed by scripts; semantic normalization maps vendor-specific pin labels into a unified ontology of 36 pin-role semantics. Role-based verification enables generic checks (e.g., VDD and GND must be on distinct nets). The system supports multi-level feedback (Full/Weak/None) for iterative correction; Full, with pin-level localization, significantly improves pass rates. A custom KiCad component library (symbols, footprints, datasheets) is provided for SKiDL integration, enabling standardized component defs and accurate pin mappings for synthesis and downstream PCB workflows.

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen targets text-to-PCB (text-to-schematic) by using SKiDL as the executable intermediate: SKiDL’s Python basis helps LLMs map functional requirements to package info and pin constraints. The authors build a domain-specific Knowledge Graph (KG) from IC datasheets to encode component types, pin-role semantics (R includes 36 pin role types), electrical properties, topological constraints and isolation boundaries; they formally define KG as KG = (C, R, A, Φ, I). The KG dramatically reduces context needs by compressing datasheet content — stated as a reduction from ~16k tokens per IC to ~300 tokens (30–70x less). Prompt engineering techniques include in-context learning (ICL) with multiple design examples and Chain-of-Thought (CoT) to guide stepwise reasoning about connectivity and pin roles. Generation is followed by a multi-stage verification pipeline (Figure 2): Python/SKiDL syntax checks, built-in Electrical Rule Checking (ERC), and topology verification. The verification algorithm builds bipartite and topology graphs (BuildTopologyGraph(σgen, KG)), trims to critical subgraphs, and performs inter- and intra-connection checks to catch violations (e.g., VCC–GND shorts), combining verification KG and design rules.

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen proposes a training-free LLM-driven pipeline for PCB schematic generation that uses an LLM as a SKiDL code proposal generator with domain-specific prompting, Chain-of-Thought, and In-Context Learning to synthesize executable design code without fine-tuning. To enforce physical correctness, the system builds an IC-domain knowledge graph (KG) from real-world datasheets capturing pin-role semantics, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints, and couples this KG with SI-driven multi-phase verification to produce interpretable error messages and iterative feedback. The authors curated a benchmark of 23 real-world circuits across digital, analog, and power domains to evaluate PCB generative ability and demonstrated end-to-end capability from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB layout files accounting for real-world IC packages. The paper highlights challenges specific to PCB schematic generation — vendor-specific schematic capture, scarce board-level datasets, and NP-hard multi-component/ routing/placement decisions — and positions PCBSchemaGen as addressing gaps left by prior work that focused mainly on IC-level digital/analog generation and lacked modeling of board-level packaging and pin constraints.

snippets: Training-free code generation paradigm: We em- ploy an LLM as a proposal generator for SKiDL code.
With domain-specific prompting, Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and In-context Learning (ICL) (Dong et al., 2023a),it can directly synthesize executable design code without fine-tuning.
KG & SI driven multi-phase verifier: We construct a domain knowledge graph from open source real-world IC datasheets with pin-role semantic, multiple con- straint types and topological constraints.
A KG and SI based driven verification framework provides specific feedback and interpretable error messages.
PCB schematic tasks Benchmarking: To our knowl- edge, we curated a first benchmark for evaluate the PCB generative ability of LLMs.
This benchmark com- prises 23 unique circuits that span digital, analog, and power domains, in which each task is associated with real-world IC components.
We demonstrated PCB- SchemaGen provided end-to-end capability from natu- ral language specification to KiCAD PCB layout file ready for following task, with real world IC packages.
PCB schematic capture is vendor specific, this makes large scale training difficult.
In this work, PCBSchemaGen constructs an IC-domain datasheet-driven KG that anchors LLM generation in real- world physical IC constraints.
PCB schematic generation under real IC package and pin constraints remains largely unaddressed.

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen frames text-to-PCB schematic generation as constraint-guided synthesis with multi-stage verification. Circuits are represented as bipartite graphs (components and nets), and topology verification is cast as a subgraph isomorphism problem; the paper uses the VF2 algorithm for efficient checking. Verification combines knowledge-graph (KG) information and design rules to detect pin/net and ERC violations, and the pipeline emits interpretable, localized error messages that are fed back to the LLM. The system supports three feedback message styles (concise, binary, and detailed with syntax/ERC/subgraph errors and precise pin/net/passive value info). It also implements subcircuit reuse via a library of validated modules to improve hierarchical/complex designs; ablation studies show retrieval helps success rates. The authors built a benchmark of 23 real-world PCB schematic tasks and a component library based on IC datasheets, intentionally including similar-package components to test LLM discrimination. Experiments evaluate various LLMs using prompt strategies (ICL and CoT) alongside the multi-stage verification, feedback loop, and subcircuit reuse, reporting main results and human expert validation.

contexts.context: The excerpt identifies PCBSchemaGen as an LLM-based system for constraint-guided schematic design for printed circuit boards (PCBs) (Zou et al., arXiv, Jan 2602). It situates this work relative to recent text-to-PCB and LLM-for-PCB efforts: LayoutCopilot focuses on interactive LLM agents for PCB layout but does not handle schematic generation, and PCBRouting concentrates on routing rather than end-to-end schematic synthesis. A cited reference (Wang et al., 2025) explored LLM understanding of chip packages but did not deliver end-to-end generative capability. The excerpt also highlights a limitation of many prior generative methods: they “mainly employ a one-pass strategy” and “lack closed-loop verification” (Chen et al., 2023), motivating a constraint-guided or iterative approach. Mentions of SKiDL Python Code and concrete schematic labels and components (e.g., TPS54302, 10uH, 22uF, 22.1k, 100k, GND) indicate the paper engages with realistic schematic elements and tool integration. contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-based pipeline that translates natural-language PCB schematic specifications into SKiDL code and ultimately KiCAD schematics/layouts. The system favors SKiDL because Python-based hardware-description languages have high information density and match LLM fine-tuning. A careful prompt-design methodology is used to improve comprehension, connectivity awareness, and adaptability. To inject IC-level physical constraints without flooding the model, PCBSchemaGen encodes essential device rules from datasheets into a compact Knowledge Graph (KG), shrinking per-device token usage from ~16k to ~300 tokens. Generation accuracy is enforced via a multi-stage verification framework combining the KG, electrical design rules, and Subgraph Isomorphism topology checks; verification feedback is returned to the LLM for iterative refinement. The design also includes semantic pin-role labeling (mapping pins to one of 36 roles) and reuse of validated subcircuits. The pipeline steps: user NL input -> agent reads KG and datasheet-derived rules -> agent generates SKiDL -> KiCAD schematic and layout generation.

snippets: PCBSchemaGen is an LLM-based framework for translat- ing natural language PCB schematic deign specification to LLM to generate SKiDL code
We proposed a sophisticated prompt design methodology to enhance the LLM’s comprehension proficiency, circuit connectivity awareness, and learning adaptability.

To improve generation accuracy, we proposed a multi-stage verification framework based on IC datasheet driven Knowledge Graph (KG), electrical design rules, and Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) checking for topology verification.
The multi-stage verification feedback provides the failure information to the LLM for iterative refinement.
However, We select SKiDL as the target representation because Python-based hardware description languages offer the highest information den- sity.

We built KG by encoding essential device rules and con- straints in a structured form, reducing the token footprint per IC component from approximately 16k tokens (typical datasheet length) to average 300 token, achieving a 30 to 70 times reduction in context usage while preserving func- tional and electrical constraints.
Pin Roles & Constraints Set up: We define a semantic la- beling function ρ : Pc →R for each component type c ∈C, mapping its pins to one of 36 roles.
The agent generates SKiDL code.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, an LLM-based, constraint-guided framework for generating PCB schematics. The paper reports substantially higher success rates than prior baselines (a GNN and an earlier LLM) when aligned by component/pin counts. Table 3 breaks performance down by task difficulty: Easy (<10 pins) shows 93.3% for PCBSchemaGen versus 53.0% and 80% for baselines; Medium (10–50 pins) 92.7%; Hard (>100 pins) 78.1%. The authors emphasize that tasks require device-level knowledge retrieval/integration, strong abstraction over circuit connectivity, spatial reasoning, and circuit-level conceptualization. Error analysis highlights two dominant failure modes: (i) misunderstanding specific pin functions, and (ii) generating meaningless connections from an inability to synthesize abstract circuit topology. The paper uses representative tasks (e.g., a differential-to-single-end op-amp buffer and a SiC MOSFET half-bridge) to illustrate successes and failures. Among models evaluated, Gemini 3 is noted as the best performer, possibly due to stronger multimodal capabilities aiding topology understanding. The work positions PCBSchemaGen as advancing text-to-PCB schematic synthesis with constraint guidance and concrete performance gains over prior approaches.

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen evaluates constraint-guided, LLM-based schematic generation for PCB design. The authors test a wide set of LLMs (DeepSeek-V3.2, Llama 3/4, MiniMax M2.1, Qwen 3 Max, GPT-OSS-120B, GPT-5.1, Gemini 3 series) with temperature 0.5 and include a fine-tuned GPT-4o-mini ablation. The evaluation targets pin-function understanding, knowledge-graph and topology constraints, topology generation, and mixed analog/digital/power synthesis across a 23-task benchmark (Table 1; e.g., QFN-8×8 integrated half-bridge). The framework uses strategies from Section 3: prompt engineering (ICL, CoT), multi-stage verification and feedback, and subcircuit library reuse. Metrics use Pass@k (k=1,5) with n=15 trials per task. Results (summarized in Table 2) show state-of-the-art open-source models can match closed models on some easier/medium tasks, but generally underperform on complex designs (large networks >20 devices, >70 pins). PCBSchemaGen is time- and cost-efficient: best model consumes ≈23k tokens per task (~$0.07) and runs in ≈2.4 minutes versus ~1.5 hours for a human (~37× speedup).

contexts.context: PCBSchemaGen is a training-free, constraint-guided framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to generate PCB schematics by producing SKiDL-style Python code. The system emphasizes a decoupled architecture: an LLM-based proposal generator (with domain-specific prompting, CoT and ICL techniques) for SKiDL code, plus a verification pipeline built from a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph (KG) and a Subgraph Isomorphism (SI) based verifier that encodes pin-role semantics and topological constraints. PCBSchemaGen targets the specific challenges of PCB schematic design — heterogeneous digital, analog, and power domains, real IC packages and pin constraints, and the scarcity of open schematic data — which make purely simulation-based verification (e.g., SPICE) insufficient. The paper reports experiments across 23 PCB schematic tasks spanning digital, analog, and power domains and claims improved design accuracy and computational efficiency. The authors highlight interpretability and reproducibility through rule-based verification and open-source their code, KG, and benchmark on GitHub.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a training-free LLM-based framework for automatic PCB schematic generation that anchors LLM synthesis to a datasheet-driven Knowledge Graph verifier enforcing real-world IC constraints. The system uses a multi-stage verification loop with a retry mechanism (most valid designs appear within three iterations, so retries are capped at three) and varying feedback granularities (Full: type and location; Weak: type only; None: binary). Ablation studies show Full feedback and in-context learning (ICL) prompting are critical for complex tasks: Full feedback substantially improves self-correction and success rates, and removing ICL collapses performance on Hard tasks (78.1% → 43.8%). Human expert blind review (460 samples) yields strong agreement with the verifier (Cohen’s κ overall = 0.913), supporting verification completeness. Visualizations highlight typical failure modes (e.g., wrong op-amp feedback resistors, mis-connected Kelvin-Source). Overall, the approach emphasizes constraint-guided generation, prompt engineering (ICL/CoT), and verifier-driven feedback to bridge functional intent and correct physical PCB implementation.

contexts.context: PCEVAL is a benchmark for assessing LLMs on physical-computing tasks that combine hardware and software (Arduino projects). It is presented as the first benchmark enabling fully automatic evaluation of both logical and physical aspects: circuit generation and code generation, across varying project complexity. The framework decomposes tasks into four dimensions: Logical Circuit Generation, Physical Circuit Generation, Code from Logical Circuits, and Code from Physical Circuits. Outputs are validated automatically using standardized test procedures and circuit validation metrics within a simulation environment; metrics measure logical correctness and physical implementation feasibility (e.g., pin conflicts, extraneous components). The benchmark includes functional success criteria (example: "The test is successful when: Piezo buzzer runs when the button is pressed") and supports reproducible, automated validation without human graders. The authors evaluated 13 LLMs and report that models perform reasonably well on code generation and logical circuit design but struggle with breadboard-level physical layout, especially correct pin connections and avoiding circuit errors. The paper contrasts prior work that relied on manual expert evaluation and often ignored physical layout constraints, highlighting PCEVAL’s contribution toward automated, hardware-aware LLM evaluation.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench evaluates LLMs on board-level schematic design using a generative pipeline that mimics human engineering by decomposing tasks (e.g., module partitioning) and a verification engine with two strategies: static rule checking and dynamic transient (SPICE) simulation. Static checks abstract netlists into a topology graph and use pin property matching and protocol consistency algorithms to find connectivity faults, while dynamic verification compiles generated netlists into SPICE scripts to drive LTspice and performs electrical metric monitoring; "the probe voltage or current at each test point must precisely fall within the preset logic threshold window." The benchmark enforces preprocessing of datasheets into structured JSON. Evaluation uses three dimensions—static checks, dynamic simulations, and global correctness—and standardized sampling (T=0.1, top-p=0.95). Models tested include Claude Sonnet 4.5, DeepSeek-v3, GPT-5.2, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Qwen3-max, and Grok-4. Static pass rates are relatively high (avg 71.84%; Claude 77.73%; Industrial 83.05%, Automotive 77.03%), dynamic pass rates lower (Claude 67.92%; Qwen3-Max 60.21%; Gemini 60.00%), and global full-design correctness is very low (Claude 8.15%; GPT-5.2 6.58%; others 4–5%), highlighting LLMs' physical-intuition gaps and hallucinations on ambiguous/multiplexed pins.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is a benchmark that evaluates large language models on board-level schematic design across static rule checking and dynamic (SPICE-like) behavior simulation. The paper reports considerably different results between static and dynamic evaluations: average static rule pass rates are around 71.84% while dynamic simulation pass rates drop to about 58.65%, and the overall correct-design success rates reported in TABLE V are very low (single-digit percentages for tested models). Key failure modes include context overload when handling very large, homogeneous component lists (many pins), entity role confusion and label conflicts across modules, and pin-multiplexing errors where LLMs assign mutually exclusive functions to the same MCU pin. The authors note models often produce functionally equivalent connections sufficient for simulation, but struggle with rigid physical constraints and global logical consistency. To mitigate these issues, HWE-Bench applies prompt-engineering interventions such as internal signal-chain inference and structured signal-chain or functional-group constraints to reduce component disorder and resolve pin-multiplexing conflicts. The benchmark highlights that circuit design non-uniqueness and strict physical rules remain major challenges for LLM-based EDA agents.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is an evaluation framework for LLMs on board-level schematic design that includes 300 real-world design tasks across 8 application domains and 2,914 IC datasheets. The benchmark emphasizes strict physical constraints and applies a novel functional group-based rule verification method for static testing. It builds a design constraint model that partitions MCU physical resources into functional bus groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) and a GPIO resource pool based on electrical attributes, and uses preset interface protocol contracts to enforce correct connectivity. A verification system introduces a pin-allocation simulation (‘pin locking’ / ‘pin-locking’ mechanism) that marks pins as ‘locked’ when a netlist connection passes attribute checks and emits LOCK CONFLICT warnings for reuse attempts, preventing resource contention and logical short circuits. HWE-Bench also integrates dynamic validation via SPICE simulation. Evaluation with this framework revealed critical limitations in current models’ physical reasoning, especially in MCU pin multiplexing scenarios. Future work aims to guide LLMs toward reliable reasoning and generation of practically deployable circuit designs.

snippets: This paper introduces the HWE-Bench evaluation framework, which encompasses 300 real-world design tasks spanning 8 application domains and 2,914 integrated circuit datasheets.
To tackle the stringent physical constraints inherent in board-level schematic design, we propose a novel functional group-based rule verification method for static testing.

design constraint model based on functional groups, partitioning all physical resources of the MCU into dedicated bus groups (e.g., I²C, SPI) and a general GPIO resource pool based on their underlying electrical attributes
By establishing these underlying norms for hardware resource scheduling during the design phase, the system effectively filters low-level logical errors.

The verification system, by introducing a ‘pin locking’ mechanism, simulates the physical allocation of hardware resources in the static audit process, ensuring the uniqueness of each physical entity in the logical chain.

Whenever a connection in the netlist passes the functional group attribute check, the corresponding physical pin is automatically marked by the system as ‘locked’, and any subsequent rules attempting to reuse that physical resource will trigger a LOCK CONFLICT warning.

Through the adoption of a ‘pin-locking’ mechanism, we effectively mitigate logical conflicts and resource contention that arise when large language models(LLMs) process MCU pin multiplexing scenarios.
Integrated with dynamic validation via SPICE simulation, our evaluation results identify critical limitations in the physical reasoning capabilities of existing models.
In future work, we will focus on guiding large language models(LLMs) to perform reliable reasoning and generate practically deployable circuit designs.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a constraint-guided framework and benchmark for LLM-based printed-circuit schematic design. It provides a task suite stratified by complexity (Easy <10 pins, Medium 10–50, Hard >100 pins) with reported success rates (e.g., Easy: 93.3% vs baselines 53.0% and 80%; Hard: 78.1%). Table 3 compares the framework against baselines including a GNN and an LLM (Plettenberg et al., 2025; Hasan et al., 2026). Experimental notes highlight that stronger multi-modal and spatial reasoning (Gemini 3 performed best) and circuit-level conceptualization are important. The paper documents common failure modes: misunderstanding pin functions and producing meaningless connections due to incorrect abstract topology synthesis. Example tasks (Task 3: differential-to-single-ended op-amp buffer; Task 11: SiC MOSFET half-bridge with HF caps) are used to illustrate successes and failures, with concrete electrical constraints (e.g., Vin = Vin+ − Vin− = 2V, Vout = 3V). Metrics reported include pass@1-style overall percentages. This material is directly relevant for designing or comparing a benchmark like HWE-Bench for board-level schematic evaluation and for defining evaluation criteria, task difficulty, and common LLM failure modes.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is an execution-driven benchmark for evaluating large language models on board-level schematic design. The benchmark contains 300 real board-level tasks across 8 application domains sourced from GitHub and OSHWLab and is paired with a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets. For each task, an LLM must generate a schematic from functional requirements and provided datasheets; outputs undergo static electrical rule checking and dynamic verification via an industry-standard LTspice simulator. The framework emulates engineering workflows by using structured prompts that guide module decomposition, component allocation, and netlist generation. HWE-Bench deliberately avoids text-similarity scoring and instead verifies functional correctness through simulation and rule-based checks. The authors evaluated multiple state-of-the-art LLMs (DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, Grok-4) and report that models show some documentation understanding but generally lack physical intuition, with the top-performing model achieving an overall pass rate of 8.15%. The paper’s contributions include task collection, standardized, machine-checkable task representations, an engineer-like test pipeline, and SOTA model evaluation.

snippets: HWE-Bench, an evaluation framework that benchmarks the ability of LLMs to perform such designs.

It consists of 300 board-level design tasks pulled from open-source and crowdsourcing platforms such as GitHub and OSHWLab, covering 8 application domains, and is complemented with a knowledge base of 2,914 real IC datasheets.
For each task, the LLMs are tasked with generating a schematic from scratch, using the provided circuit functional requirements and a set of component datasheets as input.
The resulting schematic will be checked against a static electrical rules, and then passed to a circuit simulator to verify its dynamic behavior.
our pipeline emulates the cognitive workflows of human engineers by utilizing structured prompts with rigorously defined goals.

To verify the functional correctness of the generated circuits, we abandon traditional text-similarity matching approaches ... Instead, HWE-Bench pioneers an execution-driven evaluation: the resulting netlists are first subjected to stringent static electrical rule checking, and subsequently integrated into an industry-standard LTspice simulator to verify dynamic transient behaviors.

our evaluation show that although current models achieve initial engineering usability and documentation understanding, they lack physical intuition, as the top-performing[cs.AR]  model achieved an overall pass rate of 8.15%.
We conducted a comprehensive evaluation of state-of-the-art LLMs (including DeepSeek-v3, Claude Sonnet 4.5, GPT-5.2, Qwen3-Max, Gemini 3.1 Pro, and Grok-4) on our framework.
The primary contribution of this paper is the establishment of the first execution-driven benchmarking framework tailored for board-level schematic design.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is a benchmark and end-to-end evaluation pipeline for testing LLMs on board-level schematic design. The authors assembled a structured dataset of 300 circuit design tasks and 2,914 cleaned component datasheets, converting multimodal PDFs into JSON and using guided LLM extraction to produce component descriptions and pin definitions. The generative pipeline prompts LLMs sequentially for component allocation, module-level local netlist generation (using a strict plain-text syntax like netX: Component|Pin|Function to reduce formatting hallucinations), and system-level global netlist integration. Outputs are post-processed by scripts that strip Markdown and serialize to a predefined JSON schema. For verification, HWE-Bench uses dual-stage evaluation: static rule checking (rule-based label matching to verify topology and protocol alignment) and dynamic SPICE transient simulation (LLM netlists converted to SPICE and injected into a Python testbench against a human-prepared ground-truth SPICE circuit). The primary metric is the aggregate pass rate across static and dynamic checkpoints; success requires no physical damage and compliance of key node voltages/currents with safety thresholds under specified excitations.
contexts.question: HWE-Bench benchmark for evaluating language models on board-level schematic design, LLM circuit generation evaluation

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCEVAL, a benchmark for evaluating large language models on physical computing tasks that span both logical and physical dimensions. PCEVAL decomposes evaluation into four explicit tasks: logical circuit generation, physical circuit generation, code from logical circuits, and code from physical circuits. It emphasizes automation and reproducibility to remove subjective manual grading, offering a scalable evaluation pipeline that includes hardware/software simulation and automated test procedures. Each dataset instance comprises five elements: Description, Logical circuit (pin-to-pin map), Physical circuit (breadboard-level layout with wiring constraints), Code (executable program tied to the circuit), and Test procedure (automated checks under simulated inputs). Empirical results show LLMs are relatively strong at code generation and logical schematic design but perform poorly on physically constrained layouts—struggling with breadboard mechanics, pin configurations, and general physical-constraint reasoning. PCEVAL uniquely targets generating physically valid layouts and code-from-layouts, making it directly relevant to benchmarks like HWE-Bench that focus on board-level schematic and circuit generation evaluation, and highlights the need for metrics and evaluation components that capture physical validity and layout constraints in addition to functional correctness.
contexts.question: HWE-Bench benchmark for evaluating language models on board-level schematic design, LLM circuit generation evaluation

snippets: PCEVAL (Physical Computing Evaluation), the first benchmark designed to evaluate LLMs’ abilities in both the logical and physical aspects of physical computing tasks.
decomposes the challenge into four dimensions: (1) Logical Circuit Generation, (2) Physical Circuit Generation, (3) Code from Logical Circuits, and (4) Code from Physical Circuits.
scalable and reproducible evaluation without manual expert intervention, overcoming the subjectivity and labor costs of prior manual assessments.

LLMs demonstrate competence in code generation and logical circuit design, they struggle considerably with physical circuit generation, particularly in adhering to breadboard mechanics and ensuring correct pin configurations.
limitations in current LLMs’ physical constraint reasoning

Each PCEVAL project instance is defined by five components: (D)escription, a natural language specification of objectives and requirements; (L)ogical circuit, an abstract pin-to-pin connection map; (P)hysical circuit, a breadboard-level implementation with wiring constraints; (C)ode, an executable program aligned with the given circuit; and (T)est procedure, a set of automated checks that assert expected outputs under simulated inputs.

contexts.context: HWE-Bench is presented as a comprehensive, execution-driven benchmark to evaluate large language models on end-to-end board-level schematic and circuit design tasks. Motivated by limitations of prior text- and software-centric evaluations, it emphasizes the zero-fault-tolerance nature of physical board design and the insufficiency of discriminative or isolated subtask methods. The benchmark proposes a test pipeline that emulates human engineering cognitive pathways and guides models through multi-step design tasks. Core to HWE-Bench is a dual-verification evaluation: (1) expert-level static electrical rule checking and (2) dynamic physical simulations, together establishing a new standard for validating LLM-generated physical-level system netlists. The benchmark specifically targets the model’s ability to translate unstructured natural language specifications and multimodal datasheet constraints into autonomously generated, globally verified schematic designs, filling a capability gap left by previous benchmarks that focused on parsing, assistive text generation, or isolated routing/placement subtasks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks' layered model combining structural and class hierarchies to support both encapsulation and abstraction in PCB/schematic design. Structural hierarchy (hierarchy blocks, bridges) lets sub-sheets export or bridge ports to containing blocks, preserving internal parameters; bridges are two-ported blocks that map external-facing ports to flipped internal roles. The class hierarchy enables abstract parts and subclasses (is-a relationships), allowing delaying concrete specification and reuse. An electronics layer models pin/link types and part ratings; ports carry physical properties (voltage output, current limits, current draw, voltage limits) and parameters "flow" through connected ports into links, which aggregate net properties and include assertions to check correctness. The system includes generators (e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, plus inductor, capacitor, diode, and transistor generators from parts tables) and wraps primitives in higher-level library blocks. Block constraints and link assertions provide automated checks for compatibility and invariants. Together, these features support automated component selection and propagation of electrical parameters across hierarchical and typed block abstractions.
contexts.question: Polymorphic Blocks HDL for circuit board design, Python DSL, type hierarchy, generators, automated component selection

contexts.context: The excerpt describes key aspects of the Polymorphic Blocks HDL and its use for PCB design through reusable library blocks and generators. Example systems (Simon and a datalogger) illustrate that complex elements are expressed compactly: the Simon top-level HDL is 58 lines, and a boost-converter can be instantiated with one line specifying the desired output voltage. Generator libraries encapsulate low-level details and component-sizing logic — e.g., the boost-converter generator handles component sizing automatically, and the datalogger’s backup-power block “generates a current-limited charger and automatically sizes internal elements like the transistor and reference voltage divider.” Modules are organized as library sub-blocks and users can build or reuse parts; the authors prepared common library parts for participants. The paper’s HDL includes higher-level constructs and syntactic sugar (implicit-connect, chain) and supports writing generators/part definitions as part of the workflow, enabling higher-level specification while automating low-level control and component selection.

contexts.context: The Polymorphic Blocks project presents an HDL-based approach for PCB design that spans multiple abstraction levels—from high-level system architecture down to subcircuits and component selection. The authors extend hierarchical block diagrams with polymorphism and abstract-typed blocks (for example, a generic resistor super-type) and add electronics modeling (currents and voltages). Designs written in the block-diagram HDL are elaborated into a hierarchical block graph and expanded using community libraries. That graph can be interactively refined in a GUI; parameters are automatically propagated and checked to ensure correctness. The figure and text show code-like HDL constructs (e.g., "Write led = Block(Led)") and describe the pipeline where the refined block model is exported to a netlist file for downstream board layout tools. The paper discusses the system design, fundamental abstractions, the block diagram construction HDL, interfaces for inspection and fine-tuning, and use of community libraries to expand and instantiate abstract blocks.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Polymorphic Blocks, a board-level hardware description language implemented as a Python-embedded domain-specific language. The authors position board-level HDLs as a way to raise abstraction and increase automation beyond traditional schematic capture. They emphasize generator-based, programmatic construction (a Chisel-like approach) so a generator defines methodology and can automatically size internal component values for each application. Object-oriented concepts such as type hierarchies and inheritance are proposed to raise design level: types can encode parameters (e.g., voltage and current ratings) to enable correctness checks and to support automated selection among compatible alternatives. Polymorphic Blocks is presented as a library-based flow that uses these ideas; the provided Blinky example shows block instantiation (self.Block(...)) and explicit connections (self.connect(...)). Subcomponents like IndicatorLed are themselves generators that can contain code to size parts (e.g., resistors) based on inputs. Overall, the paper highlights automated component sizing, generator reuse, and type-directed selection as core features for PCB-level HDLs.

While a Verilog-like approach of translating schematics into equivalent HDL offers limited benefits, a Chisel-like [2] approach of programmatically constructing hardware via generators can be much more powerful and re-usable.
a generator would instead define the methodology, asarXiv:2011.08242v1  code, and automatically size those components for each specific application.

parameters [6] like voltage and current ratings, enabling correctness checks.
The most recent example of a board-level HDL is Polymorphic Blocks [3], which incorporates the above concepts to support this higher-level design vision with a library-based flow.

class Blinky(Block):
self.mcu = self.Block(MagicMcu())
self.led = self.Block(IndicatorLed())
self.connect(self.mcu.gnd, self.led.gnd)
self.connect(self.mcu.digital[0], self.led.io)
The IndicatorLed is also written in HDL (not shown) as a generator, containing code to size the internal resistor based on its input voltage.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a Polymorphic Circuit Blocks model and a Python-like hardware construction language (HCL) that supports abstraction and gradual refinement. A class hierarchy lets designers instantiate abstract, generic blocks (e.g., a step-down converter or LED) and later refine them into concrete subclasses; however, replacing an abstract block with a subclass can be unsafe without checks because physical constraints (like current limits) may differ. Ports and links carry typed electrical parameters: ports expose properties (voltage_out, current_limit, current_draw, voltage_limit), and links aggregate those to compute net-level parameters (total current, acceptable voltage range) and run assertions. The electronics layer models power, digital, and analog port types and bundles for multi-wire protocols. Libraries combine primitives and generator blocks — e.g., a resistor generator using the E24 series, inductors, capacitors, diodes, transistors, and higher-level blocks (pull-ups, decoupling caps) that translate port parameters to component ratings. Automated component selection and sizing examples include RC filter calculation from cutoff frequency and resistive divider selection from E24 values. The system exposes a generator HCL/Python DSL for composing blocks (example: class Blinky(Block): ...).

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-based HDL implemented as a library of base classes with mypy annotations, enabling a typed DSL for circuit design. The system uses a hierarchical type/refinement model: abstract blocks can be interactively refined to concrete subclasses (for example, refining an abstract resistor to an SMD part), keeping high-level HDL while handling specifics via refinements. Some blocks act as generators: during lowering the generator is provided concrete parameter values and expands into internal elements. Generators execute once and in no guaranteed order, so they must handle parameter ranges via worst-case bounds. Parameter constraints are parsed into a directed constraint graph and evaluated lazily; constraint forms like assignments and subset bounds are recorded specially. The compiler incrementally lowers the high-level model, performs simplifications, and finally netlists the design as a tree walk, producing netlist files that can be imported into KiCad. The flow supports deterministic component naming tied to HDL variable names to enable netlist updates to in-progress layouts. The project is open-source on GitHub.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes a Polymorphic Circuit Blocks HDL that uses a class-hierarchy/type system for block abstraction and substitution, enabling designers to instantiate generic components (e.g., a generic LED or step-down converter) and later refine them to concrete subclasses. Ports and links carry typed physical parameters (voltage, current, thresholds) so parameter values propagate through nets and links create aggregate properties; block constraints provide automated compatibility checks. The system includes a library and generators: primitives (resistor, inductor, capacitor, diode, transistor) are produced from parts tables, including a resistor generator using the E24 series, and are wrapped into higher-level library blocks (e.g., pull-ups, decoupling caps). Higher-level blocks can perform automated component selection and sizing (RC filter design, resistive divider selection to E24). There is a programmatic generator HCL (Python-like DSL) for authoring blocks—example Blinky code shows instantiation and connects—supporting both novices composing system-level designs and experts building libraries.
contexts.question: Polymorphic Blocks HDL for circuit board design, Python DSL, type hierarchy, generators, automated component selection

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded domain specific language (HCL) for hierarchical circuit/block design. It uses classes and a library of base classes to represent reusable block templates, with Ports, Blocks, and GeneratorBlocks allowing programmatic construction of subcircuits. Generators are member functions that instantiate internal sub-blocks, ports, and parameters; they receive (concrete or bounded) parameter values during compilation and can "access solved values like digital logic thresholds, and use those to automatically size internal blocks like the resistor," enabling automated component selection and sizing. The DSL includes syntactic helpers such as implicit_connect (for common nets like power/ground) and chain (for linear signal-through topologies). A GUI visualizer supports refinement by letting users choose block subclass refinements interactively; refined subcircuits are generated and model checks run. The compiler lowers the high-level model via a tree walk performing refinement and generation, updates a constraint graph (constraints parsed into a directed graph), and requires generators to supply worst-case bounds because generators run once and in no particular order. The lowered design can be exported as a netlist for KiCad. The project is open-source on GitHub.

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks introduces an HDL built around hierarchical block diagrams, a type system, and an electronics model. The language exposes blocks, ports, and links: blocks are the primary user-facing elements, ports describe interfaces (pins, GPIO, busses) and can carry parameters, and links describe connections and parameter propagation. The system supports block-level polymorphism and a class hierarchy so users can write abstract blocks (e.g., a step-down converter) that are later refined into concrete subcircuits (e.g., specific buck converters tied to controller chips). Generators and subcircuit generators are a core mechanism (and the work cites approaches that embed generators in general-purpose languages), but this system augments generators with explicit electronics modeling to enable design support such as automated parts selection and correctness checks. The tool provides a visualization/refinement interface and can export a netlist for PCB layout. Overall, the HDL emphasizes higher-level, hierarchical specification plus generator-driven reuse, with type and parameter constraints to enable automated selection and synthesis support.

Ports, shown as small circles in fgures, represent the interface of blocks like power pins, GPIO pins, and signal busses.
Links, shown as diamonds in fgures, represent connections between ports, defning how ports connect and how parameters can propagate.

JITPCB [3] brings generators to the PCB space by embedding circuit construction primitives in a general purpose programming language.

contexts.context: Polymorphic Blocks provides a Python-embedded hardware construction language (HCL) where classes form a type hierarchy (re-usable block templates) and objects are instances. Generators are member functions that programmatically instantiate and connect sub-blocks, ports, and parameters; they run during compilation when provided with concrete parameter bounds. The system supports syntactic sugar for common connection patterns (implicit connect and chain). Generators can access solved parameter values (e.g., logic thresholds) to automatically size internal components such as resistors, enabling automated component selection and sizing based on constraints. A visualization/refinement GUI lets users pick block subclass refinements interactively so high-level designs can be concretized, with model checks. The compiler “lowers” designs by refinement, generation, and constraint-graph evaluation; constraints are parsed into a directed graph for lazy evaluation. After lowering, a netlister emits a netlist importable into tools like KiCad. The HCL is implemented as a Python library with mypy annotations and the project is open-source.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes PCBSchemaGen, a system that uses large language models (LLMs) to programmatically generate PCB schematics by synthesizing executable SKiDL code without fine-tuning. It relies on domain-specific prompting techniques (Chain-of-Thought and In-context Learning) and an iterative feedback loop where an interpretable verifier provides messages to improve generated code. To ground generation in physical constraints, the system builds a domain knowledge graph from real IC datasheets encoding pin roles, multiple constraint types, and topological constraints; a KG- and SI-driven multi-phase verifier issues specific, interpretable error messages. The authors curated a 23-circuit benchmark spanning digital, analog, and power domains with real IC packages, and claim end-to-end capability from natural-language specification to KiCAD PCB layout files. The paper contrasts this board-level, package- and pin-aware approach with prior work that focused on IC-level Verilog/analog synthesis or lacked board-level packaging and pin constraint modeling.

contexts.context: The excerpt situates programmatic PCB design within prior work on PCB HDLs and hardware construction languages. Early board HDLs (e.g., PHDL) are structural; more recent projects like JITPCB and SKiDL introduce generator-style programming by embedding circuit construction primitives in general-purpose languages. The dissertation builds on this idea and adds more detailed electronics modeling to support parts selection and correctness checks. Issues relevant to a Stanza-based (JITX-like) programming model include the structural nature of PCB descriptions, the value of generator constructs for reusable modules, and the need for richer electronics models to enable automated checks (ERC-like, parts substitution). Interaction concerns from programming tooling—especially live feedback latency and techniques to reduce it (speculation, incremental computation)—are highlighted as important for a productive programmatic PCB flow. The text also notes scalability considerations (library creation by end users) and references tools that bridge prototyping and PCB workflows (Fritzing, AutoFritz) and debugging aids (Pinpoint, BoardLab), which inform expectations around layout automation, verification (DRC/ correctness checks), and the overall toolchain (including eventual outputs such as Gerbers).

snippets: JITPCB [7] and SKiDL [73] brings generators to the PCB space by embedding circuit construction primitives in a general purpose programming language.
Unlike chip HDLs, PCB HDLs are purely structural, as it is unclear what behavioral abstractions can suit the wide space of PCB electronics.
This dissertation builds on top of that core idea, but augments it with more detailed electronics modeling to enable design support features like parts selection and correctness checks.
Hardware construction languages (HCLs) that support generators are an evolution on the basic HDL.
One critical aspect of live programming systems is the latency between code change and visible effects [66].
Prior work has discussed possible techniques to reduce latency [50, 79], such as predictively starting to compute results or returning speculative results.
While many tools focus on enabling novices, some work also examines challenges of scaling from one-of prototypes to production [34].
Fritzing [37] helps by providing a breadboard view of a circuit as a conceptual bridge to the schematic view, but is still fundamentally a schematic drawing tool.

contexts.context: Lin et al. describe an HDL-and-compiler based approach for PCB/circuit design that uses high-level hierarchical block abstractions and reusable generators to produce concrete subcircuits and working hardware. The system is intended to provide meaningful design automation: authors state “we implemented an HDL and compiler based on those principles and which is capable of providing meaningful designautomation,” and show “hardware-proven example designs, where complex subcircuits are generated from high-level specifcations.” The flow relies heavily on good, complete libraries to maximize reuse, but the authors acknowledge library coverage is incomplete and propose automating data extraction from datasheets (e.g., “Tabula [31] and DocParser could accelerate this effort”). The work targets intermediate-level designers (“we focused on supporting intermediate-level designers and projects”), emphasizes design time and portability concerns from participants, and aims to enable both experienced engineers and novices. Limitations noted include gaps in the electronics model and the need for a more polished, scalable system and larger studies. These points are relevant when considering a stanza/DSL-based programmatic PCB flow (generators, library-driven synthesis, graphical integration, and the practical need for DRC, manufacturing output, and richer part data).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes BHDL, a declarative, modular HDL for PCB design implemented in Racket with a REPL and Jupyter-driven development environment. It supports importing footprints (including from URLs) and KiCAD-format footprint libraries, allows multiple footprints per part with defaults, and provides convenient naming/prefix/pin syntaxes. For layout automation, BHDL integrates a two-stage placer: an analytical global placer (RePlace) that minimizes HPWL and a simulated annealing (SA) detailed placer to legalize overlaps. The SA placer includes a rotation dimension (random-angle rotations) to handle large, irregular PCB components that need orientation changes to fit tight spaces. The autoplacer is implemented in Julia for easier debugging and GPU acceleration. The paper notes tuning density penalties is necessary because RePlace alone can place parts too close, harming routability. Output/export capabilities include KiCAD PCB-format files exportable to Gerber and BOM generation. The excerpt does not explicitly describe DRC, but it discusses legalization, overlap removal, and routability concerns that relate to design rule considerations.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Guardado's toolchain for accelerating smart-device design through information encapsulation and automation. Appliancizer-like techniques transformed web interfaces (e.g., video player, thermostat) into physical devices without changing application source code and showed a 6.5× development speed improvement versus Amalgam. TypedSchematics is introduced as a circuit-data encapsulation approach that annotates schematic blocks with hardware-interface types (e.g., I2C, SPI), separates power connections, and provides real-time feedback (mismatched voltages, mis-connected signals, missing connections). Its mat abstraction reduces repetitive power wiring, and a flow-based programming model enables real-time interface checking. User studies reported a 2.8× faster schematic design time versus Fusion 360 and increased designer confidence. Future directions include integrating AI-generated HTML-to-UI techniques with Appliancizer for higher abstraction and adding PCB-ready breakout-board generation to bridge prototyping and production. The chapter concludes that encapsulating circuit information enables fusion of electronic-design steps, reuse, and automation, reducing complexity and improving accessibility. The excerpt does not mention JITX or the Stanza language explicitly, but it is relevant to programmatic and automated circuit creation and tool-driven design automation.

snippets: We have also demonstrated the rapid development capabilities of our tool through a preliminary user study which show a speed-up of 6.5X in development time compared to Amalgam.

TypedSchematics introduces a circuit data encapsulation technique for electronic schematics that enables the creation of an interactive design tool with a low threshold but a high ceiling, a balance that allows for fast and easy yet highly configurable schematic designs.

This encapsulation technique centers on annotating schematics with hardware interface types (e.g., I2C, SPI), giving schematic blocks knowledge of required connections rather than having wires with unknown connection types, and allowing the manual connection of inputs of schematic blocks in a faster and easier way.
TypedSchematics addresses these design challenges by separating power-related connections from connections between general components.
It also provides real-time, interactive feedback on potential design problems including mis-matched voltages, mis-connected signals in complex interfaces, and missing (but necessary) connections.
TypedSchematics’s mat abstraction eliminates the need for repetitive voltage and ground connections, reducing interaction costs.
A flow-based programming paradigm is seamlessly integrated into Mats, making it possible to perform interface checking in real time.
Results show that, on average, participants’ design time of a thermostat schematic was 2.8 times faster with TypedSchematics than with Fusion 360.
Recent research has shown the possibility of generating HTML interfaces from text descriptions [85].
Appliancizer allows designers to quickly generate electronic devices with mixed graphical and physical interfaces from HTML interfaces.
we see the possibility of integrating the design of schematics blocks into TypedSchematics.

fusion of different electronic design steps, design steps fusion, is achievable through circuit information encapsulation which accelerates the design of electronic devices through reuse and automation.

contexts.context: The excerpt describes Appliancizer, a design-automation tool that lets designers create smart-device prototypes as web pages and then automatically produce corresponding physical devices (schematics/PCB and low-level code). It treats HTML as the hardware description language, mapping HTML GUI elements to reusable "tangible controls" (modules containing schematics, code, and hardware specs). Examples include mapping knobs and sensors to HTML sliders. Appliancizer emphasizes a higher-level approach: it simulates interface events rather than electrical circuit behavior and uses modular PCB schematic pieces that experts author and novices reuse. The tool contrasts with prior systems (JITPCB, EDG, TAC) that require new hardware description languages or target circuit-level descriptions; instead, Appliancizer leverages ubiquitous web technologies and MVC patterns to generate circuits from web prototypes. It builds on prior work (Amalgam) that extended the HTML DOM to the physical domain but adds automated PCB generation and deployment. These points are relevant when comparing programmatic circuit creation approaches (HDLs, web-based mappings, modular schematic libraries, automation of PCB/layout/code generation).

contexts.context: The excerpt describes three tools that automate and programmatically generate smart-device designs by encapsulating circuit and firmware information into higher-level representations. Amalgam embeds firmware functionality into web technologies (HTML/CSS/JS), enabling rapid prototyping and allowing designers to ‘‘transform web pages into fully functional electronic devices in a couple of minutes,’’ but it provides limited low-level configurability (low threshold, low ceiling). Appliancizer builds on Amalgam to also integrate circuit design data and automatically generate complete electronic designs (including PCB layout) and low-level code from static web prototypes; it uses "essential interface mapping" to map HTML controls to tangible controls, enabling circuit synthesis and device simulation, and can turn web apps (e.g., video player, smart thermostat) into physical devices without changing source code, showing a 6.5X speed improvement over Amalgam. TypedSchematics targets schematic reuse and scalability: it offers real-time validation of mismatches, an abstraction called Mats to remove repetitive ground/voltage connections, and a flow-based model for constraint checking; users produced thermostat schematics 2.8x faster than with Fusion 360. The work highlights trade-offs between ease-of-use, automation, configurability, and library scalability in programmatic circuit creation and end-to-end smart-device design automation.

Appliancizer allows designers to simulate the interaction between physical components and components on the screen, using the same HTML elements as mock-up devices, improving idea exploration.
